body: Seems maybe this is solveable within webpack. To work around the issue, @pauldraper you can set `snapshot.module.hash = true`. to use hash of content instead of timestamp
author: markjm

body: Watchpack is properly watching `data/123.js` in addition to `main.js`. When we get the change event on `data/123.js`, we see the updated timestamp, and correctly emit `main.js` as the changed file into webpack (via "aggregated" event). However, the `fileTimestamps[main.js]` is not updated, so when we check snapshots, its still valid and goes to cache.
author: markjm

body:
Wow, thanks! (Spent all day tracking that one down, and creating a WatchFileSystem that worked for my particular needs.)

I confirmed that `snapshot.module.hash = true` fixes the issue.

author: assetinsights

body:
I'm seeing this with Angular 13. It used to work with Angular 11 (back when it used Webpack 4).

It appears that making any changes in a shared library which is symlinked into Angular 13 will not result in the changes being applied properly. 

I'm also using `preserveSymlinks` in `angular.json` which sets `resolve: { symlinks: false }` as per: https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/blob/a0784bd8cb64bd719ed499f97603522fbc9db02d/packages/angular_devkit/build_angular/src/webpack/configs/common.ts#L317

Angular 13 does catch the change and it does trigger a recompilation, but if I look at the generated .js bundle, they're never there. It seems like the previous version gets stuck until the process is restarted. (cc @alan-agius4)
author: andreialecu

body:
> Seems maybe this is solveable within webpack. To work around the issue, @pauldraper you can set `snapshot.module.hash = true`. to use hash of content instead of timestamp

This should still be a valid workaround. I tend to agree with @alexander-akait that this may be a limitation with some of the symlink settings. Perhaps `snapshot.module.hash` should be set by default in cases where those symlink flags are set like this.
author: markjm

body:
@andreialecu, your issue is not related to this one. What you are describing is actually expected and working as intended. `node_modules` are considered immutable and only get invalidated once the version in package manifest changes.

See https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/22104 and https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/20962
author: alan-agius4

body:
>  this may be a limitation with some of the symlink settings

FWIW, I would be confused why watchOptions.followSymlinks would even exist as an option. AFAIK it only makes sense to be true if resolve symlinks are disabled; i.e. this configuration.
author: pauldraper

body:
It doesn't seem to work here, even with `snapshot.module.hash = true` and `watchOptions: { followSymlinks: true, }`.
Should I consider something else ? Thanks.
author: guio12

body:
It could work by add `cache:false` in top level for yarn link + webpack hot update case.

And don't need others such as snapshot.module.hash = true and watchOptions: { followSymlinks: true, }.

https://github.com/react-atomic/reshow/blob/main/packages/reshow-app/src/webpack.client.js#L52
author: HillLiu

body: Thanks for the tip, it works indeed, but unfortunately on my project the update / watch recompilation becomes so slow (~2 minutes for each change instead of 10 seconds) it is unusable.
author: guio12

body:
> @andreialecu, your issue is not related to this one. What you are describing is actually expected and working as intended. `node_modules` are considered immutable and only get invalidated once the version in package manifest changes.
> 
> See [angular/angular-cli#22104](https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/22104) and [angular/angular-cli#20962](https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/20962)

Hi @alan-agius4, I still think Angular 13, 14 does have this issue outside of node_modules and folders without a package.json inside them. We have a symlinked model folder that is shared between and Angular app and a NodeJs project. The folder is not in node_modules. 

When we change a file. For example, adding a property to a class .. Angular recompiles but complains that the class does not contain this new property. Killing the build and starting again resolves the issue but this is starting to become a massive drag on productivity.

Is there anyway to utilise any of the strategies above in the Angular.json or disable caching for a specific folder?

Regards,
Tarek
author: thaoula

body:
> Is there anyway to utilise any of the strategies above in the Angular.json or disable caching for a specific folder?

No, you either enable or disable caching. If you do want to modify the webpack configuration you can do so using 3rd party builders. However, this is not supported by the Angular team.

But:
1. Don't use `rxjs`, because you don't use `RxFunctionList`
2. You still have `rxjs`, `rxjs` inside chunk `353.chunks.js`

body: You have this https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/RxFunctionList.tsx#L17, so we can't remove `rxjs`, why you think it should be removed?
author: alexander-akait

body:
Because `RxFunctionList` is never statically imported into the main bundle. [It is only imported dynamically](https://github.com/ditkin/graphql-tutorial/blob/main/frontend/src/App.tsx#L7). 

I expect `RxFunctionList`, and all imports that are only consumed in `RxFunctionList`, to be in a split chunk, but they are going into the main bundle.

Sorry, I used a little incorrect word.  I wanted to write "very sad".
The problem with webpack-merge is that it requires the Configuration type from the webpack package as input, and the dev-server field is missing from it.

body: You can use `type ConfigurationAndDevServer = Configuration & { devServer: DevServerConfiguration };`, in future webpack-dev-server will be just plugin, so the `devServer` option will not exists

body: Hi @alexander-akait, updated the issue's description with a link to the [minimal reproduction repository](https://github.com/ziir/webpack-repro-dynamic-import-context-replacement-deterministic-module-id) containing [the configuration file](https://github.com/ziir/webpack-repro-dynamic-import-context-replacement-deterministic-module-id/blob/main/context-a/webpack.config.js)
author: ziir

body:
I believe the issue presents specifically when the regex of `ContextReplacementPlugin` contains `|` characters.

When hashing modules (in `DeterministicModuleIdsPlugin`), we make calls to `IDHelpers.getFullModuleName` -> `makePathsRelative` -> `_makePathsRelative`. Here, the identifier is split on `|` and `!` characters, then passed to `absoluteToRequest`.

While `absoluteToRequest` handles regex's in identifiers, the splitting in `_makePathsRelative` splits up the regex in the identifier to look like 2 seperate "pieces", which `absoluteToRequest` will then attempt to `path.relative`-ize
author: markjm

body:
As discussed with @alexander-akait, I'm putting this further issue we are running into with ``eager:true`` here:

<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

If we share libs via the host using ``eager:true`` but don't use them, they are removed. Hence, they need to be loaded alongside the first remote that needs them. As we are speaking about quite a lot libs, this is dramatically slowing down the application.

@alexander-akait already mentioned in another thread that this is very like a limitation. Perhaps we find a way to work aroud it.
author: manfredsteyer

body:
yeah, that's our eager work. It will always load them, but it's only known at runtime if they are needed.
eager is an option for some rare edge cases. You usually don't want to use it.

I do call `webpack --mode production`, it does not change anything.

Moreover I do not see why shall this depend on `mode`.  With webpack I create intermediary packages, which are then used by different packages.  The intermediary packages shall not be compressed/minified/stripped, but at the same time shall not emit useless code.

That said, neither does production-mode lead in the particular case to less emitted code, nor does it make sense to reduce the code only in production mode.

I created this repro that sort-of shows it with a minimal config: https://github.com/rigor789/webpack-hmr-dobule-emit

Added a screenshot to the readme - it logs which file triggered the change. The result is that the dist folder doesn't have the hot-update files when it does a double trigger like this.

Interestingly, if I just `touch src/app.js` it doesn't double-compile.

Also interesting, triggering the change with another editor like `nano` also only triggers one compilation - makes me think that there's a different saving mechanism in VSCode that triggers the file to be saved twice...

So while I think increasing the `aggregateTimeout` is a workaround - maybe webpack shouldn't delete the previous hot-update files in case the compilation didn't emit any new files/nothing changed? When I get a double compilation, both hashes are the same.

body:
Apologies, I commented on the `aggregateTimeout` PR as I didn't know this issue was already open. Below is a copy of my comment, original here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15041#issuecomment-1033459364

> The docs still state this value is `200`: https://webpack.js.org/configuration/watch/#watchoptionsaggregatetimeout
> 
> FWIW, this causes double-rebuilds when using Visual Studio Code (all extensions disabled). For brevity I've created a repro https://github.com/levymetal/webpack5-double-compile-repro but this occurs on all my projects upgraded to webpack>=5.66.0 on multiple machines.
> 
> This might be because VSCode has previously been known to touch files twice when saving: https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/issues/9419. This wasn't fixed at the time and I don't know if it's still the case as I haven't yet attempted to log filesystem events, but it seems likely.
> 
> I'm not sure if it's Webpack's job to work around the limitations of VSCode, but as it currently stands this is a tricky issue to debug that will affect a significant number of people. IMHO this seems big enough to warrant calling out in the docs, that some editors require an `aggregateTimeout` of at least 100ms to avoid compiling twice on every save.
> 
> I wonder if this may even be the historical reason for 200ms.

body:
So on first run, cache is "invalidated" because of initial write to ResolverCachePlugin cache. This is actually expected because its the first time the pack is being created.

The second invalidation does look to be a bug due to exportsinfo hash including _usedName. I believe this should just be removed from the hash, as `MangleExportsPlugin` will run and fill this value anyway. The alternative would be to include the _usedName information in the pack as well (instead of just the hash), but the work to generate these usedNames given the exportsinfo seems pretty minimal

body: @markjm as I understand you are talking about this line? https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/91dd188e01d6bc4b2f47387ff138d6e853f871d2/lib/ExportsInfo.js#L1416

author: sokra

body: I think it's a bug in `lib/FlagDependencyExportsPlugin.js`. The order of execution differs when it's cached. Somehow it sets `canMangleProvide` to false here
author: sokra

body:
I am not as familiar with the exports info flow. Do you know the reason why we cant cache in the below situation?

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/4abe329dcdaea60371c03c0d85c3a3994d875138/lib/FlagDependencyExportsPlugin.js#L291
author: markjm

body:
We don't cache for any module where exports depend on other modules.

There is no theoretical limitation to that. We would need to snapshot and compare the dependencies for changes when doing that. And it gets a bit tricky with transitive dependencies.

Anyway to avoid that complexity we currently don't do it.

But I don't think that's related to the problem...

---

I think the problem is that once we set the canMangle flag to false, they is no way to reset that. So we probably don't want to set it in first place. I assume it's caused by missing information about dependent modules in the first iteration of exports determiniation.

body: `[fullhash]` for `file-loader` is just `[contenthash]`, `[fullhash]` available only `Compilation level` and ideally should be used as directory, i.e. `path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist/[fullhash]/')`, we can't calculate full hash when we build modules

author: AdeshKhandait

body:
@alexander-akait to clarify:

This is certainly related to #2933 in that I'm enabling the experimental module output. I did expect a re-export, and I'm just filing the bug to track that external libraries are not re-exported, when they should be.

The repro link I've included is a very trimmed down example so, while the actual repro is a little more involved, this scenario is greatly simplified:

1. Bundle (using `module` output) a file which has the line `export * from 'some-library';`
2. Normally, this would produce a bundled JS containing all the content from `some-library`, however our config marks `some-library` as external, so bundling it should preserve the export.

The use case for this is that we will load the produced ESM in the browser, and use an import-map to resolve `some-library` from another location. This allows us to switch the resolution on the fly via url change.

When we bundle it, the word `export` does not even exist in the ESM, effectively making the bundle useless in the browser because the expected named exports are missing.

This is also an issue with ESBuild, you can read about that issue here: https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/1737
author: dzearing

body:
Yep, there is limitation, shorty - we don't know exported names because you use `*` and we can't recreate export (there is no in JS `export * from myObjectWithExports;`), in theory we can do deep analyze and recreate it, but it will be with limitations (if library add new export we will not re export it and recreation increases size of bundle very), looks like esbuild uses the same approach

Another approach - avoiding runtime and just keep `export * from "./something.js";` but it is bit tricky
author: alexander-akait

body:
The scenario where we use this is:

* We have a component library consists of many components. Each component is in a separate package.
* App devs just import { Button } from component library.
* The component library has something like `export * from 'button-package';`

When we bundle the library, but externalize `button-package`, apps can no longer import `{ Button }` from the library.

It looks like named exports will address. If I say `export { Button } from 'button-package'`, it looks like the webpack output will try and import * from the library, and then explicitly export the named thing.

So the workaround is; don't export * from a library, but use named exports. (This is a better practice anyway.) I think we can use a lint rule to enforce this. I still think it would still be correct to preserve the star export if possible.
author: dzearing

body: I understand what you want to achieve, but currently only one workaround - named export
author: alexander-akait

body: Thanks @alexander-akait for the input and help. I've adjusted the title to fit more; either we can use this to track the specific ask, or close if it's already tracked in the module support issue.

body:
I assume by the conversation is still somewhat open since you asked:

Let's assume someone is updating from Webpack 5.30 to 5.68. It's very difficult to scroll through the changes between 5.30 and 5.68:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26336/152702745-60d062ea-a280-495f-9131-11248c74e82d.mp4

By contrast, here is babel's [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/babel/babel/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md):

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26336/152702755-64967d04-e0fd-4651-b51f-d45ede8fffc8.mp4

- Searchable in one file
- Scrollable (no need to paginate seemingly forever)
- Can link to sections
- Can link to git tags (albeit their git tags are one release off)

In short, GitHub Releases makes it extremely cumbersome to see changes that happened over time.  

Webpack makes many releases, GitHub Releases splits out and formats critical changes like a blog website.

body:
Back with an update!

So I found out I can remove babel-polyfill and use alternative solutions so I was able to clear that from my dependencies.
I didn't see a change in build time until I looked into what @vankop had said.

I found through [here](https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/) that terser does 2 passes. I set it to minimize with one pass and I was able to achieve a similar performance as v4. Zero change rebuilds we're also near-instant whereas with 2 passes, it seemed like it would rebuild from scratch.

Is it necessary to do two passes with terser?

body:
Thanks for your issue. This done intentionally since in esm there is no `require`, you can mark this js file as `type="javascript/auto"` in your `webpack.config`( use https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#ruletype )

Ah.. if you need `context` you still can use `import('/a/a' + name)` https://webpack.js.org/api/module-methods/#dynamic-expressions-in-import
author: vankop

body:
I understand, but the fact that it's called `require.context` is anecdotical, isn't it? I mean Webpack doesn't use the `require` function under the hood, it just parses the expression. In this case it's a shame to restrict its usage to CJS environments only, don't you think?

> Ah.. if you need context you still can use import('/a/a' + name)

I agree this is handy, but I need the filenames.
author: tavoyne

body: Webpack tries to follow specs. Since esm kind of strict environment there is no cjs stuff in it.
author: vankop

body: But `require.context` is not a _cjs stuff_, it's webpack's.
author: tavoyne

body: `require.context` exists only because webpack starts when ESM was not ready, so `require.context` is just hack for `import(...)`, because currently ESM is preferable and we should reduce usage of commonjs, we stop to add new features for `require` (i.e. CommonJS) and also block them to ESM
author: alexander-akait

body: Unless I'm missing something here, it could have been called `webpack.context` or `blabla` and work the same way. The fact that it has `require` in it is arbitrary.
author: tavoyne

body: @sokra should we add `import.meta.context` ?
author: vankop

body: I agree with you @alexander-akait. But `require.context()` has an extra functionality compared to `import()`: it's the fact that you can access filenames.
author: tavoyne
tavoyne

body: @vankop @tavoyne To be honest, I have quite different feelings, on the one hand, I understand well how it is used and why it is needed, on the other hand, we already had the experience of adding new stuff to module system and it always ended badly.
author: alexander-akait
tavoyne

body: It changes `import` logic, it is very bad idea, we should not do it, in this case better to use `import.meta.context()`, because `import.meta.*` is free to use, maybe even `import.meta.webpackContent` to prevent overlapping with other tools
author: alexander-akait

body: Makes complete sense. Could work on a PR if it's something that you'd consider integrating.
author: tavoyne

body: @sokra What do you think?
author: alexander-akait

body:
`import.meta.webpackContext` would be the name to choose here.

But we should use the chance to improve the API a bit and let it take an object instead of many arguments. This would also allow to add the other options a context takes, like `include` or `exclude` or `mode`

body:
Hey @vankop, thx again. In retrospect, I'm already _declaring_ my modules as effect free--getting that added to the stat isn't exactly what I'm after.

I seek to find which modules were shaken.

I have high confidence that my thousands of monorepo modules are all sideEffect free, which is why I mark them as such using the `module.rules.[...]` discussed above. By marking them explicitly as effectFree, my codebase will be transformed such that some modules will be shaken.

I need to observe which modules are shaken, so I can apply a manual audit of them and know with confidence that the `sideEffects: false` declaration was correct.

Is there an API to _observe_ when something is shaken? Can I tap into a shake event?

@nwalters512 Could you reduce repro to this resolve request? steps to reduce:
- reduce webpack config to resolve settings only ( and `cache: true` will be enough)

author: vankop

body:
@vankop Thanks for taking a look! I was ultimately able to isolate the parts that are causing problems: https://github.com/nwalters512/webpack-resolver-cache-plugin-bug-repro. Please follow the instructions in the README to reproduce.

In doing so, I realized that the error only occurs with a custom webpack plugin that we use to deduplicate dependencies, which is located at `deduple-plugin.js` (original, non-transpiled TS code available in `dedupe-plugin.ts`). If I remove the plugin, the error goes away. It's entirely possible that our plugin is doing something stupid and that the resolution will be to change the plugin, not webpack. However, the plugin definitely worked on webpack v5.68.0 and v4.x, so I think there's still the possibility that this is a genuine regression in webpack.

body:
Maybe this is a bug in Node? I took the example code from [kqueue](https://www.freebsd.org/cgi/man.cgi?query=kqueue) and tested it locally and it only notifies once for file change.

![2022-02-23 12 41 28](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1622446/155386171-229df61b-3efd-4661-bf82-c6dd9af1f3e0.gif)

_edit_

I think Node is observing multiple events and aggregating them into a single change event and more than 1 event is triggered on file save. https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/debd87cd9bfa1f8c0bc4f4c41f48042c584980f6/deps/uv/src/unix/kqueue.c#L455-L456
author: daniel-nagy

body:
Agreed that it seems to be a node bug - just suggesting possible fixes/mitigations we can apply at the webpack level.

Another thought - is anyone able to repro in plain terminal without having the files open in vscode? Perhaps new issue with mac build of vscode?
author: markjm

body:
Maybe it is not a bug, I guess it depends on the intent of the original author. In this case it is certainly unexpected and undesirable. If the `watch` API is behaving differently on different platforms then from that perspective you might consider it a bug.

Perhaps in hindsight the `watch` API should not be limited to `'change'` and `'rename'` events but maybe this was done for cross platform compatibility reasons. It would be nice to get someone from the Node team to way in here.

body:
> This can also be resolved by setting ignoreWhenEqual here. From my understanding, that change would make sense - but maybe missing some history as to why it is set to false

Some filesystems have mtime accuracy up to 2s. Ignoring changes where mtime doesn't change would lead to missing changes when they occur within the right 2s window.

author: markjm

body:
> Which filesystems?

FAT, but ext3 has 1s, that's also not the best.
I also assume that mtime is only set once even if the file access is splitted into multiple writes. So I guess you could still see different file content for the same mtime even with high accuracy.

> It may also be worth nothing that the issue does not appear when watching the containing directory

btw. watchpack never watches files, but only watches directories.

body:
Upvoting the request a lot. Have a similar build, we got a shared monorepository with all the common packages in an enterprise build. Build takes about 10 minutes, if cached it can run 30 seconds.

Unfortunately our common packages are all published on each change, even if there aren't changes in one of the packages.
Webpack build is based on `@scope/conf` package, which contains core config shared throughout all of the projects, once `@scope/conf/package.json` version is raised (which is every build), buildDependencies are mismatched and the entire cache invalidated. (Even though the @scope/conf contents aren't actually changed)

Spent a good couple of days trying to hack into `PackFileCacheStrategy` and `FileSystemInfo`, to allow exclusion of package.json in build dependencies with no luck, good job on isolating it :)

Instead I had to copy/paste the cache section of `WebpackOptionsApply` into a custom plugin to monkeyPatch `PackFileCacheStrategy.createSnapshot` into filtering out `package.json` files. That fixed the issue, but the price is a bit too expensive.

It would be nice to have:
- a simple `tapable` before `createSnapshot` method call to insert custom plugin logic (maybe a `tapable` inside `Cache` to return an instance of `the Strategy`?);
- a way to output the list of mismatched buildDependencies paths, so that it's possible to rewrite configuration files avoiding mismatch (possibly another `tapable`, or a publicly available field inside `PackFileCacheStrategy`).

body:
This is a valid documentation issue and most likely belongs to the [Documentation repository](https://github.com/webpack/webpack.js.org).
 
In the [Development Guide under Using Watch Mode section](https://webpack.js.org/guides/development/#using-watch-mode) the content says:

> The only downside is that you have to refresh your browser in order to see the changes. It would be much nicer if that would happen automatically as well, so let's try webpack-dev-server which will do exactly that.

The follow-on section about webpack-dev-server is expected to provide guidance about creating a setup that would reload new code as it is written and refresh the browser to show the changes. But the code example doesnot do so. The problem is in the `webpack.config.js` code example. The `devServer` configuration as mentioned will not refresh the browser. To actually refresh the page the minimal configuration that needs to be added to `devServer` is 
`watchFiles: ['src/**/*.*'],`
author: avishek143

body: What do you mean - `refresh`? `watchFiles` only for reload when you change externals files (i.e. not used in bundle, for example `.php`/`.py`/etc)
author: alexander-akait

body:
> What do you mean - `refresh`?

refresh the browser means reload the browser with the new changed code.

> `watchFiles` only for reload when you change externals files (i.e. not used in bundle, for example .php/.py/etc)

I don't think that is the case. If it is so the documentation must state the same. And there is no code anywhere which will prevent us from mentioning a file that is bundled in the `watchFiles` list.

Anyways, let's focus on the issue at hand. It is simple. The mentioned Guide and associated example doesn't reload the browser with changed code as stated. It needs to be rectified. My PR tries to do that. If there's a different solution let's apply that and rectify the Guide.

body:
you don't need to bundle worker first. In webpack@5 you can (and should) use 
```js
 new Worker(new URL('<esm worker script>', import.meta.url))
```
webpack will add `<esm worker script>` as a separate entry point and bundle it.
______

If you want to use bundled version you should use
```js
const workerUrl = new URL("worker", import.meta.url);
const worker = new Worker(workerUrl);
```

author: theOnlyBoy

body:
`runtimeValue` evaluated for module, but module could be in several entries (when evaluation happens it is unknown how much entries use this module). e.g.
webpack.config.js
```js
module.exports = { entry: { a: "./a", b: "./b" } };
```
module with runtime value (file `module.js`)
```js
const a = __RUNTIME_VALUE__;
```
e.g. in entry `a` we have module chain a -> c -> d -> module.js
in entry `b` we have module chain b -> module.js

So `__RUNTIME_VALUE__` should be evaluated once but we can not determine entry for this module.

we have added "smart" error, check `webpack@latest` you will get something like
```
BREAKING CHANGE: The request './nonce' failed to resolve only because it was resolved as fully specified
(probably because the origin is strict EcmaScript Module, e. g. a module with javascript mimetype, a '*.mjs' file, or a '*.js' file where the package.json contains '"type": "module"').
The extension in the request is mandatory for it to be fully specified.
Add the extension to the request.
```

author: indiketa

body: I think i've narrowed the error in the webpack dynamic import module. Preparing a minimal example repo...
author: indiketa

body:
The issue is:
When a module exports nothing inside a dynamic import chunk, loading another module inside the chunk will make entire loading fail.


See this repo:
https://github.com/indiketa/webpack5-dynamic-import-issue.git

Sorry, creating one without my whole project would probably take the rest of the week (I know how that sounds). I was hoping for a tip on how to narrow down what could be causing the problem. The only setting in webpack I see to observe what's happening when trying to build is `--progress` and it just sits at 10% eating up CPU and memory:

`10% building 0/1 entries 0/0 dependencies 0/0 modules`

Is there any other verbose or debug mode to indicate what parts of the config are being executed?

I tried `cache: false` to no luck.

author: tmeasday

body:
@vankop I dug into this a bit and I think I have some more context on what is going wrong here, in case it helps. Apologies in advance for my lack of understanding of how HMR stuff works in detail.

So, just to reiterate, what's happening in [the failing repro above](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15541#issuecomment-1098860467) is that we are `import()`-ing three files with a very small delay between each import. 

Actually the issue can happen even when even importing two files with a delay, but it doesn't happen every time. It's interesting to see what happen when it *does* work vs when it does.

----

When the **lazy compilation works**, on the terminal (with all logging turned on), I see:

```
[webpack-cli] Compiler is watching files for updates...
[LazyCompilationBackend] /Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/a.js is now in use and will be compiled.
[LazyCompilationBackend] /Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/b.js is now in use and will be compiled.
[webpack-cli] File 'null' was modified
[webpack-cli] Changed time is Wed Feb 13 54385 03:50:43 GMT+1100 (Australian Eastern Daylight Time) (timestamp is 1654061359843)
[webpack-dev-middleware] Compilation starting...
[webpack-cli] Compiler starting...
[webpack-cli] Compiler is using config: '/Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/webpack.config.js'
[webpack-cli] Compiler finished
[webpack-dev-middleware] Compilation finished
```

Notice in particular the two messages `.... is now in use and will be compiled.` that come in around the same time. This message is logged right before the [`lazyCompilationBackend` calls `compiler.watching.invalidate()`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/96da7660021e8aa31e163bacd3515960eb253422/lib/hmr/lazyCompilationBackend.js#L85-L86).

The two modules do not actually get "used" at the same time, there is a 2ms gap between them, but for whatever reason the new compilation triggered by the invalidate hasn't actually started yet.

On the client, on the HMR socket we see a really straightforward set of messages for a single HMR update and it all works fine:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/132554/171334596-126955d1-1a65-437c-a58e-4aecbfbd28b0.png)

---

When it doesn't work, **it is because the compilation starts before the second invalidation**:

```
[webpack-cli] Compiler is watching files for updates...
[LazyCompilationBackend] /Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/a.js is now in use and will be compiled.
[webpack-cli] File 'null' was modified
[webpack-cli] Changed time is Sun Feb 10 54385 23:26:14 GMT+1100 (Australian Eastern Daylight Time) (timestamp is 1654061171174)
[webpack-dev-middleware] Compilation starting...
[webpack-cli] Compiler starting...
[webpack-cli] Compiler is using config: '/Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/webpack.config.js'
<w> [LazyCompilationBackend] Error: read ECONNRESET
<w>     at TCP.onStreamRead (internal/stream_base_commons.js:209:20) {
<w>   errno: -54,
<w>   code: 'ECONNRESET',
<w>   syscall: 'read'
<w> }
[LazyCompilationBackend] /Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/b.js is now in use and will be compiled.
[webpack-cli] File 'null' was modified
[webpack-cli] Changed time is Sun Feb 10 54385 23:26:32 GMT+1100 (Australian Eastern Daylight Time) (timestamp is 1654061171192)
[webpack-cli] Compiler starting...
[webpack-cli] Compiler is using config: '/Users/tom/GitHub/Repros/webpack-lazy-compilation-repro-simple/webpack.config.js'
[webpack-cli] Compiler finished
[webpack-dev-middleware] Compilation finished
```

In this case, notice the "Compilation starting..." message is logged **before** the "...b.js is now in use and will be compiled." message. Ultimately this leads to the following messages over the websocket:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/132554/171334686-5aabe73c-2717-4b20-86b5-433b807bb35c.png)

(Here's where I get a bit more hazy) -- what this means is that there are actually two "changes" in compilation -- we go from an initial hash, to a second hash with `a.js`, to a third hash with `a.js` and `b.js`. However, the HMR mechanism tries to fetch the `hot-update.json` based on the initial hash, after the compilation of the third hash, and it no longer exists in the dev server.

I am not sure exactly what the root problem is. I suspect that maybe the lazy compilation backend shouldn't (or shouldn't be able to) invalidate the compilation **while it is compiling**. Perhaps somewhere in the pipeline there should be a queue of invalidations that blocks on previous compilations?
author: tmeasday

body:
@vankop FYI I implemented a workaround on SB's end by putting some pipelining/throttling in our client side code to avoid overlapping `import()` calls: https://github.com/storybookjs/storybook/pull/18432

To be clear, I don't think this is a great solution because
 (a) it feels like it's the wrong place for the fix (it's crude as it doesn't really know when the compilation is done, it probably slows things down).
 (b) it doesn't help if there is >1 client, which is the scenario from the original reproduction on this ticket (and is still an issue for us).

I wonder if the right solution is to add a similar throttle to [this `requestListener`](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/96da7660021e8aa31e163bacd3515960eb253422/lib/hmr/lazyCompilationBackend.js#L53) so that incoming requests are queued up and not handled until a previous compilation triggered by [this invalidation](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/96da7660021e8aa31e163bacd3515960eb253422/lib/hmr/lazyCompilationBackend.js#L85) is complete?

I tried it out and it did resolve the problem, although I did need to put a little delay in (>10ms <100ms) between one compilation finishing and the next one starting (I think because otherwise the HMR update gets invalidated before the browser has a chance to grab it, although I didn't dig in too much). I'm really unsure if this is the right approach or not (plus how I'd go about adding a test to webpack for it).

body:
So the problem here. I think, is the remote container initialization phase. If a host marks it as external the runtime should still register react, and just point to the global variable. 
So the share scope still contains the keys to pass them into remote containers. Otherwise if my remote has import:false set - it crashes because share scope is missing keys, but loadSingleton is still part of the logic
author: ScriptedAlchemy

body:
@ScriptedAlchemy tbh it's been a while since i've had this problem, but yeah, iirc being able to just stick the externalized react in the share-scope (if i remember my terminology right) would do the job, i.e. the remote module would then be able to pick it up. but i didn't have enough experience with webpack externals to do that myself ­¤śģ

also not sure what to do with the version-negotiation stuff -- how do we deal with the fact that we don't (and can't) know what version the externalized module has? make the user specify "i pinky promise it'll be react@17.0.2"? or perhaps fallback it to some magic `*`/ `ALWAYS_WINS_NEGOTIATION` value, since the user clearly doesn't want the module loaded via webpack? or maybe allow choosing either, with a config switch...? hairy stuff...
author: lubieowoce

body:
Well if the webpack still registered externals in its api/share scope. Then you could manually specify the version and make the import value be external React 

That way share scope registers the global variable along with its version and if it's singleton or not. 

Ideally it's still tracked, just not imported. 

If you had mixed remotes. Where some may not set external, things would break. So this would be nice to still just keep the register() methods in the runtime. 

This causes me problems in node envs where react isn't bundled. Just require()d - I had to make sure all remotes treated react as external too because the host is missing keys.

body: In two days (2024-10-17) Rust 1.82 will be released, and reference types will be on by default. The only way to build without reference types will be to use nightly and rebuild the standard library without reference types support.
author: bes

body: /cc @xtuc
author: alexander-akait

body:
Rust 1.82.0 is now released with the following note

> The WebAssembly target features multivalue and reference-types are now both enabled by default. These two features both have subtle changes implied for generated WebAssembly binaries. For the multivalue feature, WebAssembly target support has changed when upgrading to LLVM 19. Support for generating functions with multiple returns no longer works and -Ctarget-feature=+multivalue has a different meaning than it did in LLVM 18 and prior. There is no longer any supported means to generate a module that has a function with multiple returns in WebAssembly from Rust source code. For the reference-types feature the encoding of immediates in the call_indirect, a commonly used instruction by the WebAssembly backend, has changed. Validators and parsers which don't understand the reference-types proposal will no longer accept modules produced by LLVM due to this change in encoding of immediates. Additionally these features being enabled are encoded in the target_features custom section and may affect downstream tooling such as wasm-opt consuming the module. Generating a WebAssembly module that disables default features requires -Zbuild-std support from Cargo and more information can be found at rust-lang/rust#128511.

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/releases/tag/1.82.0

body:
Stripping solves the problem:
```toml
[profile.release]
strip = true
```
This isn't great for debug builds however, as you usually want the debug symbols. I believe the reason why stripping works isn't because it does any stripping, but because it likely optimizes the overlong immediates encoding back to how it was before LLVM 19. Running `wasm-opt` probably behaves the same way.

body: Note that in big projects with a workspace having multiple crates, the current webpack limitation is really annoying, because due to a [cargo limitation](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/profiles.html#profiles), the strip option must be specified at the workspace level, so that would affect all crates including the ones not compiled for WASM targets. As @CryZe mentioned, that is really a problem for debugging.
author: stephanemagnenat

body: On the weekend I tracked down the issue back into `wasm-bindgen` which apparently uses the `target_features` section to detect if it should automatically enable its full reference types support. I opened a PR that ensures that you still have to opt into that manually, so that should fix all issues that were caused by Rust 1.82: https://github.com/rustwasm/wasm-bindgen/pull/4213

body:
There is a fix #15581 ... It passes tests but the pipeline doesn't want to run on the PR

As for removing duplicate resolve.modules, that's the default of vue-cli, so not really an option given that the bug is indeed here, that was the whole point of this issue and fix in the first place, which took me hours to track down so I can imagine future users trying to use this in a vue project only to find the same bug, only to find after 2hours of debug and searching issues that the only workaround is to do

```js
  chainWebpack: config => {
    config.resolve.modules.delete('node_modules');
  }
```

The problem is the resolution adds an item like
`{context: 'node_modules/vuetify/lib/locales', request: 'vuetify/lib/locales/en.js' }`

When it should be 
`{context: 'node_modules', request: 'vuetify/lib/locales/en.js' }`

Or 

`{context: 'node_modules/vuetify/lib/locales', request: 'en.js' }` (but this one breaks tests)

body: Just to give some context -- I'm currently developing a proof of concept related to microfrontends utilising webpack module federation. I'm also using NX (https://nx.dev/) to handle the different microfrontends. NX has this notion of only having a single `package.json` (root) instead of having a `package.json` per individual microfrontend/app. ### Solution Now, webpack assumes that you have defined a "unique" `name` inside your `package.json` file per microfrontend in order for it to work. Since I'm using NX, the solution I encountered is to modify the `uniqueName` attribute in webpack config so it doesn't rely on the `name` attribute from `package.json`. Source: https://webpack.js.org/configuration/ After I've added `uniqueName` per webpack config (one for each microfrontend/app) then it worked as expected and this error went away.
author: jponc

body: Excelent @jponc , I dont use NX, but effectively my diferent packages.json did not have the name property set. Adding it to all packages.json fixed it. Thanks

author: d0sboots

body:
So, here's how this reproduces on my machine. Create an empty directory, install webpack and (optionally) a version of Node:
```
$ mkdir newdir; cd newdir
$ npm i node@16 webpack webpack-cli --fund=false --audit=false

added 122 packages in 10s
$ npm exec -- node -v
v16.14.0
```

Trying to run webpack results in the error:
```
$ npm exec -- webpack
[webpack-cli] RangeError: WebAssembly.Instance(): Out of memory: wasm memory
    at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/util/hash/wasm-hash.js:154:4)
    at module.exports (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/util/createHash.js:160:27)
    at /home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/DefinePlugin.js:289:22
    at _next31 (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:42:1)
    at _next9 (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:97:1)
    at Hook.eval [as call] (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:119:1)
    at Hook.CALL_DELEGATE [as _call] (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/Hook.js:14:14)
    at Compiler.newCompilation (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compiler.js:1109:26)
    at /home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compiler.js:1153:29
    at Hook.eval [as callAsync] (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:6:1)
```

I was wrong in my guess about the instances fix in V8; using a Node version past 17.2.0 has no effect:

```
$ npm i node@17 --fund=false --audit=false

changed 1 package in 5s

$ npm exec -- node -v
v17.7.1
$ npm exec -- webpack
[webpack-cli] RangeError: WebAssembly.Instance(): Out of memory: wasm memory
    at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/util/hash/wasm-hash.js:154:4)
    at module.exports (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/util/createHash.js:160:27)
    at /home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/DefinePlugin.js:289:22
    at _next31 (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:42:1)
    at _next9 (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:97:1)
    at Hook.eval [as call] (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:119:1)
    at Hook.CALL_DELEGATE [as _call] (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/Hook.js:14:14)
    at Compiler.newCompilation (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compiler.js:1109:26)
    at /home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compiler.js:1153:29
    at Hook.eval [as callAsync] (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:1:1), <anonymous>:6:1)
```

But it's *definitely* caused by a change in Webpack; syncing before the m4-based wasm hashing was introduced in 5.61 fixes it, always.

```
$ npm i webpack@5.60 --fund=false --audit=false

added 1 package, and changed 1 package in 877ms
$ npm exec -- webpack
[webpack-cli] Error: error:0308010C:digital envelope routines::unsupported
    at new Hash (node:internal/crypto/hash:67:19)
    at Object.createHash (node:crypto:135:10)
    at BulkUpdateDecorator.hashFactory (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/util/createHash.js:155:18)
    at BulkUpdateDecorator.digest (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/util/createHash.js:80:21)
    at /home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/DefinePlugin.js:595:38
    at _next30 (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:19:10), <anonymous>:42:1)
    at _next8 (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:19:10), <anonymous>:97:1)
    at Hook.eval [as call] (eval at create (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:19:10), <anonymous>:117:1)
    at Hook.CALL_DELEGATE [as _call] (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/tapable/lib/Hook.js:14:14)
    at Compiler.newCompilation (/home/davedude/newdir/node_modules/webpack/lib/Compiler.js:1053:26) {
  opensslErrorStack: [ 'error:03000086:digital envelope routines::initialization error' ],
  library: 'digital envelope routines',
  reason: 'unsupported',
  code: 'ERR_OSSL_EVP_UNSUPPORTED'
}
```
(The above is the error that the m4-based hashes were added to address, in Node 17. It works fine in earlier Nodes, you get a sensible error about lacking a src/ dir.)

body:
Got around to digging further on this. I suspected this was something specific to my env as well.

```
$ node
Welcome to Node.js v14.19.1.
Type ".help" for more information.
> foo = new WebAssembly.Memory({ initial:1 })
Uncaught RangeError: WebAssembly.Memory(): could not allocate memory
```

This shows that WASM is basically totally inoperable; trying to allocate anything at all is failing. I'll update again if/when I get to the bottom of this, so that if anyone else runs into the same issue they'll know how to fix it.
author: d0sboots

body:
I found the issue.

V8 uses 10GB memory allocations in WASM to implement guard regions. Most of this memory is never committed (actually given pages by the VM system), but the _virtual address space_ is still consumed.

I had a 5GB ulimit set, for safety. Once that was removed, everything worked fine.
author: d0sboots

body: @d0sboots how did you do that? I catch the same error
author: Alexsab

body: @d0sboots how did you do that? I catch the same error
author: etayakee

body:
If you put in `ulimit -a`, you should see something like this:
```
-t: cpu time (seconds)              unlimited
-f: file size (blocks)              unlimited
-d: data seg size (kbytes)          unlimited
-s: stack size (kbytes)             8192
-c: core file size (blocks)         0
-m: resident set size (kbytes)      unlimited
-u: processes                       31275
-n: file descriptors                1024
-l: locked-in-memory size (kbytes)  1004968
-v: address space (kbytes)          unlimited
-x: file locks                      unlimited
-i: pending signals                 31275
-q: bytes in POSIX msg queues       819200
-e: max nice                        0
-r: max rt priority                 0
-N 15: rt cpu time (microseconds)   unlimited
```
If the values for `-d` (data seg size), `-m` (resident set size), or especially `-v` (address space) are not "unlimited", that means you have a ulimit set and you're probably having the same issue I did.

As to how to fix it, it'll be set in the shell files... somewhere. Maybe your `.profile`, `.bashrc`, etc. Maybe the system version of the same. It depends on what shell you're running, what OS/distribution, etc.

body:
webpack does not track transitive `sideEffects` ( in your case it technically should be since `index.js` depends on `injectDevTools.js`), so you should mark all files by yourself.

In other words `sideEffects` flag does not have transitive state, so in your case `index.js` is side effects free.

body:
`dependOn` means that parent entry is loaded already, so in case:
```
entry A depends on entry B
```
when executing entry A all modules from entry B already loaded and presented in modules cache.

but I'm afraid this option does not work well with libraries, only for bundling application code.

author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait thanks for question.
In a nutshell i demonstrate problem in [this example](https://github.com/lavcraft/react-hooks-composition-in-webpack-entry-problem-example).

Some details and history
1. I have a project with sdk and subprojects who use this sdk and shared project (shared between sdk, ui and backend). 
1.1. shared project included to `ui project` as builded dependency as a npm package. (For test purpose. Dogfood this package before distribute to external project)
1.2 shared project included to `backend and sdk projects` as typescript project reference

That's mean i need to build my sdk with tool, which support typescript and project references.
At first, i built this via rollup with some hacks and separated tsc process. But it's not reliable way and depended on build call order. Also it's **not** simply development process
In this configuration i have problem with typescript paths and aliases.

My next step - try other build systems for packaging sdk. I chose `webpack` , because ts-loader is good integrated with typescript and working good with project reference.

After build sdk with webpack, new problem will appear. This problem have a good explanation by @gaearon in [react issues](https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/24230#issuecomment-1084470436).
In short:
1. SDK must have a two different endpoints for avoiding load code, which not support SSR (external WebRTC sdk).
1.1 First endpoint contain `useCall()` hook with references to files which import external WebRTC packages.
1.2 Second endpoint contain `useDevices()` hook, which can be used in a lot of pages without full WebRTC call
2. But `useDevices` also required in `useCall()` for obtain current devices. And it's problem, because
```
withCall(withDevices(component)):
  useDevices() <-- return devices
   useCall()
     useDevices()  <-- return undefined, because React.useContext(DevicesContext) check references via equals, and in isolated webpack modules contain independent instance of this.
```

From this point i start trying to resolve this problem via `dependOn` and share logic between two endpoints. May there is a more suitable way to solve this, but i couldn't find it now :(
author: lavcraft

body: Usually what you should do is extract the shared part into a separate bundle, and then use `externals` to prevent it from being copied into other bundles.
author: gaearon

body:
@gaearon right, i understand it. Thanks for this workaround. I prefer to see things through :) And i believe, if some tool have ability - it should works unless in docs not specified that it doesn't work :)

In this case, in my opinion, webpack should work with dependOn for library mode. It can simplify projects and provide ability to share code between entrypoints. Believe that webpack can be a best tool for build libraries and ready to try fix some problems with it

author: Piemontez

body:
@Piemontez Yep, that is `ts` resolver problem, firstly `ts` converts files to `js` and then `webpack` parses `js` and uses own resolver, so `extensions` will not work for `ts`, because `ts` has own resolver. 

Solutions:
1. `transpileOnly` https://github.com/TypeStrong/ts-loader#transpileonly (Recommended), just move types validation step from build (it will speedup build), and also it will emit files as is and webpack resolver will work
2. Use `paths` and create different `tsconfig.json` for `desktop` and `mobile` envs and load conditional files like modules, i.e. `import { loadfile } from "operations";` and set `operation` on different paths for desktop and mobile
3. Use `import()` and load files based on env (also good way)
4. Create custom ts plugin (it can be not easy) 
5. Rely on tree shaking and keep code together (you need ESM and avoid side effects as much as possible) 

Sorry, we can't fix it on webpack side

body:
yeah, thats how current webpack version works.. tree-shaking happens after building a tree.

Also this will not work great with incremental builds and cache. For incremental build in your case all sub tree should be build since all subtree will be invalidated in cache ( if e.g. on watch you will change imports ). Right now only affected module will be invalidated. ( Since we store all modules tree and "side effects free" does not affect module tree )

_____
Maybe this could be improved in the next major version
author: vankop

body:
Yeah this would then be a real nice feature.

Question still remains: is there only treebuilding happening? or is there also compilation upon the subtree? or something else that is expensive? Because I don't understand why it's 12x slower. Parsing some imports in couple of files shouldn't take that long.

In case of incremental builds I am not sure I follow. Why does the whole subtree need to be invalidated in cache for that file that is changed? As I see upon change only imports need to be checked what was added and what was removed. If treeshaken module was imported on change only then load that subtree and add to cache. Wouldn't this work similarly to how new imports are added from new unseen files for webpack?
author: archfz

body:
> is there only treebuilding happening?

all modules are parsed.

> or is there also compilation upon the subtree?

not sure what you mean by compilation.. module graph is optimizing  after exports analyze ( side effects apply here ). no output generated for "tree-shaken" modules

> Why does the whole subtree need to be invalidated in cache for that file that is changed?

Not modules itself by exports that this modules provide.
e.g. in case `A` module  import `B` module and `B` imports unused `C` module (A -> B -> C and C is unused and side effect free)
So on first build A and partial B module exports is known.
If we change module A the way that C module is used, this will trigger exports invalidation for module B + parsing/analyze module C subtree. Things become a lot more complex when one module is imported in different modules ( common scenario )

Maybe this could be optimized, but definitely things are more complex comparing to how right now this works ( building tree, then optimize )

author: vankop

body:
I will do some more fiddleing to see the impact of the discovery vs loaders running on graph building.

But my initial thought is that for graph building needing loaders to run through the code is an edge case, and maybe should be able to opt out. As what is and what isn't imported in most cases for sideffectfree js won't be changed by loaders. But maybe I am in the wrong here.
author: archfz

body:
I've made another branch https://github.com/archfz/webpack-treeshake-issue/tree/no_babel_on_modules and here I transformed all jsx in the 3rd party packages to js. I've also made babel not parse files from node modules.

With this change it's confirmed that the loaders are also run on module graph building. Now the slowdown is down from `12x` to only `3.5x`. Which is a big improvement, but still not quite there. This would then essentially mean that module graph building of unimported side effect free modules still add a lot of wasted resource usage.

My argument still stands. I don't see the point, in 99% of cases, running the loaders on js files prior to module graph building. Babel here is a very good example, as most probably all projects configure this loader to compile all files, even ones loaded from third party packages, due to the fact that one cannot know for sure what that package targets as ecmascript version.

Would it be easily possible to introduce an optout flag for loaders for module graph building? Or are there any counter arguments?
author: archfz

you should use `	compilation.hooks.optimizeChunkModules` with 
```js
{
   name: "Your-Plugin-Name",
   stage: STAGE_BASIC
}
```

to run before concatenation https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/ModuleConcatenationPlugin.js#L117

body:
@vankop what if the public path is not known at build time but only at runtime? 
with `postTransformPublicPath` you could pass code, which would be executed in the browser and evaluate the public path there.


related (unresolved) stack overflow questions about this feature:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69401060/migrate-from-file-loader-with-posttransformpublicpath-to-asset-modules
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/69031444/webpack-5-asset-module-missing-feature-of-url-loader-posttransformpublicpa?noredirect=1&lq=1
author: ReinisV

body:
I was really struggling with this ŌĆō somehow missing the following remark in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15693#issuecomment-1104984527:

> wait.. I think if you use `publicPath` it will not concatenate `__webpack_public_path__ + ${p}`, otherwise it will respect `__webpack_public_path__` ( this need to be documented, yes)

So my current understanding is:

1. Generally, `__webpack_public_path__` is still supported with asset modules
2. If `generator.publicPath` is *undefined* `__webpack_public_path__` will be prepended to an assetŌĆÖs path
3. **If `generator.publicPath` is *defined* this disables prepending of `__webpack_public_path__`**
4. `generator.postTransformPublicPath` is *not supported* with asset modules ŌĆō `generator.publicPath` must be used for piecing together the desired path (see 3.)

It looks like this is stilll undocumented, especially 3. ŌĆō which is not obvious at all.

body: @alexander-akait If you follow the logic, the exact same behaviour happens - `import()` is called with `./4.js`. The only change is that in my proposal, there is no abstraction between the `import()` function and its argument, so it's fully statically analysable.
author: jacobp100

body: Why you need it? There are more logic in `i.t`, look at uncompressed code, if you want to create library with statically analyzable code there is another issue about it
author: alexander-akait

body:
> If you're using webpack to produce libraries that may be used in other build tools (including webpack), statically analysable imports improve optimisations

Webpack itself really benefits from having static import expressions - so it only makes sense that it should be able to output them as such.

With the changes I've demonstrated, it does work as expected. It doesn't really matter what's happening in `i.t.` here - that happens after the module has been imported.
author: jacobp100

body:
> Webpack itself really benefits from having static import expressions - so it only makes sense that it should be able to output them as such.

Again, we have public path and we should respect it `import("./4.js"))` will not work, not each application have relative assets, there are more different approaches to store assets.

Also there are more JS size, that is not good, can you answer why you need it?
author: alexander-akait

body:
Yeah - if you set a public path, the `"./4.js"` would be updated in accordance with that. That's what currently happens in the `i.u` function. I'm not seeing the issue.

> Also there are more JS size

It's duplicated though, so it will hardly affect anything after gzip

> can you answer why you need it?

I'm really not sure which bit isn't clear. Non-statically analysable dynamic imports cause problems for about every build tool - including webpack. If you use webpack to build a library that makes use of a statically analysable dynamic import in its uncompiled code, it is not possible to produce an output that has this dynamic import and retains the static analysis.

As you can see in the example, `import("./test")` was fully statically analysable, and webpack knew exactly what to do. In the ouput, `import("./" + someFunction)` is not fully statically analysable.
author: jacobp100

body:
> Yeah - if you set a public path, the "./4.js" would be updated in accordance with that. That's what currently happens in the i.u function. I'm not seeing the issue.

Default public path is "auto", so we need `currentScript` and more logic than just `update string`

> It's duplicated though, so it will hardly affect anything after gzip

Not really

> I'm really not sure which bit isn't clear. Non-statically analysable dynamic imports cause problems for about every build tool - including webpack. If you use webpack to build a library that makes use of a statically analysable dynamic import in its uncompiled code, it is not possible to produce an output that has this dynamic import and retains the static analysis.

There is another issue about it as I written above https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/2933

There are two common solutions for using webpack:
- application, we need more logic for public path and it is impossible to output statically analyzable `import()` (in theory it is possible but we many limitations and I don't see any cons to do it)
- library https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/2933, you need to use it to setup webpack to output statically analyzable code

body:
don't use chunks in  your case ( you should control chunking in your application config ), to remove extra chunks you can use
```js
plugins: [
        new webpack.optimize.LimitChunkCountPlugin({ maxChunks: 1 })
    ]
```
also there are other cjs library types https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#outputlibrarytype
author: vankop

body:
Thanks, limiting the chunks to 1 fixes the .d.ts issue.

The issue that the application puts everything into a single chunk including code that is not needed at runtime still persists though. No wonder when there is only a single library chunk. Not sure if that is fixable with the other output library types. I guess not. Probably I need to make my lib tree-shakable and directly build it with tsc instead of webpack to keep the dynamic imports.

body:
Unfortunately, I can't provide reproducible test repo, but just in case someone will have the same issue, here is a workaround:
you'll need to disable [IIFE](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#outputiife), by setting `iife: false` inside your Webpack's configuration file.

This is either a bug inside of one of the plugins that are being used in the project that I'm working on, or it might be a bug [inside Webpack](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/3ad4fcac25a976277f2d9cceb37bc81602e96b13/lib/javascript/JavascriptModulesPlugin.js#L920-L922). Either way, this issue started affecting only after Webpack's upgrade from v4 to v5.

body:
I thought it was more of a bug, as the [Webpack CSP docs ](https://webpack.js.org/guides/csp/) say:
> Webpack is capable of adding nonce to all scripts that it loads

This is script it both loads and generates.  There's really no workaround, except having a wide open CSP.  Regardless of how it's categorized, I'm really hopeful the team can implement the fix and/or add the feature soon. Module federation is very cool. 
Thank you :)

author: vankop

body:
Hi all, 
with addition of [strict-dynamic](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy/script-src#strict-dynamic) in CSP3 nonce is getting more important. 
Here is a nice [article](https://towardsdatascience.com/content-security-policy-how-to-create-an-iron-clad-nonce-based-csp3-policy-with-webpack-and-nginx-ce5a4605db90) that explains issues with current __webpack_nonce__ implementation. 

TLDR: That nonce will not be added to entry bundle js script, and without it CSP will block it.

It would be nice if there would be a config for webpack to inject nonce to those scripts as well. Of course it would be more of a placeholder for a value that will be provided via server since it should be unique per request. 
Maybe it could be a settings for [html-webpack-plugin](https://github.com/jantimon/html-webpack-plugin) as well.
@trevorgithub I verified that solution from the article works, and will probably go in that direction for now. So maybe it helps you as well.

body:
@vankop I see, I will update the code accordingly, pardon my ignorance, I have a hard time understanding the way modules are exported/imported, but just to see if I understood more or less of what is happening:

with `import ReactDOM from 'react-dom'` in build time, webpack tries to import the `default export` from `react-dom` which doesn't exist, therefore it is evaluated as `false`, but it exists in runtime because of something webpack (or depending on the tools babel, or tsconfig) does to normalize the way the imports are made.

by importing with `import * as ReactDOM from 'react-dom'` we are importing everything that is exported (and not just the `default`) with the name of `ReactDOM` which can then be evaluated correctly.

one last question: in build time can't know we that the syntax `import ReactDOM from 'react-dom'` would be "wrong", so that we are able to bail out from the evaluation and keep the original value?

body:
> redundant variables in our configurations that are not getting replaced

I think warning in this case should be enough..
_________

Not sure how this could be implemented with current api.. maybe something like:

```js
new webpack.DefinePlugin({
    'process.env.BACKEND_URL': webpack.DefinePlugin.warnNoUse('someurl'),
    'process.env.NICE_FEATURE': webpack.DefinePlugin.warnNoUse(JSON.stringify(true)),
    'process.env.UNUSED_VAR': webpack.DefinePlugin.warnNoUse('somevalue'),
});
```
author: vankop

body:
I'm not so sure about the `.warnNoUse` notation. It most likely will lead to the following use case:
```js
new webpack.DefinePlugin({
    'process.env.BACKEND_URL': process.env.ENABLE_NOUSE_LOGGING 
        ? webpack.DefinePlugin('someurl')
        : webpack.DefinePlugin.warnNoUse('someurl'),
})
```
Which would bloat the config file. However, I must confess that I'm not familiar with the webpack API and I didn't have enough time to study it more in-depth. I'm a little busy right now, but I will take a closer look next week (starting May 16th) and try to see if it is possible to implement the unused values logging using a flag.

Worst case scenario, if it's not possible / too costly to implement the unused values logging, then someone who is actually interested if they have unused values could simply use the regular logs (ex `DefinePlugin: replaced 'process.env.BACKEND_URL' with 'someurl' in file backendClient.jsx at line 11.`) to see _**which values got used and compare with their list of all values, to see which ones remained unused**_.

body:
like this in your dist/index.html
```html
<script src="{your project path}/webpack-dynamic-demo/dist/src_dynamic-module_js.bundle.js"></script>
```
when the website run with index.html, the webpack_require__.l will try to append the dynamic import script to html.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/168572587-5e16108c-c2f7-484f-bc2f-838a47d4cb0a.png)
But at first, it will judge if the html has the script same tag, and set the 'needAttach' value.
eg. in the demo, when the index.html run, it will try to append the script with the src of 'src_dynamic-module_js.bundle.js', but at first in the judge function, it already exists, so it will not append to html. 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/168572799-6a607641-5fce-454e-a44e-70abf149d29a.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/37408756/168574580-bf339d44-2b88-4091-82b3-a2fbf2fb84dd.png)

Then, in the code behind, it will define a timeout about this js resource, but not append it to html dom

So when we have an error with network of 'src_dynamic-module_js.bundle.js', the script of it will load with error in Chrome. And as the code executes to load the dynamic import js, it judge and define timeout but not append script.
The final phenomenon is the onerror with the onScriptComplete will throw after the timeout code is executed’╝ł120000ms)

body:
Okay, I looked at https://github.com/rubixibuc/broken-mf-eager, and sorry you can't do it, because of `Shared module is not available for eager consumption`, shorty:

Here import:
```
import React from 'react'
```

but it can't be here, because (`react` is shared):
```
new ModuleFederationPlugin({
  name: "app1",
  remotes: {
    app2: "app2@[app2Url]/remoteEntry.js",
  },
  shared: {react: {singleton: true, eager: true}, "react-dom": {singleton: true, eager: true}},
}),
```

webpack can't init shared module(s) in such way (i.e. synchronously) - because module should be loaded once and share own state beween different application, there is an answer and solution https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/#troubleshooting:

You need to use and load them in `bootstrap.js`
```
import('./bootstrap.js');
```

We can't do it in the sync mode, sorry

Also you have `ExternalTemplateRemotesPlugin`, but it is only for `externals` (module.constructor.name === 'ExternalModule' https://github.com/module-federation/external-remotes-plugin/blob/main/index.js#L17), there are no externals, but yes, you can write:
```
 externals: {
    react: "React",
    "react-dom": "ReactDOM",
  },
```  

But in this case you need to have such externals - you can have `script` with CDN `src` and extra code for loading this in global scope

So I don't see any bugs here, everything works as expected.

Anyway feel free to feedback
author: alexander-akait

body:
> which tools support it? only Vite.js?

To my knowledge yes.

Webpack 5 has taken a big step by stepping away from Node in bundled code by removing NodeJS library polyfills from being included in bundles. Removing `process` seems like the next big step in the same direction.

ViteJS also provides a powerful tool to replace CommonJS `require` statements: [`import.meta.glob` and `import.meta.globEager`](https://vitejs.dev/guide/features.html#glob-import).
author: philippevezina

body: yeah, webpack supports `import.meta.webpackContext` https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15446
author: vankop

body: To be honestly I am always thinking what we should use `globalThis` to check env, i.e. `globalThis.ENV` /`globalThis.NODE_ENV`/etc , `import.meta` only for import meta and functions related to import (hot replacement/resolving/etc), not sure why we should store env in `import.meta.env`, also using `globalThis` is solution for any platform - browser/node/deno, `import.meta.env` only for bundlers, and you need again using bundler to change it, just my option
author: alexander-akait

body: For example we can found many issues like https://github.com/immerjs/immer/issues/557 (and workaround https://github.com/immerjs/immer/issues/557#issuecomment-621491287) for these packages, `import.meta.env` is an unofficial solution for any platform.
author: alexander-akait

body: Anyway we can merge https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/15841/ to solve problems with packages which already migrate on `import.meta.url`, but the question is more bigger, will be great if developers from Node.js, Deno, Rollup, Parcel, Esbuild and other had an discussion here. It is not good that vite just decide to use `import.meta.env` for env and start to recommend it, it can be destructive for ecosystem.
author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait Just FYI, I looked at the tc39 proposal for `import.meta`. It seems the ECMAScript committee decided it _should_ be extensible.[^1] Particular hosts, such as Node.js and Deno, may lock down the API if they choose. I wouldn't extend it for a Node.js script. But in the browser, it's fair game.

[^1]: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-import-meta#will-this-object-be-locked-down-in-any-way

body:
You can try to use [import-meta-loader](https://www.npmjs.com/package/import-meta-loader).
It will support you to use vite's import.meta in webpack. Because it can convert vite's import.meta syntax to webpack's syntax.

example:
```js
// vite => webpack
import.meta.env //=> process.env
import.meta.env.MODE //=> process.env.NODE_ENV
import.meta.env.BASE_URL //=> process.env.BASE_URL
import.meta.env.PROD //=> process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'
import.meta.env.DEV //=> process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production'

new URL('filePath', import.meta.url).href //=> require('filePath')

import.meta.glob('filePath') //=> ... require.context('dirPath', useSubdirectories: boolean, RegExp, 'lazy') ...
import.meta.glob('filePath', { eager: true }) //=> vite3 ... require.context('dirPath', useSubdirectories: boolean, RegExp, 'sync') ...
import.meta.globEager('filePath') //=> vite2 ... require.context('dirPath', useSubdirectories: boolean, RegExp, 'sync') ...
```

body:
I'd rather encourage the explicit import as mentioned above than rely on the bundler monkey patching attributes global variables that are then used in the code, after just having been bit by the problem. 

The dependency on the .env attributes leads to code that _fails at runtime_ if you build it with the wrong bundler. This is just technical debt imposed on anyone that uses a bundler with this kind of API in their projects, and that future linter authors will have to add a rule for.

body:
So..
For `shared module @apollo/client/core -> /.../mfe-secondary-entry-point-demo/node_modules/@apollo/client/core/index.js`
You have package.json in `/.../mfe-secondary-entry-point-demo/node_modules/@apollo/client/core` equals to
```json
{
  "name": "@apollo/client/core",
  "type": "module",
  "main": "core.cjs",
  "module": "index.js",
  "types": "index.d.ts",
  "sideEffects": false
}
```
and webpack use it instead of package.json `/.../mfe-secondary-entry-point-demo/node_modules/@apollo/client` since regarding Node.js resolve algorithm this is nearest package.json to source

Same for `shared module @apollo/client/link/batch -> /.../mfe-secondary-entry-point-demo/node_modules/@apollo/client/link/batch/index.js`
nearest package.json is `/.../mfe-secondary-entry-point-demo/node_modules/@apollo/client/link/batch`
```json
{
  "name": "@apollo/client/link/batch",
  "type": "module",
  "main": "batch.cjs",
  "module": "index.js",
  "types": "index.d.ts",
  "sideEffects": false
}
```

body:
Yep this does fix the issue!

The only potential issue I see is that `compilation.modules` is still out of order, so if some external plugin also relies on the order of `compilation.modules`, then it would also have an issue related to this. Would it make more sense to sort `compilation.modules` upstream of here? If `compilation.modules` always has deterministic ordering, then module concatenation will always have deterministic ordering (as far as I can tell). I know that factorizing in different orders shouldn't lead to different concat results, but it looks like it does because if you follow the callbacks up from the `factory.create` callback in `compilation.factorizeModule`, you eventually get to `compilation.addModule`, which results in the module that finishes factorizing first getting inserted to the `compilation.modules` set first.

Or is `compilation.modules` not intended to have a reliable order?
author: pgoldberg

body:
> Or is compilation.modules not intended to have a reliable order?

yes, they not intended.

body: need to take a look stats with `stats.source=true`, is it possible with `ng`?
author: vankop

body:
> this is some angular magic that fails with side-effects...

Indeed very weird, brought this up in https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/23246#issuecomment-1144573753

> need to take a look stats with `stats.source=true`, is it possible with `ng`?

You should be able to apply it in [`custom-webpack.config.json`](https://github.com/amitbeck/ng-webpack-sideEffects-repro/blob/9f0fc52d0210efc4cdedd2082e550a19e3a7fa9c/custom-webpack.config.js) which should extend Angular's webpack config. If for some reason that doesn't work, just set `stats.source=true` directly in Angular's webpack config in `node_modules/@angular-devkit/build-angular/src/webpack/configs/common.js`.
author: amitbeck
vankop

body:
Apparently [the Angular AOT compiler removes anything under `NgModule.declarations`](https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/23246#issuecomment-1144610149), such as `CustomControlStatusDirective`. It's indeed an issue with Angular's emitted code and not webpack. To overcome this, one must indeed add another reference to that import, such as [declaring a `static` property in that `NgModule` class](https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/21952#issuecomment-945752429).
@vankop Thanks a lot for the investigation!
author: amitbeck

body:
yeah, issue should be solved by preventing removing reexport. webpack@5 can handle reexport, so instead of 
```
import * as i2 from "./utils/forms/custom-control-status.directive";
```
should be
```
import * as i2 from "./utils/forms"; // or another form of importing "./index"
```
