author: abirmingham

body:
You're welcome.  Issue #17009 sounds potentially related.

I notice that the [list of entrypoints generated in the webpack config](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/webpack.config.js#L13-L16) seems deterministic (generated from numbers 0..6) - is there a reason to use that many input files?  The contents of each code file seem to be the same.
author: jayaddison

body: @jayaddison good question! While attempting to reproduce the issue, I noticed that projects under a certain size took a lot longer to reproduce the problem, or were unable to reproduce the problem at all. I wanted a short feedback loop, so I continued adding entrypoints until the problem was reliably reproduced in under 10 iterations. The same is true of the TS files in the `autogenerated` folder.
author: abirmingham

body:
Could you try removing more of the webpack config, JavaScript code in the modules, etc to continue to narrow this down without affecting the repeatability of it?  (I will try to do the same soon too, but not sure when I'll get around to it)

It'll probably be a slightly annoying process, but I think we should be able to continue to remove items until -- ideally -- there's a removal where we cross some threshold and the problem stops occurring.

Then there'll be the equally tricky part of debugging from there to determine why whatever small difference that is causes the problem in the first place.
author: jayaddison

body: Note: I briefly wondered whether [`realContentHash`](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/optimization/#optimizationrealcontenthash) could be relevant here; the documentation does mention that when it is set to `false`, content hashes may not be deterministic.  However: the [default for that setting is to match `production` flag](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/87660921808566ef3b8796f8df61bd79fc026108/lib/config/defaults.js#L1332) -- and that's set [`true` in the repro `webpack.config.js`](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/webpack.config.js#L19).
author: jayaddison

body:
A few more observations:

* Removing the [`threadloader`](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/webpack.config.js#L46) from the repro configuration seems to produce different results, something that's probably not ideal.
* From inspection of one of the differing-files using `cmp`, the difference tends to be in an object whose properties are integers and whose values look like hashes near the very end of the output `.js` files, just after a special-case `==='monaco'` check (maybe not relevant for the investigation, but a useful tracer: this may be due to some [`webpackChunkName` directives](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757/blob/28d3e3a5783d37e5543a2d28a151e67d43caea59/src/entrypoint_2.js#L27)).  Usually only one of the property values differs.
* It's possible to move most of the `repro.sh` contents into the `Dockerfile`, as long as the `webpack.config.js` and `src` directories are also `COPY`'d into the layers during build.  This is something I'm going to reduce size (albeit not really time, yet) of the iteration loop.
author: jayaddison

body:
I've confirmed that this variance is replicable as far back as `webpack=5.56.1`.  It may be replicable in earlier versions too, but that's the least-recent version I've tested so far (I was squinting at 3fa83c6c77de88b9874ecd06322ada787b90ac25 - but reproducing the issue on v5.56.1 predates that change, so I think it is unrelated).

So far it does seem that the [`thread-loader`](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/thread-loader) component makes this issue more repeatable.  Whether it is definitively the cause, however, I cannot yet confirm/reject.
author: jayaddison

body:
Hey @jayaddison - I was able to spend more time on this, and attempted the following changes:

#### 1
- Changes:
-- Dropped thread-loader to 2 workers, 2 workerParallelJobs
-- Dropped entrypoints from `Array(7)` to `Array(3)`
-- Load CPU: `for i in 1 2 3 4; do while : ; do : ; done & done`
- Results:
-- Reproduced on second build

#### 2
- Changes:
-- Dropped entrypoints from `Array(3)` to `Array(1)`
- Results:
-- Reproduced on 8th build
#### 3
- Changes:
-- Removed all extra entrypoints
- Results:
-- 13 builds failed to reproduce

#### 4
- Changes:
-- Reverted change # 3
-- Removed all references to `autogenerated` directory
- Results:
-- 50 builds failed to reproduce

#### 5
- Changes:
-- Reverted change #4
-- Removed half of autogenerated files
- Results:
-- Reproduced on 21st build

Here is the commit containing my stopping point, which can be seen in the repro repository on branch `10_23_2023_minimal_build`:
```
commit 21c561c1483963a1069943e5b351190faa990d89 (HEAD -> main, abirmingham-github/10_23_2023_minimal_build)
Author: Alex Birmingham <abirmingham@extrahop.com>
Date:   Mon Oct 23 15:39:51 2023 -0700

    Attempt More Minimal Reproduction
    
    - Delete half of autogenerated files
    - Reduce entrypoints from 8 to 2
    - Reduce thread-loader workers from 4 to 2
    - Reduce thread-loader workerParallelJobs from 3 to 
```

Note that in the final iteration the bug was only reproduced after the 21st build, and in a prior iteration I stopped running builds at 13 builds. So it is certainly possible that the bug would have been reproduced if builds had continued.

This is a tricky one. It's unclear to me whether the extra complexity is necessary or is simply helpful in reproducing the issue in a less prohibitive amount of time.

Thank you for your time!
author: abirmingham

body: Thanks - yep, more clues.  I do have a feeling that the `autogenerated` content -- and potentially the stack/module depth of it, the way that the modules are connected -- could be relevant.  And the entrypoints seem relevant, as you've confirmed.  At the moment I do think threading could be a relevant interaction too.  Perhaps it requires all three of those in combination, but we'll need more info to prove or disprove that.
author: jayaddison

I can roughly describe my situation, I hope that helps.

1. There are a lot of third-party utils packages exporting fn1, fn2, etc. and these packages are configured with sideEffects:false.
 
2. There is a large number of pages that use fn1, fn2, etc. For example, pageA uses fn1, fn2, etc. For example, pageA uses fn1, fn2, fn3, PageB uses fn1, fn3, PageC uses fn2, fn3. Many similar scenarios.

4. the output generates a large number of chunks and vendors. The presence of a large number of vendors is very similar. There are a lot of vendors that are very similar. The only difference in filenames is the contentHash.

5. The difference between the two packages is that the import vendors are not in the same order.
I've tried turning off compression and this still happens frequently.

body:
In fact, looking at the original problem now, I found that most of the changes were related only to the fact that the name of one chunk was changed. That is, when the name of one chunk changes, this change also occurs in other files and and that's why you see a lot of changed hashes. 

In the original problem this only happens in one place

Expected

body:
I investigated this issue using the reproduction repository:
[https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757](https://github.com/abirmingham/repro-webpack-issue-17757?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Results:

On Webpack 5.89.0, I was able to reproduce nondeterministic contenthash across builds.
The issue appeared most frequently when using thread-loader for parallel processing.
Changing other configuration options (e.g., optimization settings) did not eliminate the behavior.
After upgrading to Webpack 5.105.4, builds became deterministic.
Repeated builds (including with multiple thread-loader workers) no longer regenerated contenthash without input changes.

It appears the issue is no longer reproducible on recent Webpack versions.

Environment:
Node: v22.21.0
OS: Windows

It would be very helpful if you could try an earlier version, such as v5.104.2, and let us know whether the issue still reproduces, appreciate it.

body:
I investigated the behavior across newer Webpack versions starting from v5.104.1, where nondeterministic contenthash changes were reproducible.

On v5.104.1, the hashes frequently diverged when using thread-loader.
Adding CircularDependencyPlugin increased the divergence, although no circular dependencies were reported.

I also tested several additional factors:
- Disabled Terser parallelization (parallel: false)
- Changed the hashFunction algorithm
- Compared stats outputs between builds
- Verified file contents by removing [contenthash] from filenames

None of these changes resolved the issue on v5.104.1 ŌĆö hashes still diverged while emitted file contents remained identical.

### Testing v5.105.0

Before testing, I performed a clean setup:
- Deleted node_modules
- Deleted package-lock.json
- Cleared npm cache (npm cache clean --force)
- Reinstalled dependencies

Using the same configurations as before:

Initially observed a small number of divergences between builds 391ŌåÆ400
(possibly due to filesystem artifacts, dirty output, or residual cache)
After rebuilding and repeating tests, no further divergences occurred
Subsequent runs remained stable across all configurations

### Testing v5.105.4 (latest)

Similar behavior was observed:
- One divergence occurred during the first runs
- After rebuilding and retesting, hashes remained stable
- No reproducible nondeterministic behavior was observed

body:
Hi, IŌĆÖve been going through this issue and the recent discussion.

From what I understand, the nondeterministic contenthash behavior seems to have improved in newer Webpack versions (>=5.105.x), possibly related to fixes around chunkId determinism and circular dependencies.

Would it be helpful if I:
- Try reproducing this on older versions (e.g., 5.104.1 vs 5.105.x)
- Compare build outputs and stats
- Create a simplified reproduction case

Please let me know how I can best assist here.
author: pritam-31

body:
@hai-x I tested across versions as requested.

Deterministic ID strategies (based on stable module identifiers) alone are not sufficient to guarantee stable contenthash.

In earlier versions, module and chunk ordering depended on graph traversal, which was influenced by non-deterministic root selection in graphs without a clear root (e.g., cyclic/complex graphs). Since findGraphRoots relied on edge (insertion) ordering, traversal order could vary across builds.

The fix in PR #20452 (1313375f9) ensures deterministic root selection, which stabilizes traversal order. This results in consistent module and chunk ordering, and therefore stable contenthash.

This behavior reproduces in v5.104.1 and no longer reproduces in v5.105.2+.

Would appreciate confirmation that this matches the intended fix.
author: Karan09542

body:
Not sure if this is the same issue.  But I ran into ECONNREFUSED error trying to proxy normal http requests to localhost.  If I downgrade to node 16.20.1 then the proxying works again with localhost.

I did find out that starting with [node 17+, node prefers IPV6 over IPV4.](https://github.com/chimurai/http-proxy-middleware#nodejs-17-econnrefused-issue-with-ipv6-and-localhost-705)  So DNS resolution for localhost isn't guaranteed to be 127.0.0.1, it could be ::1.  When I used the ip address proxying worked again using node 17+.
author: humblepie

body:
> Not sure if this is the same issue. But I ran into ECONNREFUSED error trying to proxy normal http requests to localhost. If I downgrade to node 16.20.1 then the proxying works again with localhost.
> 
> I did find out that starting with [node 17+, node prefers IPV6 over IPV4.](https://github.com/chimurai/http-proxy-middleware#nodejs-17-econnrefused-issue-with-ipv6-and-localhost-705) So DNS resolution for localhost isn't guaranteed to be 127.0.0.1, it could be ::1. When I used the ip address proxying worked again using node 17+.

It is not the same issue. Our "target" is not a localhost, is a remote backend with https (TLS 1.2).
Using a backend with TLS 1.3 works well.

body:
There are no problems with webpack itself, you ask typescript to use `require`, but try to load ESM module, please use:
```
{
    "compilerOptions": {
        "noImplicitAny": true,
        "target": "ES5",
        "moduleResolution": "bundler",
        "module": "esnext",
        "allowSyntheticDefaultImports": true
    }
}
```

and ts-loader will generate `import ... from ...`.

Yeah, if you have commonjs project, you can't use `import wrapAnsi from 'wrap-ansi';`, you need to use dynamic `import("wrap-ansi")`, we can't load ESM module using `require()`. There's nothing we can do with it. 

Feel free to feedback

body:
I have found the solution to my problem. Maybe this helps:
The compiled Typescript code was running on the client-side/browser. Since my webpack.config.ts file was configured to compile this code for the server side, require() (which does not exist in the browser) was used during compilation.
I changed my config.ts to compile client code files as `target: 'web',` and server code as` target: 'node'`
author: Pixelherz23

body: You can't use Node.js modules in a browsers, `whatwg-fetch` uses dynamic `require(...)` so webpack keeps this as is in a code, but this code is not design to use in a browser so you got this problem, maybe you use an old version of `whatwg-fetch` or also check you have `target: 'web'`, feel free to feedback

body:
We have an API for such purposes and other tools use it (not only webpack, other bundlers too) - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/postcss-loader/blob/master/src/index.js#L191

`@fullhuman/postcss-purgecss` should use postcss message API, i.e. run this code:
```js
result.messages.push(
  {
    type: "dependency",
    file: "/absolute/path/to/file.php",
    plugin: "@fullhuman/postcss-purgecss",
  }
)
```

Webpack knows nothing about PHP files, also just watching PHP files and rerun compilation will very ineffective, using such API (example above) webpack rebuild only modules which depend on `/absolute/path/to/file.php` (i.e. in module where you imported CSS file where class was generated), so you will have fast rebuild.

body:
I want to say it is not a bug, just change `externalsType: 'module'` to `externalsType: "import",`.

I think we should add a new value for `externalsType` - `import-module`/`module-import` (maybe the best name) and set it by default for ES modules, i.e. keep `import(...)` and `import ... from "module";` for externals how they were written
author: alexander-akait

body: Also you can specify the type for an each item using https://webpack.js.org/configuration/externals/#string
author: alexander-akait

body:
Thank you @alexander-akait , that actually solves the hoisting, but it brings that hoisting still back to the main bundle into a function. For example, `index.ts` dynamically imports `lazy.ts`, and `lazy.ts` dynamically imports an external named `external.ts`. The definition of `externals` is hoisted to the main bundle `index.js`, instead it should have been inside `external.js`

I don't think we need another `externalsType`, why would someone who specifies `module` would want the imports hoisted for dynamic imports? That feels wrong. If they want it to be hoisted, then define them as `import "foo"` instead of `import("foo")`. It feels more like a bug. By definition in webpack docs: ` webpack will output ECMAScript module syntax whenever possible`

My recommendation would be to keep `externalsType` as `module` and don't transform it, just keep it in its place.
author: mohamedmansour

body: @mohamedmansour No, it is not a bug, I can't find a discussion about it, but there was already a question about it here, there are scenarios when developers want to hoist (preload big modules in the first run), some want to load dynamically (lazy evaluation), especial in diffucult applications (you can improve perf in some cases, for example some modules can take a lot of time on import but you used them in rare cases and you can't change vendor library code), that is why I said, we need the another value to allow to keep dynamic import as dynamic and static as static. The same logic works for other values of the `externalsType` option.
author: alexander-akait
mohamedmansour

body: As I said above we just need a new value -  `import-module`, we can't keep them as is when you have `import` or `module`, because it will be breaking change and can break a lot of configurations

body: Honestly there are no problems here, you set `"type": "module"`, it means **all** files are ESM, but `banner.js` is using `require` (you know that `require` does not exist in ESM), just rename `banner.js` to `banner.cjs` (or alternative solution is putting `package.json` with `type: "commonjs"` inside `src`). This is how Node.js and ESM logic works. I don't think we should change anything otherwise we won't follow and align ESM logic, also it will require introducing global variables and other things.

body:
I found that the issue has always existed since version [5.41.0](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/releases/tag/v5.41.0) which add EcmaScript modules support.

I'm willing to fix this issue, and I've spent some time looking at it.

The problem I found is that when `renderStartup` in ModuleLibraryPlugin `moduleGraph.getExportsInfo(module).exports` equals to [] which caused by `optimization.providedExports: false`.
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/6842c9a2c9b70e760143188ffa5baa19a72799cb/lib/library/ModuleLibraryPlugin.js#L80


I have a solution that when `library === module` and  `providedExport === false` we also flag exportsInfo by `FlagDependencyExportsPlugin` but only for the entry module. Not sure if this is the optimal solution, but I tested it and it is indeed feasible. 

But I also feel that doing so violates the meaning of `providedExport`. I will send PR to fix this issue.

body:
I found something suspicious which might allow an easy fix: 

1. Various top level symbols are marked by the InnerGraphPlugin: 
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/optimize/InnerGraphPlugin.js#L133
2. The `InnerGraph.tagTopLevelSymbol` first defines a variable in the parser with the name and then additionally tags the variable with the top level symbol. 
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/optimize/InnerGraph.js#L269-L279
3. The problem: 
    1. `parser.defineVariable` [adds a variable](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L4385-L4391) with the current scope as value. 
    2. `parser.tagVariable` [also defines a variable](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L4356-L4380) but with the correct top level symbol as value. But as there is already a VariableInfo registered from `defineVariable` [resulting in the name not to be set](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/611bded369b5a9ea695f2669b25a6f88b67d7826/lib/javascript/JavascriptParser.js#L4373) 

I see two potential fixes in this code path: 

1. In `InnerGraph.tagTopLevelSymbol` we remove the call to `parser.defineVariable`. (or move it into the if-statement when a tag already exists). 
   * Feels like the safer option as the change is quite isolated and just ensures we do not double-define the variable with missing info. 
3. In `JavaScriptParser.tagVariable` we fill the name we know instead of `true` into the VariableInfo. 
   * It seems the old name was used before and things were refactored as part of https://github.com/webpack/webpack/commit/ec518945f18618d4c3b4992f129442e73dd331da I'm not so sure about the impact of this change.

body:
Hi maintainers,

I'm K Anjith Goud, a 2nd-year Information Technology student from India. I'm exploring contributions for GSoC 2025 and IŌĆÖm particularly interested in Webpack due to my strong background in JavaScript, Node.js, and web development.

While going through open issues, I found #18113 (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/18113) very interesting. I've successfully reproduced the issue using the sample repo provided, and I'm diving into the internals of `JavascriptParser.js` to better understand the behavior.

From what IŌĆÖve observed, the root cause seems to be that `getFreeInfoFromVariable` returns `true` instead of the class name string when the class is defined in the same file. As a result, `getMemberExpressionInfo` fails to return the necessary data, which stops `callHooksForExpression` from executing the hook.

A possible fix could be updating the logic inside `getFreeInfoFromVariable` to identify local class declarations and return their name instead of `true`, or alternatively, modifying `callHooksForExpression` to handle `true` as a valid identifier in some special cases.

IŌĆÖd love to hear your thoughts on whether this direction sounds correct or if youŌĆÖd suggest a different approach. IŌĆÖm excited to dive deeper and contribute meaningfully to the project.

Best regards,  
K ANJITH GOUD
author: anjith1

body:
Based on the [JavascriptParser.unittest.js#L115](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/test/JavascriptParser.unittest.js#L115) here, this seems to be the expected behavior. As long as the variable is declared within the current scope (excluding undeclared globals variables or imported variables), its hooks.call won't be triggered, The behavior of hooks.new is consistent with this.

So hooks.new and hooks.call are currently only used internally by Webpack. When used directly by users, they are limited to scenarios involving undeclared global variables or imported variables.

cc @alexander-akait @hai-x
author: xiaoxiaojx

body: Users have to tag the variable `tagVariable()` at preDeclarator hook (or any hook before innerGraph call `defineVariable()` in `tagTopLevelSymbol()`, yes the order matters here) if they want call hooks for the unfree/declared variable, an example is [WorkerPlugin](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/feaa9339966416effed2ebb75a8a8ac206138018/lib/dependencies/WorkerPlugin.js#L492-L503)

body:
Thank you for the quick response!

Options 1 and 2 seem like the safest immediate fixes with minimal impact, though we acknowledge they might not cover all future scenarios.

Option 3 (and its browser-compatible variant) do offer a more universal fix. It does seem like the most ideal solution to our specific problem. However, our main concern revolves around potential side-effects across other `getValueType` usages. It would be great to know more about the specific operations `removeOperations` aims to exclude from your perspective. That would help us to gauge this option's viability better. That being said, we only pass in our custom cache object in the development environment, but browser compatibility is important to us.

body:
Also stumbled into this issue whilst upgrading a project to Webpack 5.

I've noticed it doesn't seem to mangle _all_ of the properties in the imported JSON, just the ones referenced wherever it has been imported. For example...

Source file:

```ts
import graphqlSpec from './graphqlSpec.json'; // {"data":{"schema":{"types":[...

const { types: schemaTypes } = graphqlSpec.data.schema;

schemaTypes.reduce(...
```

Production build output:

```js
const i = JSON.parse('{"p":{"w":{"gK":[...etc'); // Objects within gK are **not** mangled

const { types: w } = i.p.w;

// Reading w fails because types has been mangled to gK
w.reduce(...
```

author: pgoldberg

body:
Create a Webpack configuration with the usedExports: "global" option set.
Import a CommonJS module that has a self-reference or circular dependency inside a Web Worker.
Webpack will throw the error: Self-reference dependency has unused export name: This should not happen.
Expected Behavior
Webpack should handle self-referencing or circular dependencies correctly without throwing an error, even with usedExports: "global" set.
The usedExports: "global" optimization should work without causing issues in such cases.
Potential Workaround
Since this issue might be related to the specific optimizations when usedExports is set to "global", you could try the following:

Use a different optimization setting: Instead of "global", try using "false" or "true" for usedExports, as this could bypass the issue by not triggering the optimization that causes the error.

optimization: {
  usedExports: "false", // or "true"
}

Ensure the module structure is not self-referencing: If possible, refactor the CommonJS module to avoid circular dependencies or self-referencing exports, though this might not always be feasible depending on your codebase.

Use a different bundling strategy: Depending on your setup, consider testing alternative bundlers or adjusting your worker-related configurations to avoid relying on usedExports: "global" if that is causing the issue.

Update Webpack or Patch: Since the issue is potentially related to a Webpack bug (#12646, #12648), check if there are any newer Webpack versions or patches that address this problem. If not, you might need to apply a custom fix or rollback to a version where the issue isn't present.

Additional Resources
Review the issues on Webpack's GitHub related to circular dependencies and self-referencing modules.
Check Webpack's documentation for any updates on usedExports optimization and its behavior with CommonJS and web workers.
If you're able to isolate the issue in a minimal example (as you did in your repo), it can be helpful to provide it to the Webpack team for further investigation.

/cc @vankop I think we need to start support `import.meta.dirname` and `import.meta.filename`, there are two improvements:
1. Use `import.meta.dirname` and `import.meta.filename` instead `__webpack_fileURLToPath__(import.meta.url + "/..")` if `target`/`browserslist` support it (if it is node target or node like)
2. Don't output `undefined` for `import.meta.dirname` and `import.meta.filename`, i.e. output them as is if they supported or replace on our shim with `__webpack_fileURLToPath__(import.meta.url + "/..")` (I think we need options here i.e. `node.importMetaDirname` and `node.importMetaFilename`)

body:
Hi @alexander-akait, we recently ran into an issue with `import.meta.dirname` being undefined, and I'm interested in contributing some of the changes proposed in this thread ­¤śī 

I glanced through the relevant codepaths (`lib/config/defaults.js`, `lib/dependencies/ImportMetaPlugin.js`, `lib/NodeStuffPlugin.js` and the test setup), and I have a sketch for how to implement this.

However, before I go down that path, I want to recap my understanding of the specification ­¤ōØ  As you mentioned earlier, we will need options for `node.importMetaDirname` and `node.importMetaFilename`.

**These options seem applicable for the first phase:**
  - `'none'` or `'unsupported'`: the current default in v5. Emits these properties as undefined, with a webpack comment.
  - `false` or `'node-module'`: Webpack won't touch the `import.meta.filename`/`import.meta.dirname` code, leaving the default Node.js behavior. The dirname of the **output** file when run in a Node.js environment.
    - If the browserslist targets do not support these properties, we use an `import.meta.url` shim.
    - **Question**: Should this be the future default, when `output.module` is enabled? Can be opted-in manually, or with `futureDefaults`.

**This could be in a second phase:**
  - `true` or `'resolved-in-context'`: The dirname/filename of the input file relative to the [context option](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#context).
    - This is symmetric with the `__filename` and `__dirname` `true` option, because I can imagine people wanting to resolve based on the input files.
    - **Question**: Should this support both CJS and ESM output?

**And a third phase, if there is a need:**
- `node-cjs`: Replace `import.meta.filename` / `import.meta.dirname` in ESM modules with `__filename` / `__dirname` equivalents when emitting CJS.
  - This would be the inverse of the `node.__filename` / `node.__dirname` `node-module` option. I have not seen much need for this at the moment, but let me know if it makes sense.

I wrote the names to be analogous to [node.__dirname](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/node/#node__filename) and [node.__filename](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/node/#node__dirname), but I personally find the `true` and `false` values confusing. So I wrote some suggestions about stringy values, but we can bikeshed that if you prefer ­¤śī 

**How does the above sound to you? Would it be ok if I pick up the first phase of this work?** I'd like to avoid duplicated effort, so I thought it best to let you know.

--- 

Aside: While implementing `import.meta.filename` / `import.meta.dirname` for ESM would help remove the shim and thus fix the original issue in this thread, I think the fix itself could be done in a follow-up.
author: fpapado
fpapado

author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait when `realContentHash` is enabled can `webpack` instead use a non-truncated `contenthash` during its first pass (making collision near impossible) and then only truncate (if requested) during the replacement phase with the calculated real content hash?

edit: Or maybe you could also include a needle like `_WEBPACK_CONTENTHASH_{value}_`  pre-replacement?
author: issacgerges

body:
> Or maybe you could also include a needle like _WEBPACK_CONTENTHASH_{value}_ pre-replacement?

Yes, it is our idea to solve this problem in webpack 6, but it requires change logic in many plugins (and in vendor plugins), that is why we can't use this solution right now, but we still thinking how we can solve it more better. 

> in vendor plugins

body:
We are developing a server architecture where you have a "core bundle" (where the actual entry point of the application is) and several "client" ones, and we want to be able to split this code so that we can deploy the application with zero, one or just a few client bundles instead of all of them.

The rationale is twofold:
1) since we can have different on-premise deployments at different clients, and we do not wish to expose client-specific business logic to someone who isn't the intended user;
2) when updating our application deployments without altering the underlying node_modules, we would like to install only the core or client bundle(s) that actually got modified instead of the entirety of the codebase reduced to a single, monolithic bundle.
author: Arandhras

body:

I went ahead and pushed a slightly cleaned-up version of my original workaround fix to the PR for now, but I think I like the idea of having an override for `this.binary` on a module rule a bit more. I do think that if we go that route, there should be some documentation somewhere about the fact that Asset Modules will **not** receive source maps unless the `binary: false` override is used.

author: alexander-akait

body:
> > I was planning on submitting a PR to sass-loader if this got accepted so that they can include a sourceMapUrl on the result.
> 
> I think better allow to setup `sourceMap: boolean | "from-options"` (we don't set any custom source maps options and respect your) and also we skip this - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/sass-loader/blob/master/src/index.js#L107, so you can flexible setup source maps options

Actually the main problem that I identified was not an issue with the `sourceMap` option itself, but rather the fact that the custom SCSS importer used by `sass-loader` does not include `sourceMapUrl` in its importer result:
https://github.com/webpack-contrib/sass-loader/blob/master/src/utils.js#L694

This causes source mappings for imported SCSS files to be completely garbled in the browser because it tries to use the actual contents of the file as the source URL. Adding `sourceMapUrl: canonicalUrl` fixes the issue, but I'm not sure if that's the correct fix as it would result in absolute URLs being returned for imported files.

body:
You can disable `optimization.providedExports` in development, webpack enabled it by default in development, the only case I encountered where enabling `providedExports` in development mode was effective is `re-exports + cycle reference`, `SideEffectsFlagPlugin` will use provided exports to skip the re-exports module, which avoid the error caused by cycle reference in re-exports module, but in actual usage scenarios, this situation is very rare. (I'm not sure whether there are other reasons for enabling providedExports in development by default)

In your provided reproduce, with `optimization.providedExports = false`, ~2s -> ~70ms for every rebuild
author: ahabhgk

body: Maybe good place to look how we can speedup it (cache? better logic? etc)
author: alexander-akait

body:
We experimented with disabling `optimization.providedExports`, and while this did make the development build faster, it also silenced errors related to incorrectly named exports. For example, changing a named export while in watch mode would normally show a build error if not all imports were updated with the new name, but after removing the `providedExports` optimization this was no longer the case. I'm not sure if this is a bug or expected behaviour.

At the moment our temporary solution has been to use a slightly updated version of the [old json-loader](https://github.com/webpack-contrib/json-loader) instead of using Webpack's built-in JSON generation, but it'd be great to remove this shim and get a fix in upstream.

I'm happy spending some more time on this writing a max depth PR and potentially looking at performance optimizations when I get a chance!

Other concerns:
1) If the module object is ever handed to a function (or the other similar bailouts that webpack uses for export mangling), then we have to assume live bindings for everything, and also that export order may matter
1) When the value being exported is a `const`, `let`, or `var` declaration, rather than a `function` or `class`, assigning the value has to happen at the *end* of the module instead of being hoisted.

body:
Some note: 
1.  We register export definitions early before `evaluation` because in circular reference, another module may access this moduleŌĆÖs namespace while this module is still evaluating.
2. We register export definitions by `getter` is to keep live binding behavior and to also avoid the DCE issue brought by `1`.
3. Ideally we can optimize the `export const` case when no circular module reference founded. Related implementations in other bundlers: https://github.com/web-infra-dev/rspack/pull/14045, https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/82760.

body:
If you are using Webpack5 + Vue2 to build an SSR framework, `vue-server-renderer` will have related problems when using Vue2 under Webpack5. The `server-bundle.js` packaged by webpack5 will have this statement below. (see [url-assets](https://webpack.js.org/guides/asset-modules/#url-assets))
```js
__webpack_require__.b = require('url').pathToFileURL(__filename)
```
but `vue-server-renderer` [wraps a layer of function](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/blob/9e88707940088cb1f4cd7dd210c9168a50dc347c/packages/server-renderer/src/bundle-renderer/create-bundle-runner.ts#L67C5-L67C20) and provides empty `__dirname` and `__filename`. [This PR](https://github.com/vuejs/vue/pull/12242) provides a solution but has not been officially merged by Vue so far.

body:
Share the thing they both need as a singleton. Sharing exposed modules as singletons won't work since an exposed module is a singleton, but the sub-dependencies in there are not, unless you mark them as such.

If you have a host where its own remote entry is consumed as well, then that's your actual issue. There are two module graphs on the page, and the instance in the host is a different runtime and module cache than the remote entry. In such a case, you need a single runtime chunk or can sometimes work around it by sharing and not exposing. But the reason it "works" is that the remote doesn't actually load a copy and the duplicate runtime happens to use one from the other. 

While the workaround works. If you do have host uses its own remote somehow in the tree. Then chunk loading will eventually break etc

body:
The problem is that you have two runtime chunks. We cannot auto-fix that. (It should be a) single runtime chunk for the host to load its own remote. Tomorrow I will see if I can ship a slight update to how it works. I have something to handle making both single runtime and standalone runtime. Then you use the runtime plugin to switch between which remote URL to use depending on if the remote is also the host or not.

Single runtime means the remote won't work off the host. Or without the host's parent runtime.
If your host has other dynamic imports that load after its remote, you will get chunk load timeout or failed to load errors from webpack. Since the remote will overwrite the hosts chunk loading global.

body:
@tomprince @alexander-akait 

I've noticed an issue in `webpack/lib/webworker/ImportScriptsChunkLoadingPlugin.js` where the `asyncChunkLoading` flag is set to `true`, causing unnecessary asynchronous chunk loading in worker entrypoints. This setting conflicts with scenarios like creating a custom worker for Monaco editor, where `importScripts` is used synchronously. Specifically, when `importScripts` is used to load a webpack bundle, the expected synchronous behavior is not maintained, leading to errors unless `asyncChunkLoading` is set to `false`.

I have experience in debugging and enhancing JavaScript build tools, and I'm confident that I can address this issue effectively by refining the chunk loading process to respect the synchronous nature of `importScripts`.

I would like to take this token. Can you please assign this to me?

Best,
Swayam.
author: Swayam-code

body: @Swayam-code Can you provide a real reproducible example?
author: alexander-akait

body:
This is expected, because removing dead code is on `terser` side (set `optimization.minimize` to false to see the code what webpack generates), so yes, chunk will be outputed, dynamic imports are not analizable like `import ... from ...` and we know nothing about your code execution (and logic when something will be called). Technically this can be solved, but we will need a second time to build the graph, which will be extremely bad for performance. Also the main generated code doesn't containt any extra code for the lazy chunk, so it seems to me that this logic is the best solution. 

Feel free to feedback

With `usedExports` on, Webpack is clearly aware that `getAsyncChunk` is unused, as it adds

body:
Expected, you have multile runtimes on the same page, but the one HMR updating function, your patch doesn't fix the problem really, just workaround for your case (and will break HMR for another cases, when you split your chunks), please use `optimization: { runtimeChunk: 'single' }`. When you have multiple chunks with own runtime on the same page, you always need to use `runtimeChunk: 'single'` for HMR.

Feel free to feedback
author: alexander-akait

body:
Hey @alexander-akait , in our case the problem is that `optimization: { runtimeChunk: 'single' }` doesn't fit the needs, so that's why I went for that approach. The idea is not that is mandatory or anything, is simply to have the ability to add something in case you have multiple runtimes in the same page (which can be very valid specifically in micro frontend architectures), and in that case having the ability to differentiate the runtimes.

For normal cases it would simply work as it has been working. The patch of course is just a workaround to validate that was the problem, but the idea is being able to maybe provide that way of differentiating the runtimes (even if they are in the same page), that's why I was proposing the usage of `[name]` or `[id]` as options that could be provided in `output.hotUpdateGlobal` or  maybe allowing the usage of a function in `output.hotUpdateGlobal` that can receive the chunk.

Maybe it was wrong to open a bug because this might be more like asking for a feature instead?

Let me know what you think, thanks!
author: MarioCadenas

body: Using chunks ids is a bad idea and wrong here, not each chunk has runtime,  technical we can allow to setup `hotUpdateGlobal: "webpackHotUpdate[unique-name][runtime]"` and get the `runtime` value from https://webpack.js.org/concepts/entry-points/#entrydescription-object

body: /cc @fi3ework we have a perf regression here due - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18348, any ideas how we can skip it, especial for development mode
author: alexander-akait

body: I think we need to move this logic - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/18348/files#diff-820a04017e95cec05fd58ca44cd7f6c9cfa5b698a144471d5a0f523932486c56R1403 to `ConcatenatedModule`, so we will run it only when necessary...
author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait The expensive part is re-parsing the AST of modules, and it's hard to eliminate as we can't get the info from the previous steps, it also caused a regression in Rspack, but because JS is not multi-threaded this problem is more serious.

What about adding an option to control this behavior, like `optimization.entryIife` with options:

- `true`: default, keep the way before #18348.
- `"development"`: keep IIFE only in development mode.
- `"production"`: keep IIFE only in production mode.
- `false`: keep the way after #18348.

This feature makes build time and production run speed trade-offs, for most cases, maybe we could choose build time as the default.

I could adding the new option if it's good to go or any other suggestion is welcomed!
author: fi3ework

body: @fi3ework What's about to do it when only `concatenateModules: true`? So `development` will not be affacted. We can make `entryIife` for developers which want to disable it and prefer speed time, because if I undestand correctly this code makes nonthing when you have `concatenateModules: false`?
author: alexander-akait

body:
> because if I undestand correctly this code makes nonthing when you have concatenateModules: false?

No matter with or without `concatenateModules` enabled, this optimization will take effect. When `concatenateModules` is enabled, the entry module is usually a large concatenated module in most cases, so the performance impact will be significant, while without enabling it, the module is usually of normal size.
author: fi3ework

That's expected, try to run - `node -e 'import(`#site/../test/api/index.js`);'` (instead `test` you can use any value) without webpack and you will got the same problem, you will have this problem using other bundlers too and just node.js/bun/deno, we fixed it a long time ago, but looks like you don't update your deps before. 

Shortly - you can use `..` before `#`, it means leave context, you need to specify more deep dynamic imports if you want to bundle them. But I don't fully undestand why you just don't use `webpackIgnore` magic comments, because you don't need to bundle them, they are external for you application and cooking on `npm run configure`, your just lose build performance and increase bundle size.

I don't think we can fix this because we want to be compatible with Node.js resolution logic.

Anyway feel free to feedback.

body:
Also seeing this error when using `skipLibCheck: false`

Note: I initially had more Webpack errors, but they were solved by upgrading `@types/node` to the latest `22.5.4`. But this specific error remains after the upgrade.

Note the new `strictBuiltinIteratorReturn: false` option does not fix it 
ref: https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-5-6/#strict-builtin-iterator-checks-(and---strictbuiltiniteratorreturn)3


---

Source code: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/util/SortableSet.js

I'm not sure why the types have this:

```ts
	/**
	 * Iterates over values in the set.
	 */
	[Symbol.iterator](): IterableIterator<T>;
```

The implementation does not create a custom iterator so IMHO this declaration could be removed to use the built-in Set iterator? (which now has new [iterator helper methods](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-5-6/#iterator-helper-methods2))

I tried removing this custom iterator locally and the error disappeared, without introducing new errors in Webpack types.

body:
FYI:
Workaround in our case is to `build` web workers with webpack, each for one [name].mjs file 
Then use this built workers in app. (e.g. like assets with relative path instead of URL object)

cons: 
- increased worker size
- needs to rebuilt and reserve, if any changes in worker

pros: 
- it works

author: einarq

body:
Unknown. I want to reduce the number of patches I applied to webpack via my plugin.
Syncing with webpack is not a priority due to output velocity requirements at bytedance. But in the process of building v2, i did patch many problems in webpack, i want to move those out of my repo. 

I want to maintain central control over federation, since I do not have merge and release powers over webpack - I will not move anything into this repo that may hinder iteration speeds. Either way, webpack will have to add my packages as a dependency, it could re-export my classes if it wanted to. 

My runtime is released in tandem with my plugins during updates. You can wait to see what happens, but it will take you a few hours to migrate, or you can wait 6-12 months for something that might not happen. 

Either way, you'll still be installing module-federation/enhanced. 

I need to be able to release new versions of the system within an SLA of 15 minutes; getting blocked by release cycles is a non-negotiable. 

The challenge we have seen is Bytedance engineering moves faster than any community based project. Federation is a core technology that powers most of the thousands of apps we own. 

Backmerging parts is a spare time effort of which only i do. 

Just migrate.

body: Reference hoisting is also used in v2 as a way to hoist the injected npm packages that constitue the new module federation runtime. Since splitChunks will move our node modules out of eager consumption for the remote or host. I use `ModuleFederationPlugin.getcompilationhooks().addFederationRuntimeDependency(dep)` - which Hoist plugin will track all eager referenced modules and move them back into runtime chunk as well. Ensuing federation v2 runtime is eager and can be __webpack_require__ inside a RuntimeModule

body:
These changes make an asynchronous startup. So main.js exports a promise that resolves the inner require. Entry acts the same as if it were import().

I changed from passive mode to active. Which usually is for ESM async entry. So I use startup entrypoint runtime requirement.

That's require.x for its chunks and split chunks. Then the code I add to startup helpers checks if the chunk has federation-entry-startup or something as a runtime requirement. If so, I think I call the remotes and consumes handlers myself for the entry chunk ID, like main.

Require.x will call the handler for all else like dependOn, etc. But there needs to be an extra wrap to also ensure the main chunk.

author: gergokee

body:
I found the root of the problem and it is inside `nextjs` - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/FlagIncludedChunksPlugin.js#L50

`compilation.modules` is not deterministic and always has a different order of `modules` and due this `moduleBits` and `hash` are always different - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/FlagIncludedChunksPlugin.js#L65 and when comparing hashes - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/optimize/FlagIncludedChunksPlugin.js#L109 in some situations this condition is `true` or `false` - so the order is random. 

This can happen due to changing the order of modules in some async hooks. In pure webpack this does not happen because we do not do such things, but apparently in some `nextjs` plugin this happens. Unfortunately, debugging in which requires deeper knowledge in `nextjs`, this takes me much more time, I have already spent about 5-6 hours to find where the problem is and why. Anyway I intend to fix this because we have async hooks before this, which could potentially happen in other unofficial plugins too.

Although in any case I would advise to fix it on the `nextjs` side too, because it can lead to other undesirable consequences.

Unfortunately this is not possible, this option is only for calculation during bundling time (i.e. it can be string or function but only in bundling time) and used to find relative paths and for dev server to understand which URLs need to be handled, webpack is not only about JS, Also we support and CSS and HTML and etc there it is impossible.

This will also most likely break other plugins that expect this to be a string, especially those aimed at runtime changes.

@Jack-Works

The main problem is that our API is used by other plugins too, 

so maybe we need `${RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath}(${RuntimeGlobals.publicPath} + ${myCustomAsset});`

author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait I would like to create a PR. But I have two solutions for this. I can't decide which is better.
1. add a new option called dynamicPublicPath. 
    * Pros
       it runs only at runtime. it will not affect the publicPath option.
    * Cons
       the number of options has increased. 
2. change the internal logic of publicPath.
    * Pros
       No new options are added.
    * Cons
     calculation at runtime instead of  bundling time. But for functions that don't want to return the difference value for each run,  it may also affect the performance.

author: Jack-Works

body:
@sapenlei @Jack-Works My idead
- Add `__webpack_dynamic_public_path__` like https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/APIPlugin.js#L37
- Now we have `RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath` 
- If `RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath` is existing in `runtimeRequirements` (https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/asset/AssetGenerator.js#L431C4-L431C23) we generate something like:

```js
assetPath = runtimeRequirements.has(RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath) 
  ? runtimeTemplate.generateFunction(RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath, runtimeTemplate.concatenation({ expr: RuntimeGlobals.publicPath }, filename)))
  : runtimeTemplate.concatenation({ expr: RuntimeGlobals.publicPath }, filename));
```  
 
 This is a rough pseudo code to show the idea, maybe we can even improve the logic and implement `runtimeTemplate.generatePublicPath(filename)` which will encapsulate this logic in itself, and in the future will allow you to reuse it and add more logic if necessary
 
 This approach will allow you to avoid new options, and plugins can simply use `runtimeRequirements.add(RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath)`, devs will be able to override this using `__webpack_dynamic_public_path__`
author: alexander-akait

body:
> ```js
> assetPath = runtimeRequirements.has(RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath) 
>   ? runtimeTemplate.generateFunction(RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath, runtimeTemplate.concatenation({ expr: RuntimeGlobals.publicPath }, filename)))
>   : runtimeTemplate.concatenation({ expr: RuntimeGlobals.publicPath }, filename));
> ```
I have a question. What's the way for a plugin to access the `runtimeRequirements` here and add `RuntimeGlobals.dynamicPublicPath`? I've looked at the existing hooks, and their trigger time seems to be later than AssetGenerator. Maybe I'm missing something.
author: inottn

body: Since the hooks you're observing are triggered after the AssetGenerator, we need a way to intervene earlier in the build process. I think we can use Compilation.hooks to access and modify runtime requirements. The compilation object allow us to manipulate runtime requirements.

body:
Hi @cdoublev and Webpack team,

I'm new to open-source contributions and came across this issue. I was able to reproduce the bug as described, and hereŌĆÖs a summary of my findings:

Issue Summary: Webpack was merging the `pages/home.js` module with other chunks instead of creating a separate async chunk, resulting in unexpected behavior during the project build.

Steps Taken:
1) I modified webpack.config.js by setting `mode` to `production` and updating the `splitChunks` settings to adjust chunking thresholds.
2) After rebuilding, pages/home.js was successfully generated as a separate async chunk, which resolved the issue.

Suggested Solution: To achieve the expected behavior, the following `splitChunks` configuration worked:
`splitChunks: {
    chunks: 'all',
    minSize: 1000,
    maxSize: 20000,
}`

Let me know if thereŌĆÖs any additional information or further testing I can assist with. IŌĆÖd be happy to help!

`

body:
> Yeah, let's fix and use `__webpack_id__` and `__webpack_ids__`

this is simple to change but it would definitely break chunk consumption across versions

questions,

am i correct in understanding that, chunk formats are/should be capable of consuming each other? is this related to the `__esModule` name i see in some places?

presently the names may be created even when a chunk is not consumed by another chunk or a runtime. such as when a file

1. represents a 'side-effectful import' that defines no exports, and runs immediately at the top level of the file
2. is an application entry file
3. is a library entry file for use by non-webpack importers

and the slightly different case that made me notice this,

4. is a library entry file that is also imported by another chunk

it seems like some other chunk formats only emit things conditionally, but i don't understand all the requirements. are there any docs on this?
author: turbocrime

body:
> this is simple to change but it would definitely break chunk consumption across versions

yes, because es modules are all experimental and we recommend using them `[cotententhash]` (for applications), it should not be a problem to change.

> am i correct in understanding that, chunk formats are/should be capable of consuming each other? is this related to the __esModule name i see in some places?

technically yes, chunks in ESM format are able to consume each other of different builds (within one version of webpack, which is guaranteed, it will work for different versions in most cases, but we can change the runtime logic and it may break, but this happens extremely rarely), the minimizer will delete these things if they were not used, but to be honest I don't think it is used by many and I don't think we should really support this

We should add more tests for such cases...

In any case, use the prefix `__webpack_id__` and `__webpack_ids__` is worth using because technically we avoid IIFE using scope analyzer and try to concatenate modules, but it analyzes only your code and doesn't analyze runtime generated code (we assume that it will never have collisions) and if you have `id` variable in entry modules, we will have collision.

So I would say there are two problems here:
- collisions, let's fix it using `webpack` prefix, because this may happen not only for the initial chunk
- avoid generation `id` and `ids` for initial chunks

Also we have `type: "modern-module"` - https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#type-modern-module

this looks related, but it seems to only be for `AssignLibraryPlugin` which is not used by `library: { type: "module" }` among a few others

maybe collision avoidance should be a generalized tool useable by all library plugins, or a dedicated formatting step, or provided by `AbstractLibraryPlugin` or a new abstract chunk formatting base class

> Emitted library entry files should not export names unless requested by the developer

This is a library if you wrote top level export it will be exported.

This is not a webpack issue and unfortunately we can't fix it here. This applies to how we work with CSS (css -> js -> extract CSS) and to `MiniCssExtractPlugin`.
 
 Bare JS imports are considered side free by default and can therefore be moved to any location in the file. 
 
 It should be fixed in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/14893 (I will add test). We can also fix this here - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/mini-css-extract-plugin.

body:
@alexander-akait I did a quick test (on the rspack branch of the reproduction)

I added a simple side effect:

`@libraries/teaser/src/side-effect.ts`
```diff
@@ -0,0 +1 @@
+console.log("hello world")
```

`@libraries/teaser/src/index.ts`
```diff
@@ -1 +1,2 @@
 export * from './teaser';
+export * from "./side-effect"
```

then I compiled it twice - onece with package.json `"sideEffects": true` and once with `"sideEffects": false`

for the javascript part both bundlers **rspack and webpack worked the same way**: `console.log("hello world")` was only part of the bundle for `"sideEffects": true`
author: jantimon

body:
@jantimon I looked deeper into your example and I want to say that you have the wrong `sideEffects` settings. You can't have `sideEffects: false` in packages that import `CSS` and **inject** it into DOM, any DOM manipulations are **sideEffects**. That's why you got the wrong order.

I looked at other bundles and it seems they just don't support sideEffects or support it selectively.
author: alexander-akait

body: If you want to achieve the absence of unnecessary initial requests, I recommend using `import(...)`, if there are problems in some library you can set it at the loader level just using - https://webpack.js.org/configuration/module/#rulesideeffects.
author: alexander-akait

body: Also - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/15610#issuecomment-1085777006
author: alexander-akait

body: Let me remind you that there is no such thing in js like `import styles from './teaser.module.css';`, this is processed by loaders and plugins and always automatically brought into the DOM and create side effects, so if you want to use `sideEffects: false` or `sideEffects": ["*.css"]` and have initial bare CSS most likely you made a mistake
author: alexander-akait

body:
I get your point about CSS imports being side effects and side effects in javascript should not affect the order, but I want to share why the current behavior is causing real headaches in production

In the reproduction repo, you can see that just changing or removing a barrel file (like `@segments/carousel/index.ts`) can change up the CSS order across the entire app without touching a single css file or import. This isn't just a small issue - in larger apps, this means a simple refactor can silently break styles in completely unrelated components because a class like `.teaserCarouselButton` class could appears in a different order in the final bundle

Other bundlers might handle sideEffects "less correct" for CSS, but at least they give us a consistent CSS order we can work with and understand.
For me the "order can not be guaranteed" part just doesn't make much sense for CSS (even as a css modules user)

When discussing about those problems you very often hear things like just use tailwind or just use styled-components or just increase the specificity or just use `!important` or ensure that two CSS files never target the same dom element.
And although those might be options I believe vanilla CSS with simple understandable selectors should also be possible

For me the ideal tradeoff would be something in between - keep the good parts of JS tree-shaking but make CSS ordering predictable (like in rspack, vite and parcel)
author: jantimon
jantimon

body:
Remove:
- `watchFiles: ['./src/**/*']`, `watchFiles` is just to refresh the page for additional files that I am not involved in the bundling, for example PHP.
- Use `import App from "./app";` instead `import App from "./App";`, paths are case independent in macos only, but some unofficial webpack's plugins may be written without taking this into account (I recommend using linter), relying on this behavior is also not safe because there may be developers on your team using a different operating system. We do not validate this automatically because it can greatly slow down performance and recommend using other utilities for this.

After these changes all works fine.

> Webpack's acting well. Use static imports to import modules in a single file, or dynamic imports to import external modules. 


> In case I need to run the side effects of a module dynamically (i.e. global statements) but need to pack into single file?

You can have `import ... from "...";` and `require(...)` in any place of your code, so just rewrite your `import(...)` and put it before calling your function where your need dynamic value.

body:
Extending Worker Syntax:
A new loader can be introduced to parse inline worker code and convert it into a Blob.
The loader would generate a URL using URL.createObjectURL().
Advanced Configuration:
A worker configuration option can be added to webpack.config.js.
This option would allow enabling features like caching and dynamic imports.
Fallback Mechanism:
Webpack should gracefully fall back to traditional separate worker files if Blob or URL.createObjectURL() is not supported.
Optional Webpack Plugin:
A plugin can be created to handle inline workers, manage Blobs, and inject them into the bundle.

body:
A bumped into a `tsc` issue `eslint.config.ts` because of this one. `@types/eslint` package was introduced via this chain of deps:

```
pnpm why @types/eslint

@sentry/nextjs 10.23.0
ŌööŌöĆŌö¼ @sentry/webpack-plugin 4.6.0
  ŌööŌöĆŌö¼ webpack 5.102.1 peer
    ŌööŌöĆŌö¼ @types/eslint-scope 3.7.7
      ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ @types/eslint 9.6.1
```

This was the error:

```
TS2375: Type 'Config<RulesConfig> | CompatibleConfig | ReactFlatConfig | ReactHooksFlatConfig |
{ name: string; ignores: string[]; } | ...' is not assignable to type 'Config<RulesConfig>' with
'exactOptionalPropertyTypes: true'. Consider adding 'undefined' to the types of the target's properties.
```

I managed to git rid of it via this change to `pnpm-workspace.yaml`:

```diff
 overrides:
+  "@types/eslint": "npm:eslint@>=9" ## https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19118
```

It was also necessary to delete `tsconfig.tsbuildinfo` before re-running `tsc` to prevent errors from caching.

Typings have been embedded into `eslint` since https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/18928 so usage of `@types/eslint` is de-facto deprecated. External typings are stricter than the latest ones so can trigger `tsc` errors in users' ESLint configs.

body:
There is actually no problem here, this is expected behavior. In the new version we have improved `browserslist` and `environment` support (auto detection features of your environment), so if your `browserslist` support `import()` and you set `experiments.outputModule` (https://webpack.js.org/configuration/experiments/#experimentsoutputmodule) to `true` we use ECMA modules format for chunks, unfortunately they don't have HMR support yet, but we plan to implement it in the near future.

To fix it you can do:
- Disable `experiments.outputModule`
- If you still want generate ECMA modules, but don't use `import` and use `jsnop` to load chunks, you need to set `output.chunkFormat` to `array-push`

Feel free to feedback

author: UtkarshBirla28

body:
From our docs https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#runtime-chunk:

- `chunkLoading` is about how we will load async chunks - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L1134

- `runtime` is about should we generate runtime for this entry at all, or not

- `asyncChunks` is a valid solution for you, but you use `splitChunks` and set `chunks: 'all'`, so you forcefully ask webpack to extract some modules, you need filter chunks in such cases, because options above were using for entries level, but split chunks were applied globally in your case

Shortly when you have `asyncChunks` we avoid creation of async chunks - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/fab79c1b5dc0b596929187c1bcfd90c08d9e4fbc/lib/buildChunkGraph.js#L402, i.e. any dynamic `import()`'s don't create extra chunks, but splitChunks works with the built chunk graph and allow merge/extract modules into new chunks, these options can be confused, but they are doing different things

Feel free to feedback

body:
The reason is that, external modules should only be present in the main chunk, not async chunks, normally this is done by a plugin called `EnsureChunkCondition`.

However hot update chunk is a special case, imaging that we don't have any external module in the first build, but we import an external module in the second build, we should indeed insert external module into al modules, so the external module should be placed in hot update chunk, but it can't as it is an external module which cannot be present at async chunks

author: im-shubhamm

body:
**I don't think this is a bug. Here is my analysis:**


**Direct Cause**
In the default configuration of the LazyCompilationPlugin, the entries option is set to true. This means that Webpack treats the application's entry files as lazy compilation modules as well.
When the page is accessed for the first time, the browser executes the entry file, but instead of receiving the application's initialization code (such as ReactDOM.render), it gets a Promise that waits for the server to finish building. Since the browser's main logic flow is blocked by this Promise (or the framework cannot handle the entry export being a Promise), the page fails to mount and appears blank.

**Deeper Cause**
Design trade-off: The original intention of lazy compilation is on-demand building to reduce startup time. Although it is technically possible to lazily compile the entry file, in reality, the entry file must be executed immediately to launch the application.
State synchronization delay: On the first request, the proxy module uses client-side scripts (client.keepAlive) to notify the server to start building. Before the server finishes building and pushes updates to the browser via HMR (Hot Module Replacement), the browser is in a "waiting" state. If the HMR handshake or update logic is not properly handled within the entry fileŌĆÖs Promise chain, or there is a timing gap, the user sees a blank page. After refreshing, the server has already cached the result (active state is true), so it returns the built content directly, and the page loads normally.

body:
Hi ­¤æŗ
IŌĆÖve been investigating this issue and was able to reproduce it using the provided repro repo.

On first load I see the runtime error:

__WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE_react__ is not defined

After inspecting the emitted bundle:

The hot-update chunk references the external via module.exports = __WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE_react__

The main runtime bundle does not appear to define this external variable

I then traced the generation path in ExternalModule:

The React external is handled via the default external source (getSourceForDefaultCase)

This generator returns an expression referencing the external but does not declare any runtimeRequirements

In contrast, other external types (e.g. script externals) explicitly declare runtime requirements

My current hypothesis is that when lazyCompilation + HMR are combined, the external runtime initialization may not be ensured before the hot update executes due to missing runtime requirements.

IŌĆÖm continuing to investigate but wanted to share findings in case this aligns with expected behavior or known constraints.

author: iblawal
stormslowly

body:
"Hi @stormslowly,
This is a great catch. YouŌĆÖre spot on about the 'lagging discovery'- if the initial wrapper doesn't account for the external's parameter, HMR essentially pushes a function body into a scope that doesn't have the corresponding identifier, leading to that ReferenceError.
Regarding your 'try-best' idea:
    . The Pro: Pre-populating static externals via compilation.addInclude during finishMake is a clever way to 'force' the wrapper signature to be future-proof. Since these are externals, the 'extra footprint' is virtually non-existent (just the stub/reference), so the dev-mode performance hit should be negligible.
    . The Concern: We should double-check if addInclude might inadvertently force these modules into the initial bundle even if they are only ever used in an async chunk later on. While they are 'externals' (and thus expected to be in the global scope/UMD), we want to ensure we aren't messing with the user's intended chunk splitting logic more than necessary.
One question for your investigation: If we go the addInclude route, how do you plan to handle cases where different entry points have different external requirements? Would you apply this globally to the main entry, or try to be surgical based on the dependency graph?


________________________________
From: pshu ***@***.***>
Sent: Monday, April 20, 2026 10:05 AM
To: webpack/webpack ***@***.***>
Cc: iblawal ***@***.***>; Comment ***@***.***>
Subject: Re: [webpack/webpack] After turning on lazyCompilation, the first load of externals will fail (Issue #19134)

[https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/415655?s=20&v=4]stormslowly left a comment (webpack/webpack#19134)<https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19134#issuecomment-4279664415>

Sharing a direction for discussion ŌĆö not sure if it holds up, would appreciate a sanity check.

What I think is happening

Lazy compilation makes external-dependency discovery inherently lagging. On the first compile there's only a proxy, so the UMD factory closure is generated without __WEBPACK_EXTERNAL_MODULE_react__ in its parameter list. When HMR later installs a react module whose factory body references that identifier, the lookup falls through the old closure's scope chain and throws ReferenceError.

One complication: externals supports function / RegExp forms, where the effective set isn't knowable until something requests a module. So any fix has to pick what subset it's willing to cover.

A "try-best" idea I'd like feedback on

For the statically-enumerable subset of externals (object / string / array-of-objects), the requests are known upfront. Could we pre-populate them into the entry during finishMake so the wrapper signature is finalized on the first compile? In dev mode the extra footprint is a handful of stubs, which seems acceptable if it avoids the race entirely for the common case.

Rough sketch:

```js
compiler.hooks.finishMake.tapAsync(PLUGIN_NAME, (compilation, callback) => {
const requests = staticExternalRequests(compiler.options.externals);
// for each request:
// compilation.addInclude(context, new EntryDependency(request), { name: undefined }, cb)
});
```

Function / `RegExp` externals wouldn't be covered by this and would likely need a different mechanism.

Question

Does this direction seem reasonable, or is there something about the lazy-compilation or externals flow that makes this a bad fit? Happy to be pointed at whatever I'm missing.

ŌĆö
Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub<https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19134?email_source=notifications&email_token=BP34CTO5TJGWDNI3GI6ONQ34WXY5NA5CNFSNUABFM5UWIORPF5TWS5BNNB2WEL2JONZXKZKDN5WW2ZLOOQXTIMRXHE3DMNBUGE22M4TFMFZW63VHMNXW23LFNZ2KKZLWMVXHJLDGN5XXIZLSL5RWY2LDNM#issuecomment-4279664415>, or unsubscribe<https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/BP34CTPBZ2RFEZBD22RMQ634WXY5NAVCNFSM6AAAAABVITHGDWVHI2DSMVQWIX3LMV43OSLTON2WKQ3PNVWWK3TUHM2DENZZGY3DINBRGU>.
You are receiving this because you commented.Message ID: ***@***.***>
author: iblawal

body:
@stormslowly  

Hi, thanks for sharing. For external modules, [they have their own chunkCondition](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/11a351947927354282a64694b9bcd7cc0707f75f/lib/ExternalModule.js#L734). IŌĆÖm just concerned that `compilation.addInclude` could break this behavior, meaning every external module would end up in the entry chunk.
author: hai-x

body:
Yes.
The "try best" solution is a trade-off in dev mode between a better development experience within lazy compilation and chunk size.  In my opinion, in dev mode, the cost of the chunk size increase is acceptable.

With your reminder, I think we can do some filtering and add parts of external deps to `addInclude` to avoid other costs and unexpected size effects. e.g. external type ["css-import", "module"].
Currently, this is a very rough idea.

Thank you @hai-x  for your comments.
author: stormslowly

body:
Thank you both for the insights. @hai-x, the concern regarding chunkCondition for external modules is valid, we definitely want to avoid accidentally pulling everything into the entry chunk.
@stormslowly, I agree that a dev-mode trade-off is often acceptable for better DX, but filtering for specific types like ['css-import', 'module'] sounds like a much safer middle ground. IŌĆÖm interested in seeing how we can refine that filtering logic to minimize the impact on chunk size.

body:
Was able to trace this down to the path separator being different on the two platforms. Below is the deterministic module ID mapped to the full module identifier (which is used to determine the sort order using `<` and `>` comparisons):

```text
150417: "C:\\Users\\danade\\Playground\\jszip-bundle-repro\\node_modules\\jszip\\lib\\nodejsUtils.js"
460905: "C:\\Users\\danade\\Playground\\jszip-bundle-repro\\node_modules\\jszip\\lib\\nodejs\\NodejsStreamInputAdapter.js"
554644: "C:\\Users\\danade\\Playground\\jszip-bundle-repro\\node_modules\\jszip\\lib\\nodejs\\NodejsStreamOutputAdapter.js"

460905: "/workspaces/playground/jszip-bundle-repro/node_modules/jszip/lib/nodejs/NodejsStreamInputAdapter.js"
554644: "/workspaces/playground/jszip-bundle-repro/node_modules/jszip/lib/nodejs/NodejsStreamOutputAdapter.js"
150417: "/workspaces/playground/jszip-bundle-repro/node_modules/jszip/lib/nodejsUtils.js"
```

Using this and given:
`/` char code: 47
`U` char code: 85
`\` char code: 92

This means that the sort order is inherently different on the two platforms since directory separators are given different values.
author: D4N14L

body:
Speaking with @dmichon-msft, it seems that it would be helpful to provide a hook that we can use to define a comparator that gets used across the bundle operation. Currently it appears that `compareModulesByIdentifier` gets used pretty universally, ex. during module concatenation: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/3919c844eca394d73ca930e4fc5506fb86e2b094/lib/optimize/ModuleConcatenationPlugin.js#L815-L816

Here are other uses: https://github.com/search?q=repo%3Awebpack/webpack%20compareModulesByIdentifier&type=code

If we could override this behavior across the board, this _should_ help resolve the issue.

Alternatively, that method could be modified to handle the difference in path separators, however this may miss other scenarios where the full module ID would cause an unstable sort on other platforms (ex. externals identifiers start with `external ...` which may not mesh correctly with non-external identifiers which path from `/...` for Linux and `<driveLetter>:\` for Windows)
author: D4N14L

body: We should draft a PR for this, which will include this as a test in our snapshot tests in the repo.

The surface symptom could be addressed by having the default sort for final render use `compareModulesByIdOrIdentifier` instead of `compareModulesByIdentifier`.

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/fab79c1b5dc0b596929187c1bcfd90c08d9e4fbc/lib/javascript/JavascriptModulesPlugin.js#L676-L683

Hi @alexander-akait,

I originally found this problem in our Production build where both Terser and module concatenation are enabled.  The values still show up as full names.  They cannot be removed because they are legimately used, they just aren't used by the intermediate module doing the import/export (the [Consolidator.ts](https://github.com/scameron/webpack-issue-mangling/blob/main/src/Consolidator.ts) module in my repo case).

I don't mind trying to push a fix but my problem is that I don't really have a good understanding of the meaning of the different states that can be set on the export info.  Today it calls `setUsedInUnknownWay` but what is the correct state of [Consolidator](https://github.com/scameron/webpack-issue-mangling/blob/main/src/Consolidator.ts)'s usage of [Enums2](https://github.com/scameron/webpack-issue-mangling/blob/main/src/Enums2.ts) when they are just being returned via [Provider](https://github.com/scameron/webpack-issue-mangling/blob/main/src/Provider.ts) and not explicitly referenced?

body:
@saurabhraghuvanshii, sorry that the PR didn't work out.  Thanks so much for taking a good crack at it, though.

One thing I came across recently that you might be interested is from this comment thread:
https://github.com/orgs/webpack/discussions/16863#discussioncomment-7292793

There's a link there to this article that describes the approach in some detail:
https://vercel.com/blog/how-we-optimized-package-imports-in-next-js

It basically performs an optimization where it takes pass-through rexports and it removes them altogether by rewiring the consumer and the target together with a direct dependency.  That goes even further than simply fixing the problem in this issue, it also eliminates barrel files altogether, which shrinks the build and makes it a lot faster.

I guess this is implemented in Next.js but I'm not sure if it's available as open source or not.  One reply to my question describes their approach as been quite heavy and complicated.

If it was possible to bring this kind of thing into standard Webpack as an optional optimization, I think that would be a really great improvement.  Probably not a small effort, though...

body:
Hi @alexander-akait,

This is great news that you were able to make a fix for this issue.  What a great use of AI tools!

I tried it out in our builds and it seems to work fine, with no immediate functional issues detected.  In terms of how much of a size difference the fix makes, I compared a build made with v5.107.2 against the new v5.108.1 and it does make some different, although it's not huge.  The build size is reduced by about **2.4MB**  and at runtime by about **300-400KB** depending on the workflow.  In both cases, this represents a reduction of about **0.8%**.

But considering this fix requires no code change at all in the application code and affects only internal name mangling, I still think this is an excellent result.

Thanks again for getting this one through!
