1. Check Node.js and npm Versions:
    Ensure you have the latest stable versions of Node.js and npm installed. You can update them using:
                           npm install -g n
                           n stable 
                           npm install -g npm@latest


2.Clear npm Cache:
       Sometimes corrupted cache files can cause unexpected behavior. Clear the npm cache:
                           npm cache clean --force


3.Reinstall Dependencies:
      Remove and reinstall all project dependencies:
                           npm uninstall -D <package-name> 
                           npm install


4.Set NODE_OPTIONS (Windows):
    To set the NODE_OPTIONS environment variable in Windows, you can use the set command:
                           set "NODE_OPTIONS=--openssl-legacy-provider"


5.Check for Compatibility Issues:
                            If the issue persists, carefully review the documentation or issue trackers of the JavaScript libraries or dependencies you're using. There might be known compatibility issues with newer Node.js or OpenSSL versions.

6.Run webpack:

         To run webpack, use the command: npx webpack


The --openssl-legacy-provider option should be used as a temporary workaround. It's generally recommended to update your dependencies to be compatible with the latest OpenSSL versions whenever possible.
If you're encountering difficulties or the issue persists, please provide more context:
The specific JavaScript library or dependency causing the error.


The relevant parts of your package.json file.
The exact commands you are running.




I hope this helps!

body:
Sorry, we don't support it, because it is unsafe operation, yes rollup (under vite) does it (they more aggressive in some optimizations), but generally it is unsafe. 

We use `terser` by default (for such optimizations), look at https://github.com/terser/terser: 

> pure_getters (default: "strict") -- If you pass true for this, Terser will assume that object property access (e.g. foo.bar or foo["bar"]) doesn't have any side effects. Specify "strict" to treat foo.bar as side-effect-free only when foo is certain to not throw, i.e. not null or undefined.

Feel free to feedback

body:
> Do not raise 'Module parse failed: Top-level-await is only supported in EcmaScript Modules' on entry module.
(At least the pure CommonJS modules case is fine.)

Unfortunately, we do not want to change the semantics of how top await works in JS, and such a message is more for the creators of libraries (not only) so that they do not make incorrect code. 

You can always generate async IIFE and all will work correctly.

Also you can always implement similar logic in your plugin, support for such exotic things in the core is not desirable.

Feel free to feedback

body:
That's expected, you are using an asset as an entry point (i.e. non JS resource) and ask to inline itself (i.e. `asset/source`), but you don't provide a context where we should inline it (i.e. we can inline asset in JS/CSS/WASM/any custom plugin), so we don't generate any JS, it was a bug fix in 5.96.0 and unfortunately your code relied on buggy behavior. 

To fix it just provide a context, i.e. from which original do you want to load, for example:

```js
new EntryPlugin(context, `data:application/node,module.exports = require("${filePath}?loadResource");`, {
  name: "resource",
}),
```

or

```js
new EntryPlugin(context, `data:application/javascript,export { default } from "${filePath}?loadResource";`, {
  name: "resource",
}),
``` 

in the last case you need to use `context.resource.default` instead `context.resource` in `_evaluate`

It was fixed in `5.96.0` because asset source generator can behave differently for different file types (in JS we need to quote our content, but for CSS and `@import`, i.e. you can inline assets inside CSS no need to generate quotes), so by default it does nothing if it doesn't know where it was requested from and by whom (JS/CSS/WASM/etc).

Feel free to feedback if you have any questions

body:
Hi @pengjunshu-houzz,

I investigated the issue and confirmed that a dynamically linked module referenced from a chunk fails if it hasnŌĆÖt been instantiated yet.

Possible Workarounds:
1.Ensure DLL is Instantiated First
Explicitly import the DLL module earlier in your entry script before loading any chunk-dependent modules.
This worked as seen in PR #1.
2.Use Deterministic Module IDs
Using deterministic module IDs helps, as shown in repro #2, but it complicates debugging.
3. Disable Async Chunks in Vendor

If applicable, disabling async chunking in the vendor bundle prevents JSONP callback issues, as seen in repro #3.
author: Niharika201

body: use python -m http.server instead of python3 -m http.server. error will not occur
author: syed-yaga

body:
1. Ensure the DLL is Instantiated Before the Chunk Loads

As shown in pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro#1, explicitly loading the DLL before the chunk prevents the issue.



2. Use Deterministic Module IDs

pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro#2 shows that using deterministic module IDs avoids the issue, though it makes debugging harder.

Try adding this Webpack config:

output: {
  chunkFilename: '[id].[contenthash].js'
}



3. Disable Async Chunks in the Vendor DLL

pengjunhsu-houzz/webpack-chunked-dll-repro#3 suggests preventing Webpack from generating async chunks in the vendor DLL to avoid jsonp callbacks interfering with instantiation.

Try setting:

optimization: {
  splitChunks: false
}




Expected Fix

Webpack should correctly handle module resolution in DLLs without requiring pre-instantiation. If this is an issue affecting many users, it might be worth filing or checking an official Webpack bug report for a fix in future versions.
author: angalagouthamkumar

body:
@pengjunhsu-houzz

I want to say it is expected. 

Short story: set `output.uniqueName: "app"` for `webpack.config.js` and `output.uniqueName: "dll"` for `webpack.dll.config.js`.

Long story - by default webpack takes the `name` of  `package.json` and use it as a postfix for the runtime functions (extra code for loading asynchronous chunks), in you case you have `self["webpackChunkwebpack_dll_repro"]` (look at generated initial chunks for common build and dll builds), it is a global function to load async chunks, but because you generate two different builds with the same `name` (as I said before we take the `name` of `package.json`) every build have own `self["webpackChunkwebpack_dll_repro"]` function and then override each other after loading, that is why when you load async chunk you break the code. 

After using `uniqueName` you will have two different functions to load async chunks - `self["webpackChunkapp"]` and `self["webpackChunkdll"]` and they will not override each other and everything will woks fine.

Feel free to feedback, if you want to can send a PR to improve docs about it

body:
Yeah, a bug, let's fix it.

Just note we officially support `swc` using - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/terser-webpack-plugin/?tab=readme-ov-file#swc (cache + multi threading), so you can't avoid using `swc-minify-webpack-plugin`
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Yeah, a bug, let's fix it.
> 
> Just note we officially support `swc` using - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/terser-webpack-plugin/?tab=readme-ov-file#swc (cache + multi threading), so you can't avoid using `swc-minify-webpack-plugin`

I was experimenting with performance between the two options and it seemed like `swc-minify-webpack-plugin` was marginally faster than using SWC through `terser-webpack-plugin`. I wasn't comprehensive with my testing, though, so it could have been a coincidence.
author: ArcanoxDragon
alexander-akait

body:
> [@ArcanoxDragon](https://github.com/ArcanoxDragon) I made some bench checks with `swc-minify-webpack-plugin` and `terser-webpack-plugin`:
> 
> Using this:
> 
> import type { JsMinifyOptions as SwcOptions } from "@swc/core";
> import TerserPlugin from "terser-webpack-plugin";
> 
> new TerserPlugin<SwcOptions>({
>   parallel: false,
>   minify: TerserPlugin.swcMinify,
>   terserOptions: {
>     compress: false,
>     mangle: !environment.noMangle,
>     // @ts-ignore
>     ecma: 2022,
>   }
> }),
> 
> is faster:
> 
>     * without cache around 150-200 ms (using your repo)
> 
>     * around 50-100 ms with enabled cache (using your repo)
> 
> 
> These number can better for big projects.
> 
> `swc-minify-webpack-plugin` just doesn't spent time on worker threads, so you have better time (you just set `parallel: false` to have the same behavior for `terser-webpack-plugin`)

Off-topic of the original issue, of course, but I'm seeing pretty close numbers between these two now during some additional testing. 

Our actual project is quite massive - it has over 150 script _entries_, and hundreds of modules. If I use the `parallel` option, it takes orders of magnitude longer to compile, but with `parallel: false` it's almost identical to `swc-minify-webpack-plugin`, so I suppose I can switch back to the TerserPlugin since that's recommended.

body:
Sorry, I don't think we want to change our logic due this exotic usage. 

> I wrote a hack plugin to remove this same origin check from the runtime.

We have hooks - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/web/JsonpChunkLoadingRuntimeModule.js (you can find them for each type and jsonp/es modules), so you can use them to modify runtime code without any hacks. They were literally created to solve problems like these.

And I will call it the right decision - using a custom plugin. 

> This has been reported a few times, but I don't think a correct resolution has been reached

Unfortunately, I don't think we should add this option or change our current logic, since I can't find any similar issue/discussions before. 

Anyway - feedback welcome.

> If there is a downside, then why do we not do the same origin check for prefetch tags?

Old browsers had bugs with this behavior, so we added this check, I'm honestly not sure if this problem is really relevant now, but changing this in some way could be a critical change (as I remember). It might be worth going deeper into the commit history, but I'm afraid it might not be that easy to find.

body:
@denis-migdal Just remove `enabledLibraryTypes` you don't need it at all, it is for libraries, but you build an application

Also you don't need such options:
- `compareBeforeEmit` (true by default)
- `concatenateModules` (true by default for the `production` mode) 

After this you will see:

```
Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading '0')
    at c (utils.ts:55:19)
    at l (astnode2tree.ts:48:19)
    at $ (index.ts:111:17)
    at index.ts:78:5
```

This is because:

**src/sbry/ast2js/utils.ts**

```
export const PY_CODE = __DEBUG__ ? new ARRAY_TYPE(4*MAX_NB_ASTNODES) : null as unknown as InstanceType<typeof ARRAY_TYPE>;
export const JS_CODE = __DEBUG__ ? new ARRAY_TYPE(4*MAX_NB_ASTNODES) : null as unknown as InstanceType<typeof ARRAY_TYPE>;
```
I'm not sure about your app structure but if you remove `__DEBUG__` or set it to `true` using `DefinePlugin` all works fine, looks like you need set Debug for this entry too or refactor your logic, but it is not related to webpack itself.

If you want to build an library, please use `output.library: { type: "module" }`, don't mix libraries with applications, it is different places.

Feel free to feedback

body:
@denis-migdal 

> Doesn't entries[].library.type work too ?

You just don't need it, because you already set `output.module`

> Mixing them causes issues ?
> An entry might depends on a library we build.

Mostly no, but there are different in build, when you build application we output runtime code for `browser` (because your target is `web`, for example you use CSS and we generate extra code for CSS to load it, because you can't use CSS files in Node.js), if you want a build library for Node.js I recommend to set `target: 'node'` and use `output.library`, we will avoid extra runtime code. If you want to build `universal` target, i.e. for browser and node you need rewrite your code and set `target: ['web', 'node']`.

author: syed-yaga

body:
It looks like Webpack v5.96.0 introduced a change that broke CSS file handling when using type: 'asset/source'. This happens because the updated AssetSourceGenerator.js causes originModule to be null, preventing CSS files from being processed correctly.

Workarounds:

1. Use raw-loader instead:
Replace your CSS rule with:

{
    test: /\.css$/,
    include: [
        helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
        new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
    ],
    use: ['raw-loader']
}

This correctly packages the CSS files.


2. Downgrade Webpack to v5.95.0:
If raw-loader isnŌĆÖt an option, rolling back Webpack to v5.95.0 will restore previous behavior.


3. Try asset/resource Instead:
Changing type: 'asset/source' to type: 'asset/resource' may work depending on your setup.


4. Check for Webpack Updates:
Since this is a regression, keep an eye on Webpack's GitHub for an official fix in a future release.



Let me know if you need help implementing any of these!
author: angalagouthamkumar

body:
This is a known regression in Webpack v5.96.0 related to how the `asset/source` type handles CSS files. Here are several solutions to fix your issue:
1.  Specify the "generator" Option

When using `type: 'asset/source'`, you need to explicitly configure the generator in Webpack v5.96.0+:

{
  test: /\.css$/,
  type: 'asset/source',
  include: [
    helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
    new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
  ],
  generator: {
    dataUrl: content => content.toString()
  }
}

2. Use the "raw-loader" 

This is a valid workaround and is actually the recommended approach for handling CSS files that need to be imported as strings:
{
  test: /\.css$/,
  include: [
    helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
    new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
  ],
  use: ['raw-loader']
}

3. Use Angular's Built-in CSS Processing

Since you're using Angular, you might want to leverage Angular's built-in CSS processing instead of a custom configuration:
{
  test: /\.css$/,
  include: [
    helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
    new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
  ],
  use: ['css-loader', 'postcss-loader']
}

4. Downgrade Webpack Temporarily

If you need a quick fix while waiting for a potential bug fix in Webpack:

npm install webpack@5.95.0 --save-dev

5. Use the `to-string-loader`

This loader is specifically designed for Angular components that need CSS as strings:

{
  test: /\.css$/,
  include: [
    helpers.root('_COMMON', 'archive', 'webcomponent'),
    new RegExp('OPF\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep + 'src\\' + path.sep + '.*\\' + path.sep)
  ],
  use: ['to-string-loader', 'css-loader']
}


Explanation:
The issue is that in Webpack v5.96.0, the `AssetSourceGenerator.js` file was modified to handle source maps differently, which inadvertently broke the handling of CSS files with `type: 'asset/source'`.

The most future-proof solution is either Solution 1 (explicitly configuring the generator) or Solution 2 (using `raw-loader`), as these approaches are less likely to be affected by future Webpack changes.
author: pranjalisr

body:
Duplicate of https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19235 and https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/18928, it is not a known issue, it was changed due a bad logic - using `type: 'asset/source'` and using asset as an entry point (or as an entry point for a child compilation) generates only asset and nothing more (i.e. no extra JS or CSS code to loading this asset), if you need to generate asset + JS code (for loading an asset module) you need to `import`/`require` from JS code, you don't need `raw-loader` or something else.


I don't known how `@Component` was transpiled and you don't provide a reproducible example so I can point where you need to fix it (there is a bug report in angular repo - https://github.com/angular/angular-cli/issues/29728). Anyway if you provide it I will show you.

The main logic is - if you want to generate asset and JS code to load this asset you need to `require`/`import` this module from JS.

Feel free to feedback

body:
This issue occurs because the # character in the file path is being misinterpreted as a fragment identifier in URLs, causing Webpack Dev Server to fail when resolving module paths.

Possible Workarounds

Rename the directory: The simplest fix is to remove the # from the path since Webpack Dev Server does not properly handle it.

Use a different working directory: Move your project to a path without special characters.

Check if Webpack has an escape workaround: Since this is an issue with path resolution, it might be worth opening an issue with Webpack to see if a fix is possible.

Hi ­¤æŗ

I'm currently exploring the webpack caching system and would like to work on this issue.

From the discussion it seems the solution could involve adding a filtering mechanism (like a `storeFilter`) in `IdleFileCachePlugin` to avoid caching large asset binaries.

Please let me know if this approach makes sense or if there's a preferred direction before I start working on it.

author: ARCHILODHA

body:
Hi ­¤æŗ

I'm exploring the webpack cache implementation and looking into this issue.

From reading the code, it seems binary assets are cached through the `store()` method in `PackFileCacheStrategy`, which eventually stores them via `pack.set(identifier, etag, data)`.

Would introducing a filtering mechanism (e.g. `storeFilter(identifier, data)`) before storing items be the right approach to avoid caching large binary assets?

Happy to work on implementing this if it aligns with the intended design.

Thanks!
author: ARCHILODHA

body:
Hi, I'd like to work on this. I looked at the diff @Knagis posted ŌĆö skipping identifier.includes("|asset/") and identifier.startsWith("Compilation/assets|") in IdleFileCachePlugin.js's store hook works, but an unconditional skip means all asset modules get excluded from cache regardless of size, which could hurt incremental build times for projects with lots of small assets (icons, small JSON, etc.).
I'd propose instead adding a configurable threshold, e.g. cache.maxAssetSize (bytes), defaulting to something reasonable so behavior improves out of the box, but assets under the threshold still get cached. Happy to implement this with tests if that approach sounds right ŌĆö let me know if you'd prefer a different shape (e.g. a boolean cache.cacheAssetModules opt-out instead).

So at the moment we have no plans to change this, to simplify regular expressions we have plans to support globs.

The documentation is a bit simplified and doesn't show the type, but if you want to improve it you can send a PR.

> Can you clarify?

author: hai-x

body:
@hai-x We have here two bad DX things (as I see):

1. Impossible to pass all options to devtool without using plugin

So to solve it we can allow to pass an object notation:

```
{
  type: "source-map" | "eval" | "other-values" // we just will do the same as we have - options.devtool.includes("source-map") | options.devtool.includes("eval"),
 cheap: true | false // in plugin it is a `columns` so `cheap` is an alias for `columns`, same for other options
  ...optionsForPlugin // options can be omited and default will be used
}
```

Also I think we can make soft deprecation for all `output.devtool*` and `output.sourceMapFilename`, i.e. just put `TODO`

2. We can't specify different devtool for different assets, for example JS and CSS, I think we can use this

```
[
  {
    test: /\.css$/
    type: "source-map" 
    ...otherOptions
  },
  {
    test: /\.js$/
    type: "eval" ,
    ...otherOptions
  }
]
```

I think we should use this when `css` support enabled and it is `mode: "development"`

3. Implement `ignoreList` as described above

So we can split these implements into multiple PRs - the `2` we can implement after `1` and `3`
author: alexander-akait

This might be related but I also started having issues this morning:
When upgrading to webpack 5.99.0, our application started experiencing a runtime error: `ReferenceError: shouldCancel is not defined` in redux-saga. body:
The type is valid, because you can have here `ExternalModule`/`ConcatenatedModuleModule`/`NormalModule`/`CssModules`/etc, i.e. any instance of `Module`, you need to narrow your type if you want to use properties of `NormalModule`, because other module types can not have some properties, also plugin API allows to implement own Module's (i.e. `extends Module`) and so we can't use type like `NormalModule | ExternalModule | ConcatenatedModuleModule`.

Feel free to feedback

body: Hello, because we don't support `named export` and we don't have such configuration example in our readme, `module.exports` means `export default`, when you use `export const module` you exports **named** variable, we never support it, so just use `export default` instead `export const module`, feel free to feedback

body:
It appears that version 5.97.0 introduces a `JS-generated type` for CSS. In this case, the error `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'exports')` causes a `module render error` and leads to a failure in processAssets for `hotUpdateChunk` in `HotModuleReplacementPlugin`. This finally causes `webpack-dev-middleware` is unable to recover from the failure.

So I think we can 
1. make sure cssData exists when reading `exports`.
2. Catch errors in `processAssets`  to prevent uncaught exceptions from causing HMR failures.

I've done some attempts and I've figured out something more:
- I've tried working with previous versions of our packages, but the issue was already there, only I hadn't found out
- I've tried pointing to remotes that are reachable and not reachable, but the issue is always there, so I think that the content of the remote app doesn't impact the issue
- I wanted to try working without the runtime file, but the app stops working. With `runtime` set to `false`, I have to set `experiments.outputModule` to `false` to make the rest of the app work, but with those settings the import of the remote app appears without single quotes:

  ```js
  module.exports = remote@http://localhost:3001/assets/remoteEntry.js;
  ```
- This happens even if I change the library settings in `ModuleFederationPlugin` to 
  ```js
  library: { type: 'var', name: 'host' }`
  ```
- Setting `runtime` to `single` doesn't solve the issue, because Webpack still outputs two `runtime-app` files
- Passing a `runtime` string to the `ModuleFederationPlugin` settings, or setting the field to `false` doesn't solve the issue

body:
@alexander-akait Thanks for taking the time to look into the issue. 

I've done some further investigation and I'd like to share what I've found.

### Why I don't think it's expected
I don't think the behavior I'm experiencing is expected, because:
1. The two generated files have the same name, the only thing that changes is the chunk hash
2. The content of the files is also very similar, sometimes the only thing that changes is the chunk hash of the referenced file(s)
3. The first output of the watch command and the output of the build command don't have this issue.

To make it easier to reason about it, I've added a new folder to my repository [generated-output-example](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/tree/main/generated-output-example).

In the folder there are two `runtime-app` files: [runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js) and [runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js). The first one is referenced under entrypoints in the [asset-manifest.json](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/asset-manifest.json) file, while the second one is referenced by the [app.51cd46ce3502605c797c.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/app.51cd46ce3502605c797c.js) file, which is also referenced in the [asset-manifest.json](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/asset-manifest.json) file. 

### What is happening when running the code in the browser
First thing, I've discovered that this doesn't only happen when changing code in the file that loads the micro frontend, it happens with every change to any file that's dynamically loaded by the main file (`app.js`). Anyway this is what happens:
1. After we save a change in a file that's dynamically loaded, the changes are saved in a chunk
2. The correct chunk is referenced by one of the two runtimes that are generated
3. The browser loads the two runtimes (one is requested by the manifest and the other by the `app.js` file)
4. Only the runtime referenced in the manifest holds the reference to the chunk with the changes
5. The runtime referenced in `app.js` references a chunk that doesnŌĆÖt have the changes
6. The browser only loads the chunk thatŌĆÖs referenced in the runtime thatŌĆÖs loaded by the app file and not the chunk that's references in the runtime that's listed in the manifest
7. If I look for the changes in the browser (Open the Developers Tools and Search) the change is never loaded by the browser

### How to check this in my repository
1. Checkout the project
2. Clear the public folder
3. Run watch
4. Add a log to `design-area.js` (for example `console.log(ŌĆÖtestŌĆÖ);`)
5. Look for the log inside the public folder and mark down the chunk hash. If there are more than one, mark all the chunk hashes of the files you find
6. Now open `asset-manifest.json` and open the `runtime-app` thatŌĆÖs referenced there
7. Open that file and look for the chunk hash: it should be there
8. Now go to the app file referenced in `asset-manifest.json`
9. Look for the `runtime-app` referenced there
10. Open the `runtime-app` file and look for the chunk hash(es) of the files with the initial log: itŌĆÖs not there

Let's do the same check inside my [generated-output-example](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/tree/main/generated-output-example) folder. 

1. I've added this log to the `design-area.js` file: `console.log('second test log');` (the log is not committed to the project)
2. The output code is only present in this chunk: [resources_js_chunks_design-area_js.6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/resources_js_chunks_design-area_js.6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12.js), so the chunk hash I should look for is `6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12`
3. If I open [asset-manifest](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/asset-manifest.json), I will find this runtime-app under entrypoints: [runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js)
4. If I look for the chunk hash `6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12` in this runtime, I can find it
5. Now I open the app that's referenced in the manifest, which is: [app.51cd46ce3502605c797c.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/app.51cd46ce3502605c797c.js)
6. The app references a different runtime: [runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js)
7. If I open the file and look for the chunk hash `6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12`, it is not there!

I hope this helps debugging.

-> [runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js)
-> [resources_js_chunks_design-area_js.2876b31bdeb0b3a137d5.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/resources_js_chunks_design-area_js.2876b31bdeb0b3a137d5.js)
-> no log here

The changes are in 
[resources_js_chunks_design-area_js.6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/resources_js_chunks_design-area_js.6572e66ecc4cdcf33f12.js)
which is only referenced by [runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/js/runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js) which in turn is referenced in the [manifest](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro/blob/main/generated-output-example/asset-manifest.json) file.

author: alexander-akait

body:
> If we will have wrong values for runtimes we will generate wrong runtime code and it will be broken, but your code works...

I wouldn't say that my code works, because the `app.js` chunk references the wrong runtime, so I'm not able to see the correct changes in the browser when running `watch`. 

I've done a couple more attempts to prove my point. 

### 1. Overwrite the generated `app` file
With the current setup, I've looked for the linked runtime in the generated `app` file, let's say the line:
```js
import __webpack_require__ from "./runtime-app.2f49b02885c5fb16b004.js";
```
And replaced it with the correct runtime, let's say:
```js
import __webpack_require__ from "./runtime-app.d0415bae809a613ce32c.js";
```

In this case the code works.

### 2. Remove the Module Federation plugin
I've removed the Module Federation plugin from the Webpack configuration and replaced the import of the remote application with an empty object.
```js
const { default: remoteMountMethod } = {};
```
Instead of:
```js
const { default: remoteMountMethod } = await import(
  'remote_bucket/remoteMountMethod'
);
```

Now the app chunk doesn't even reference a runtime, and everything works as expected.

### 3. Configure a different plugin
I've configured `webpack-assets-manifest` instead of `webpack-manifest-plugin`: the two manifests generate files with the same issues.
author: gtempesta-pixartprinting

body:
### Update

I've done some more attempts that can point to a possible solution. 

### 1. `CleanWebpackPlugin`

I've added [`CleanWebpackPlugin`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/clean-webpack-plugin) to the Webpack configuration. I thought that this would force the main chunk to link an existing asset, but it wasn't the case: it kept linking an old chunk which was already deleted, making the issue even more apparent. 

(This setting is not present in the main branch, as it doesn't add any value). 

### 2. `realContentHash`

Another attempt:

- changed `chunkhash` to `contenthash` in the `output` settings
- set `realContentHash` to `true` in the `optimization` settings

At this point making a change in the `design-area.js` file finally triggered an error from Webpack which made everything more clear:
```
ERROR in RealContentHashPlugin
Some kind of unexpected caching problem occurred.
An asset was cached with a reference to another asset (952ed40e29224276644e) that's not in the compilation anymore.
Either the asset was incorrectly cached, or the referenced asset should also be restored from cache.
```

### 3. Cache settings

At this point I went to the [Cache documentation](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/cache/) and tried different configurations, until I've found one that fixed the issue:

```js
cache: false,
```

I tried to apply this change to the original project, but **this is not sustainable**: every change now takes about 30s to build. 

Any other change from the documentation (for example changing the type to `filesystem`) doesn't solve the issue. 

But at this point it's clear that the issue is on the way that Webpack manages the cache: can anyone of the maintainers take a deep look at this topic?

You can already play with those settings in the [linked repository](https://github.com/gtempesta/webpack-issue-repro), just comment / uncomment `realContentHash: true` and `cache: false`. 

To trigger the issue you just have to start the `watch` process and add or edit logs in the `design-area.js` file.

body:
Shorty, because we generate this code in the chunk code to load runtime logic:
```js
import __webpack_require__ from "./runtime-app.cd925ca1545fa8a9af92.js";
```

we don't consider runtime chunk hash and including chunks (and modules) (due to this content hash doesn't work too), ideally we need to get runtime chunk of current chunk, then get all chunks from runtime (and nested) and consider them as a part of chunk hash, fix should be here - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/esm/ModuleChunkFormatPlugin.js#L206

body:
I have no ideas, even though we can see that it's down to mangling.

With regard to async, race conditions etc, it should be no problem. This plugin parallelises calls to Terser, but it itself is single thread and does no IO.

The place we get new names is the base54 object. We can see it here:

https://github.com/terser/terser/blob/d62b39f13f42c2ec6d17b7f92c7c8b4a014220c7/lib/scope.js#L1015

The consider function is called with small strings from surrounding code that will *not* be mangled (such as comments, property names, strings, keywords like "var" or "const" or "return"), to provide it with the frequency of characters. The get function is called to get a symbol. It's possible to, through the [nth_identifier option](https://terser.org/docs/options/#mangle-properties-options), pass a version of this object that simply returns symbols alphabetically instead of this contextual character counting.

One thing that *might* happen is that some unrelated code, like a comment or a string, is being passed into consider and influencing the names that are coming out. Is it possible that some code is different at the input? Such that "p" appears more often on AMD but on ARM "f" wins? Even a comment that gets removed?

We could isolate this by setting compress:false and mangle:true.
author: fabiosantoscode

body:
There's no code difference between the two files in the source code, and there's no comments or strings that are different in the uncompressed code. (edit: To be clear, I ran these tests with `compress:false, mangle:true` and I still saw the chunk diffs) This is a tough nut to crack...

One thing that comes to mind is it could be related to the Sentry plugin... Sentry injects a  sentryDebugId into the code using the BannerPlugin -- I opened a similar issue in the past with the sentry team and they ended up switching from using the chunk hash to the content hash which theoretically should have solved any issues arising from that...

 https://github.com/getsentry/sentry-javascript/issues/15745

Sometimes the only thing different in the file is the sentry debugId which should be impossible since its based on content hash... No idea how to explain this or reason about it because it just makes zero sense. If I replace one debugId with the other the files have identical hashes and contents.

Sometimes the debugId is different but _also_ some mangled names are swapped, in which case I would expect the debugId to be different since the contenthash is different. This introduces a chicken-or-egg problem because it's hard to tell if the debugId was different and therefore the mangled names got swapped because of the change in character frequency introduced by the different debugIds, _or_ if the debugId is different because the mangled names were different and that caused the contenthash to be different. 

The more builds I do the more I'm realizing it is usually 4-5 specific chunks that always end up with a difference... There's no code difference between the two architectures, and without mangling they're exactly the same. Somehow the sentry IDs can still be different despite the exact same code, so not sure what's going on there.

And then sometimes there's 0 difference and it's perfect. ­¤ś×
author: Redmega

body:
If Sentry can output different IDs into the source code, it's very normal that Terser would reorder mangled names because of it.

But you did say there's no diff between outputs other than this mangling difference, so it couldn't be this.
author: fabiosantoscode

body: @Redmega I have an idea why this might happen, but I think I need reproducible test repo or webpack configuration, shortly - webpack optimizations hooks are recursive, i.e. when you add new assets (or change them) and we try to minify again, we cache it to avoid extra work inside webpack (and cache it inside plugins), but the contenthash plugin works only after all minifications, so you can have a JS file and we minify it, them other plugins can inject extra content (for example comment) and we minify it again, so contenthash and content will be the same (looks like the same), that is why I need configuration - to understand hooks, you can try to debug it locally, here - https://github.com/webpack-contrib/terser-webpack-plugin/blob/master/src/index.js#L487, just look at input (just save them and look at diff) and how many times you run, it can help

body:
There is no problem here who is better  - `vite` or `webpack`, there is a **problem** with your library, webpack is simply stricter here, as it allows you to catch problems at the bundling stage, while white is less demanding, but this does not eliminate the problem that other tools and other assemblers will not work with your library in the same way, the behavior of webpack completely follows the Node.js/deno/bug behavior.

What's the problem?

You **can't* using `import` and `reexport` without extensions, this is literally mandatory to use for ES modules.

https://nodejs.org/api/esm.html#import-specifiers

> Relative specifiers like './startup.js' or '../config.mjs'. They refer to a path relative to the location of the importing file. **The file extension is always necessary for these.**

https://docs.deno.com/runtime/fundamentals/modules/

> With ECMAScript modules, local import specifiers must always include the full file extension. **It cannot be omitted.**

Using your library can be broken for other developers.

You have `"type": "module"`, it means your library is fully compatibility with ES modules.

body:
hi @echosonusharma This issue isn't caused by Webpack. you can refer to the suggestion provided by ChatGPT for more context.
> ­¤Ü© Why your onInstalled and onStartup listeners don't fire properly
WASM imports can block or delay the service worker startup: When you import a WASM module at the top level, it may delay or prevent the service worker from initializing correctly. If the service worker fails to start promptly, Chrome wonŌĆÖt register the listeners like onInstalled and onStartup.
Service Worker has strict lifecycle rules: If any of the top-level code (like an import or heavy sync operation) hangs or takes too long, the service worker may silently fail or not fully initialize.

body:
It looks like we compile the code twice: first for `entry.js` and then for `main.js`. 

When bundling `entry.js`, the `import("./module")` statement is transformed into a runtime expression, such as `import(__webpack_require__.p + "./" + __webpack_require__.u(chunkId))`. 

However, when we subsequently bundle `main.js` and using `resolve.alias`, this runtime expression becomes unanalyzable, leading to a runtime error.

body:
Sorry double bundling is very limited and there is an issue - https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/17121

> import() is statically analyse-able

Anyway you still can solve it, just use `entry` as a external source (just pseudo example, URL can be different due your architecture):

```
externals: {
  entry: 'module http://my-cdn.com/entry.js'
},
```

Double bundling is always bad idea, I strongly recommend avoid it and search other approaches - externals/module federation/dynamic `import()` loading already bundled code using `webpackIgnore` magic comment - https://webpack.js.org/api/module-methods/#webpackignore/etc, bundler does a lot of optimizations, so code in many places can be optimized/renamed/changed for perf reasons and so non analyzable, that happens here.

Unfortunately, solving this will require quite a lot of effort and will still not lead to an ideal solution.

Anyway feel free to feedback
author: alexander-akait

body:
I think there is another issue related to this, which is when the `output.filename` contains a directory segment, for example `filename: 'js/[id].js'`. In this scenario the generated url does not contain `/./` but instead `/../` which causes it to resolve wrong in the browser. I've created a simple example here: https://github.com/mariusGundersen/webpack-module-publicPath-issue

The problem was introduced here: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/19434/files#diff-cf94a953c7dcf19d00514b7cf5e7566b153ab8aa21c2002318fb5a38ff24b861R217-R221

I think it's a mistake to include the `rootOutputDir` in this case, since it is calculated to produce a relative path from the chunk location. But since the `publicPath` is prepended we no longer need to navigate relative to the current chunk, we are navigating relative to the `publicPath`.  If the `rootOutputDir` is removed then it works correctly. It might be that `rootOutputDir` should be included when `publicPath` is `auto`, so that the relevant code is then


```diff
"// setup Promise in chunk cache",
`var promise = ${importFunctionName}(${
	compilation.outputOptions.publicPath === "auto"
-		? ""
+		? JSON.stringify(rootOutputDir)
-		: `${RuntimeGlobals.publicPath} + `
+		: RuntimeGlobals.publicPath
- }${JSON.stringify(rootOutputDir)} + ${
+ } + ${
	RuntimeGlobals.getChunkScriptFilename
}(chunkId))
```

We introduced some ESM output optimizations in v5.99.0. Regarding your reproduction, it should work correctly in development mode with `dev-server` or in production mode builds alone.
 
> We sometimes want to check locally that the production build will work as expected in the host

Thank you for bringing up your concern. I recommend checking the production build output, as the `dev-server` will include HMR logic in your library bundle. Also, we do not support HMR for ESM library currently. :(


Anyway, I think this is a bug and may bring other regression.

author: alexander-akait

body:
In case someone stumble upon this in the future, I got it to work by also sharing `react/jsx-runtime` and `react/jsx-dev-runtime`, even though you have `react` and `react-dom`, the jsx and jsx dev runtimes seems to be treated as different modules by the bundlers. The root cause of the error is due to `React.__SECRET_INTERNALS_DO_NOT_USE_OR_YOU_WILL_BE_FIRED` being removed from React 19 runtime, so in your case it seems to call the variable but it no longer exist in React 19.

```
        shared: {
          react: { singleton: true, requiredVersion: deps.react },
          "react-dom": { singleton: true, requiredVersion: deps["react-dom"] },
          "react/jsx-runtime": { singleton: true, requiredVersion: deps.react },
          "react/jsx-dev-runtime": {
            singleton: true,
            requiredVersion: deps.react,
          },
        },
```

Ron Sims II provided a thorough explanation regarding the error in this stackoverflow thread:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/66194269/typeerror-cannot-read-propertyreactcurrentdispatcherof-undefined

Disclaimer: havent implemented it in prod yet but so far there doesn't seem to be any weird behaviour for the setup as long as your dependencies support the react 19 runtime
author: aarontanjaya

body:
Thanks for the tip. Sharing react/jsx-runtime and react/jsx-dev-runtime
resolved the module federation issue between React 19 and 18 for me as
well. I appreciate you sharing the workaround and the StackOverflow link.


On Sun, Jun 28, 2026, 10:59 PM aarontanjaya ***@***.***>
wrote:

> *aarontanjaya* left a comment (webpack/webpack#19563)
> <https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/19563#issuecomment-4828562385>
>
> In case someone stumble upon this in the future, I got it to work by also
> sharing react/jsx-runtime and react/jsx-dev-runtime, even though you have
> react and react-dom, the jsx and jsx dev runtimes seems to be treated as
> different modules by the bundlers. The root cause of the error is due to
> React.__SECRET_INTERNALS_DO_NOT_USE_OR_YOU_WILL_BE_FIRED being removed
> from React 19 runtime, so in your case it seems to call the variable but it
> no longer exist in React 19.

body:
**Thank you @alexander-akait for the quick response!**

IŌĆÖve prepared a minimal reproduction inside our boilerplate template ([gdc-template.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20882213/gdc-template.zip)). It includes both **Webpack** and **Vite** configurations. After extracting the archive, you can simply run:

```
npm install
npm run start
```
This will launch the Webpack version.

### A few important notes:

 - The app connects to our internal backend, and while IŌĆÖm **not allowed to share credentials or provide access**, the issue can still be **simulated without backend access**. You can observe the problem just by analyzing the build output.
 - Specifically, the final bundle will contain two instances of `@codemirror/state` and `@codemirror/view`.
 - If you **uncomment** the deduplication/aliasing lines in the Webpack config, you'll see that these packages are properly deduplicated and appear only once in the bundle, resolving the issue.

For context, weŌĆÖre not directly importing CodeMirror in our codebase. These packages are pulled in transitively by dependencies (including 3rd-party ones). All are using the **same version**, but deduplication does not occur automatically without explicit config.

Let me know if you need a stripped-down version that runs fully offlineŌĆöI can work on that if needed.

Thanks again for your help!

[gdc-template.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/20882213/gdc-template.zip)

author: alexander-akait

body:
The amd/umd guarantees that all external dependencies are loaded and executed before running your bundle.

Do you expect that external dependencies are executed after bundle?
author: hai-x

body: I expect that code added by [AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/a11302288d2a8851ff89405a122c1d04709574ea/lib/runtime/AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule.js#L53) to be executed synchronously on file execution, not inside amd/umd module factory. Because now it's called asynchronously after loading and executing dependencies, which makes document.currentScript, used to determine dynamic public path, unusable in this scenario.
author: roblan

body:
> Because now it's called asynchronously after loading and executing dependencies

To be clear, this is what [AMD(Asynchronous module definition)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asynchronous_module_definition) does. And this is not within the scope of `Webpack`.

> used to determine dynamic public path, unusable in this scenario. 

Why isn't `output.publicPath` set to "/eglebegle/" to align with `devServer.devMiddleware.publicPath`
author: hai-x

body:
> To be clear, this is what [AMD(Asynchronous module definition)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Asynchronous_module_definition) does. And this is not within the scope of Webpack.

It is in scope of webpack. Yes, module factory should and must be called asynchronously when all dependencies are loaded. BUT [AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/a11302288d2a8851ff89405a122c1d04709574ea/lib/runtime/AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule.js#L53) is a code injected by webpack to handle dynamic imports, and it is **not** a amd external dependency.

> Why isn't output.publicPath set to "/eglebegle/" to align with devServer.devMiddleware.publicPath

To show that setting publicPath to 'auto' does not work if there are external dependencies in generated amd module - this is the bug that is reported here.

I do not know how to make it any more clear
If output.publicPath is set to 'auto' (https://webpack.js.org/guides/public-path/#automatic-publicpath) dynamic imports in generated amd module will not work if module has any external dependencies. If there is none - they work just fine.

Why? Because code injected by webpack to determine public path automatically is run inside amd module factory. It should not be there. It should be run during file execution to have access to correct document.currentScript value.

I have updated repository to show when it works and when it does not.
author: roblan

body:
> Because code injected by webpack to determine public path automatically is run inside amd module factory.

Yeah. All we did was adding amd/umd wrapper for your bundle.

> It should not be there. It should be run during file execution to have access to correct document.currentScript value.

I believe [optimization.runtimeChunk](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/optimization/#optimizationruntimechunk) could be helpful for you. It will split all `runtimeModules` into a single chunk without wrapper.
author: hai-x

body: setting optimization.runtimeChunk to true and concatenating every entry with it's runtime would do the trick
author: roblan
alexander-akait

body:
I'm not sure why loading 2 script (almost) at the same time in not correct async code? they are separate libraries, not dependent on each other, that should not interfere with each other.

And load function **is** injecting code using script tags. Created dynamically, but script tag nonethless.

What I meant by "calculate it earlier" was a mechanism working like the effect of runtimeChunk = true and file concatenation. I would expect this to be build in, when target is amd/umd and public path set to auto.
author: roblan

body:
> I'm not sure why loading 2 script (almost) at the same time in not correct async code? they are separate libraries, not dependent on each other, that should not interfere with each other.

I don't say it is wrong, yes, it is a valid usage if they don't depend on each other, I just emphasized why it worked for the second chunk.

> And load function is injecting code using script tags. Created dynamically, but script tag nonethless.
> What I meant by "calculate it earlier" was a mechanism working like the effect of runtimeChunk = true and file concatenation. I would expect this to be build in, when target is amd/umd and public path set to auto.

How do you imagine calculating this earlier? The fact that it works (with `runtimeChunk`) is a consequence of the fact that the runtime chunk is injected first, since it is a separate file, so it works. Automatically public path initially relies on script tags, so dynamic loading will not work as expected, you can always implement your own path definition logic using plugins.

Could you give some more information why you do it this way and not use the classic approach?

> [@MickWest](https://github.com/MickWest) We looked at your profile files using https://ui.perfetto.dev/, and there is an interesting place - CleanPlugin. Will both runs be with empty `dist` (`output.path`)? If not please provide with empty, because on the second you have extra 6 second on clean plugin, sounds like you have files there

Interesting! If I delete the content of output.path before building, then the time returns to normal (2 seconds). Rebuilding after that (with the app and data in output.path) the build time goes back to 8 seconds. 

Setting output.clean: false has the same effect (i.e. faster, just 2 seconds)

My output folder contains code and data totaling 212 MB in 4,929 items. However, it can BUILD it in a fraction of a second, so emptying it should take no real time (and obviously does not, in 5.99.9)
author: MickWest

body: @MickWest Yeah, we see it on your profile, can you try debug locally and try to my solution, need understand which comment increase operations... Deleting of a directory is fast, but we use more intelligence approach, we compare emitted files with already exist to allow keeping assets which were not generated by webpack, the problem maybe there. Just interesting why you have such many files?
author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait 
> can you try debug locally and try to my solution, need understand which comment increase operations... 

Neither of the files seemed to make a difference. I copied each one over the local lib/CleanPlugin.js in node_modules/webpack

> Deleting of a directory is fast, but we use more intelligence approach, we compare emitted files with already exist to allow keeping assets which were not generated by webpack, the problem maybe there. 

Sounds like it. I "clean" the folder because the entire folder is generated with webpack, I WANT to delete anything that was not deleted with webpack. Basically I do it to ensure a clean build so deleted project data files(3D models, images, scripts, etc)  are not left, and previous versions of the compiled code are also deleted. 

> Just interesting why you have such many files?

Somewhat laziness, all but about 350 of them are from the AWS/Vendor PHP library. Of the remaining 350, about 200 are assets like 3D models and image files. I could certainly tighten it up, but that's not really the point, as it worked fine before. 

My project is a never version of Sitrec, but the behaviour exists in the older project.
https://github.com/MickWest/sitrec

Build instructions are in the readme

Is there a reason not to replace the unreachable statement (with zero declarations) with `""` instead of with `"{}"`?

https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/1d9cc240a29d17f3986d9e9f96f2589d823e832c/lib/ConstPlugin.js#L548-L549

---

> > Note: You might call this a bug in Firefox, since the {} is technically a block statement here and not an empty object expression. But I suppose it's specifically a warning for when you meant to type return {} and accidentally inserted a newline. So I would not expect them to consider this a bug on their end.
> 
> We always insert newline here, so that doesn't happen

I understand; it was just an aside speculating on why Firefox shows a warning in this case, despite it technically not being an expression after the return.
author: Mesoptier

body:
In your case it is safe to replace code on nothing, but not for all cases. The main problem we need to calculate context of a unused statement and it will take take (worse performance) and that is why we just replace it on `{}`

Example where we should use `{}`:

```js
if (process.env.NODE_ENV === "production") { /* a lot of code */ } 
else { /* a lot of code */ }
```

When you will bundle something for production you will get:

```js
if (process.env.NODE_ENV === "production") { /* a lot of code */ } 
else // removed by dead control flow
{}
```

And then terser (your minimizer) will remove empty branch and you will have:

```
if (process.env.NODE_ENV === "production") { /* a lot of code */ } 
```

Technically we can improve this, but I'm not sure if it's really worth it and if the loss in performance is worth it in this case.

/cc @hai-x

author: alexander-akait

body:
Am I correct in thinking that an unused statement in `ŌĆÄJavascriptParser.walkStatements` is always safe to be replaced with an empty string?

If so, `ConstPlugin.eliminateUnusedStatement` could accept an additional `inStatementList = false` parameter, that is only set for unused statements from `ŌĆÄJavascriptParser.walkStatements`. If `inStatementList === true`, the `{}` could be removed.

The wrapping `{}` could also be removed when there are hoisted declarations. That would also fix the warning @hai-x mentioned.

---

Not sure how up-to-date this is, but this comment lists the heuristics Firefox uses for when to show the warning:
https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1005110#c23 It notably does not include block statements, which is the issue (for both with/without hoisted declarations).

- pin `5.101.2` temporary

author: alexander-akait

body:
@alexander-akait I don't believe this is a `react-refresh-webpack-plugin` issue as previously suspected. The interceptor is simply wrapping the original `factory` from Webpack with its own wrapper - if we removed the interceptor, Webpack would be throwing the same error instead ([JavascriptModulesPlugin.js#L1509-L1512](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/javascript/JavascriptModulesPlugin.js#L1509-L1512)).

I've been tracking assets compiled by lazy compilation and found it compiles the same assets multiple times. Sometimes the second compilation excludes modules that were previously included. In cases where `factory` is undefined, I confirmed the module being loaded was missing from the assets it was included in during a previous compilation.

I suspect this might be related to mixing static and dynamic imports - the same module X could be imported statically in one place and dynamically in another. When a module is missing from a follow-up compile, I've checked the module connections and noted that the module still depends on the missing module. I plan to check today if it got moved into a separate asset/chunk in the second compile.

I'd love to provide a reproduction, but this appears to be a race condition since it isn't always the same assets that break. Creating a minimal reproduction is challenging without diving deeper into the root cause, which I'm working on now.

Any pointers on where to look would be helpful.
author: AdrianBannister

Did you get the same error?

author: alexander-akait

body:
> Did you get the same error?

Yes, but for context I work with @mjames-c and am looking at this while he's on leave.

> Hm, do you understand it?

I've worked with Webpack internals quite a few times. We have a couple of custom plugins that I've maintained and I recently rewrote our custom chunking plugin (hooks into `compilation.hooks.optimizeChunks`). This plugin is disabled for lazy compilation though to avoid muddying the waters. So I understand some of the internals, but still building my knowledge.

> If there are any specific signs which modules?

Looking at the logs, it's often our `proto` modules that are missing. That's why I mentioned the mix of dynamic and static imports - for our fake mode in dev we import some proto modules statically for mocking data, but the services using those protos are dynamically imported. It's not only protos though, some business logic modules are missing as well. I also found a mismatch for an asset called `vendors-node_modules_pnpm_rxjs_6_6_7_node_modules_rxjs__esm5_index_js.js` where it dropped from 103 modules to 20 modules, removing modules like `../node_modules/.pnpm/rxjs@6.6.7/node_modules/rxjs/_esm5/internal/Observable.js`.

> Is it happens for initial chunk without loading async or verse vice?

No, it's not happening for the initial chunk. It's happening after the runtime has started loading modules with jsonp chunk loading.

> That is really hard to say where can be a problem... How often you faced with it?

We've only just started using `lazyCompilation`, so it's been happening from inception. If we do a normal build with filesystem cache enabled and then switch to `lazyCompilation`, it seems to go much smoother.
author: AdrianBannister

body: @AdrianBannister If I understand correctly, the plugin - https://github.com/pmmmwh/react-refresh-webpack-plugin is also involved? Do you have other non official plugins, storybook uses lazy compilation with a lot of dynamic and initial chunks, and all works fine, that is why I think the problem can be somewhere in non official plugins... Is it possible to create reproducible test repo? I'll tell you honestly - without it we won't be able to help in any way, the error will be quite complex
author: alexander-akait

body:
After some more digging I found the culprit for this one. In our local dev config we set `output.chunkFilename` and `output.filename` to `[name].js`. With the chunk names being reused in some of the subsequent builds it would override the previous output. I've fixed this by changing it to `[name].[contenthash].js`.

As long as the `chunk.id` is unique across compilations this shouldn't be a problem for the default config.

You don't need to change your codebase, webpack **rewrite** your imports/exports (i.e. `require`/`module.exports`/`import .. from "..."`/`export`/etc) to requested format

Why do you set `experiments.outputModule`? I feel you don't understand what does this option do and what is it for.

body:
> What you can't seem to wrap your head around, is that it's been working in the way that I'd expect for at least 32 versions until 5.96.0 was rolled out. I set outputModule because the documentation said it was required in order to utilize type: 'module' per entry.

> Perhaps the way that it was functioning was a bug, and was fixed in 5.96.0. To me, the current functionality is more of a hindrance because the User now has to branch logic in the config to build things separately. Separate builds compounds the headache when watchers and directory/file cleanup comes in.

This is exactly how it always worked and was designed this way, you can set `output.module: false` to get `< 5.96.0` output (in such case all code will be like ES modules but without ES modules syntax), but I still think you don't understand how this option works and what it was created for.

Again - `experiments.outputModule` say webpack - please generate ES modules in bundled files (your codebase **can** be in any format, it is not related to your source code),  in `5.96.0` we introduce ES modules split chunks support using `export`/`import` (ES modules syntax), and so you got ES modules syntax in split chunk in `5.96.0` (because you literally set `outputModule` to `true`, i.e. please use ES module syntax).

Your application entry without `type` (i.e. use the global output format) and you have `experiments.outputModule` (it sets the global output format to ES modules), so webpack generates ES module syntax. If you want to skip ES modules syntax (ES module keywords) generation you can set `output.module: false` (set global output format to ES modules but without `import`/`export` keywords) and your application will work like before.

body: We just care about nightly releases. To fix this we need a github workflow with cron at 0000 every day.
author: evenstensberg

body:
```yml
name: Nightly Release

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 2 * * *' # Runs at 2:00 AM UTC every day
  workflow_dispatch: # Allow manual triggers

jobs:
  nightly:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          registry-url: 'https://registry.npmjs.org/'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Build project
        run: npm run build

      - name: Version package with nightly tag
        run: |
          DATE=$(date +'%Y%m%d')
          npm version prerelease --preid nightly.$DATE --no-git-tag-version

      - name: Publish to npm (nightly)
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
        run: npm publish --tag nightly --access public
```

I'm looking into this as well, no thoughts yet.

Issue here is we rename `__webpack_require__` using javascript parser, which doesn't mutate the underlying AST directly, so export parser plugin cannot know if export specifier has changed

It's hard to change current parser arch to mutate the AST. One possible solution is to make export parser plugin to get the tagged data from compatibility plugin

author: arikorn

body:
@arikorn 

> Looking at your code, is that an actual fix or just a diagnostic test (i.e. it looks like you were mainly undoing a fix to a different problem)?

This is the actual fix. The logic becomes buggy when a concat module is shared in multiple chunks, and itŌĆÖs no longer needed because the original issue has already been resolved through another, more reliable approach.

> taking a cue from your PR, I just disabled the concatenateModules optimization and everything is fine even with the current webpack release (5.102.1). What downside is there in skipping this optimization? At least in Companion, it doesn't affect file size much, if at all. (And of course, once the problem is fixed we can re-enable it -- or not? sometimes I wonder how much value there is in squeezing every last bit of optimization out of a codebase... Any thoughts?)

In my opinion, `concatenateModules` improves not only bundle size but also runtime performance:

Each module in `webpack` is wrapped in one function. So fewer isolated modules mean fewer function stacks.
Also, module exports are normally stored on a module object, so importing something like `import x from "x"` requires a property lookup on that object.

With `concatenateModules` enabled, those exports become direct variable references instead of object properties. In other words, `static variable access` is generally cheaper than `object-property access` at runtime (IŌĆÖm not entirely sure how modern engines optimize this nowadays).

>  once the problem is fixed we can re-enable it

Yeah, we plan to make a release within this two weeks.
author: hai-x

body:
Thank you @hai-x for the extra detail. I'll look forward to the next release!  Meanwhile, just to wrap up this issue...

First of all, thank you @alexander-akait  and @hai-x for your patience, advice and help!

Webpack is now fully working on Companion again with the following workarounds

- Set `module.parser.javascript.importMeta: false`
- Enable module output (in experiments and output),
- Ensure that `devtool` is neither `eval` or `undefined` (the latter defaulting to 'eval' when mode = 'development'),
- Set `module.parser.javascript.url: false`. I'm pretty sure this one can be fixed, but I was running into what were essentially one of @dhdaines issues in #16878. (May comment there too...)
- Disable the `concatenateModules` optimization

Here's some thoughts/concerns that arose from our process:

1. Preamble: Personally, I have no issue with disabling these features in fact, I am grateful that you have put in those options. Webpack does a whole lot more, and I don't feel compelled to use every feature "just because"! (That said, we do have to do some things a bit more manually now, such as moving certain assets...)
2. Foremost: If `import.meta` processing replaces `import.meta.url` with a hardcode string, I don't see how it can be useful for delivering to multiple locations -- wouldn't hardcoding the build-platform's file structure cause it to break when run in a different folder? (as it did for me) I am sure you understand this problem, because you already treat `import.meta.url` differently when it's inside `new URL(...,)`. This leads to inconsistent behavior where sometimes it is left as a variable and other times it's hardcoded...
3.The solution to the topic of this Issue was not to use `devtool: '...eval...'`: Is the incompatibility of 'eval' and 'modules' documented? Is it a bug? -- perhaps that's part of why "module output" is considered experimental? At the very least I would say the difference between `false` and `undefined` (when mode is development) seems problematic.
4. Alexander's suggestion not to use `node:` options when the target is node still doesn't sit right with me, though I'm happy to heed it. Again, is this documented? As mentioned above, I did not see such advice in the reference documentation.

What do you think?
author: arikorn

body:
> Set module.parser.javascript.importMeta: false

Unfortunately, we can't do anything about this, which is why we have the option to change the behavior; there is no universal way to solve this problem.

> Enable module output (in experiments and output),

They are stable, you can use them, we just avoid breaking change right now

> Ensure that devtool is neither eval or undefined (the latter defaulting to 'eval' when mode = 'development'),

We are planning to fix it and adding support `import.meat` for eval devtool

> Disable the concatenateModules optimization

You **should not** to do it

> Foremost: If import.meta processing replaces import.meta.url with a hardcode string, I don't see how it can be useful for delivering to multiple locations -- wouldn't hardcoding the build-platform's file structure cause it to break when run in a different folder? (as it did for me) I am sure you understand this problem, because you already treat import.meta.url differently when it's inside new URL(...,). This leads to inconsistent behavior where sometimes it is left as a variable and other times it's hardcoded...

This is the most difficult part of the settings, since you are using the node platform, for you keeping `import.mtea.url` can be normally, but if you compile code for browser or non ES modules env, we need replace it on something

> Alexander's suggestion not to use node: options when the target is node still doesn't sit right with me, though I'm happy to heed it. Again, is this documented? As mentioned above, I did not see such advice in the reference documentation.

You can use `node:`, feel free to do it

What harm would there be, particularly for a node app? -- although to be fair we do have a substantial browser component as well.  (Also, or alternatively, is there any way to see how many modules are actually being concatenated?)

body: That is expected because, because of https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/config/defaults.js#L640C16-L640C28, by default for performance reasons webpack considers `node_modules` as immutable directory (managed), you need to stop webpack if you want to run something external that can modify node_modules, or write a simple to reload/purge cache

author: aboks

body:
> Still, is there a logical reason why the filesystem purge is needed as part of 'normal' run but not when watching

If we clear the file system during watching, we will need to read files on every build (compilation), meaning cache will not work (we will purge it).

But we need to refresh file system before using (you can reuse compilers between different build), also custom file systems can use it for initialization/clearing of some internal values
