author: KhushiYadav-26

body:
Hi ,
IŌĆÖve investigated the issue and prepared a fix. Could you please assign this issue to me so I can open a PR?

The bug is caused by an incorrect optimization step where Webpack drops the import reference inside an unused function and rewrites it as a global variable instead of the imported binding. I've isolated the exact transformation stage and verified the fix locally.

Happy to proceed once assigned. Thanks!
author: Anurag-1401

body:
You can enable `minimize: true` to minify your code, which will remove the unused function declaration.

The underlying reason is that we analyze `importŌĆōexport` relationships in the module using [innerGraph (inner-module tree-shaking)](https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/#inner-module-tree-shaking). Since the unused function is never expected to execute at runtime, it won't be included in the parser analysis. 

If you really want to guarantee import identifier correctness, you can disable innerGraph as a workaround by setting `optimization.innerGraph: false`.
author: hai-x

body: `minimize: true` doesn't prevent the `const __` from being renamed by Webpack, which is what led me to finding this. We have Terser configured to preserve identifiers named `__` on its end, but it can't do that when Webpack has already renamed it.
author: anomiex

body:
> minimize: true doesn't prevent the const __ from being renamed by Webpack

Yeah, it doesnŌĆÖt. It only helps remove the unused function.

> This causes an unrelated variable in the entry module that happens to have the same name to be renamed.
 
The reason for renaming top-level symbols is to prevent modules inside `__webpack_modules__` from accessing symbols in the parent scope of the entry module. This ensures that the inlined entry module can run at the top level instead of being wrapped in an IIFE. ThatŌĆÖs also why `optimization.avoidEntryIife: false` helps.

I think you can rename `const __ = i18n.__;` to something like `const __i18n = i18n.__;` directly in the source code.
author: hai-x
alexander-akait

body:
> I think you can rename `const __ = i18n.__;` to something like `const __i18n = i18n.__;` directly in the source code.

Our i18n system requires that the function be named `__`. So, no, we cannot.

I'd like to do things differently so we don't have to preserve that name in the minified bundles, but the only other options I've found are either including around 30 MB of `.map` files in the distribution (and some of our users are size-conscious and would likely point to this as "bloat", even though the files aren't sent to end users' browsers), forgoing the automated translation system entirely, or trying to hack around it somehow. None of those appeal.

> Can you provide the real code/example where you need to handle `unused`?

No, because we don't. But we do need the `__` in the entry point to not be renamed, and the preservation of `unused` with the incorrect global variable reference is causing that to be broken.

If that renaming were happening because of a valid optimization, I wouldn't have reported a bug here, same as I've never reported a "bug" with the module concatenation feature that also sometimes renames the symbols (instead we just turn `concatenateModules` off in our builds). But in this case, the renaming turned out to be because of the bug reported here, where an unused function somehow has its imported-variable reference incorrectly changed to a global-variable reference.

I'm surprised by how much people here are defending the preservation of `unused` with the incorrect global variable reference. It's clearly a bug that that happens. Either the unused function should be removed entirely or the variable reference inside it should be to the correct imported variable.
author: anomiex

body:
@anomiex 

>  Either the unused function should be removed entirely or the variable reference inside it should be to the correct imported variable.

We literally do it, your terser options **prevent it**, and you can't use other names and you can't change terser setup (if I understand this correctly), we're not backing down on anything, we want to understand why an unchanged reference in unused function is a problem for you, these code should **never** executed and so will not a problem.

> But we do need the __ in the entry point to not be renamed, and the preservation of unused with the incorrect global variable reference is causing that to be broken.

> If that renaming were happening because of a valid optimization, I wouldn't have reported a bug here, same as I've never reported a "bug" with the module concatenation feature that also sometimes renames the symbols (instead we just turn concatenateModules off in our builds). But in this case, the renaming turned out to be because of the bug reported here, where an unused function somehow has its imported-variable reference incorrectly changed to a global-variable reference.

Sorry, I can't understand your here.

If you really want to keep `__` untouch, you can disable `innerGraph: false`, I don't see any problems with webpack here, looks like you mix global and local variables without explicit using something like, `window.__` (or from other libraries), webpack can't understand, he logically assumes that all variables in this case are local and can be renamed, and unused functions will be removed at the minification stage. And to be honest, I donŌĆÖt understand what exactly we need to fix here and how should we fix this, do not rename variables (but it is valid for local variables), do not concatenate modules (but it is valid too), handle unused function (but why if you never use it)?

I'm asking you questions not to argue or convince you, I want to understand your case more deeply and understand what solutions I can offer you and how we can solve these problem.
author: alexander-akait
anomiex

body:
> Yes, Terser does remove the unused function. But only after Webpack has already renamed the constant due to Webpack's incorrect transformation.

> I've already explained how it causes a problem: it causes Webpack to unnecessarily rename a constant in unrelated code.

Sorry, but where is incorrect transformation? It is a local variables, so we **can** rename it, it is an optimization, if you don't want it, just disable it, again your code rely on mixed local/global/etc logic, this is quite unusual, and most likely, classic optimizations will not work for you

> The unwanted side effect occurs before Terser runs. Therefore, it does not matter what Terser does.

Which? Do you have a custom plugin? If yes, you can use webpack hooks to prevent some optimization for some function/variables/etc

> The function unused is now trying to refer to a global variable named __. This global variable reference causes unwanted side effects in other parts of the bundle.

> You can ignore the exact details of the unwanted side effect. The important part is that this does cause a problem even though the code is never executed.

But it is **unused** function, it will mean it **never been used**.

You said above no global/local/etc mixed logic, but literally in the next sentence you claim the opposite

Sorry, I don't see any bugs here, webpack uses the classic optimization here - we don't process/handle what isn't used, `unused` function is **unused** so we don't need to spend time on it and we don't need to touch it, because minimizer (any - terser/swc/etc) will remove it later, you still can disable `innerGraph` here and we will handle the `unused` function
author: alexander-akait

body:
>> I've already explained how it causes a problem: it causes Webpack to unnecessarily rename a constant in unrelated code.
>
> Sorry, but where is incorrect transformation?

I've already explained this in detail. The incorrect transformation is the conversion of a module-local variable reference into a global variable reference.

> again your code rely on mixed local/global/etc logic,

Again, this is incorrect. As long as you continue to think this, you are not understanding the problem.

> Which? Do you have a custom plugin?

The reproduction case provided here does not contain any custom plugin. You should be able to clearly see this.

Since it seems to have confused you earlier, I'm not going to discuss the more complex real life use case that caused me to discover this bug.

> But it is **unused** function, it will mean it **never been used**.

The incorrect transformation of the unused function causes side effects elsewhere. I don't know how to better explain this to you.

> You said above no global/local/etc mixed logic, but literally in the next sentence you claim the opposite

I think you need to go back to what I wrote earler, starting with "Let me try to explain it to you, step by step", and read it again more carefully. You seem to be confusing my explanation of the incorrect transformation Webpack applies with what the input code is doing.

> Sorry, I don't see any bugs here, webpack uses the classic optimization here - we don't process/handle what isn't used, `unused` function is **unused** so we don't need to spend time on it and we don't need to touch it, because minimizer (any - terser/swc/etc) will remove it later, you still can disable `innerGraph` here and we will handle the `unused` function

The bug is that, in not touching it while removing the module-local variable declarations it depends on, Webpack **is** transforming those module-local variable references into global variable references.

This incorrect introduction of global variable references causes side effects in Webpack's later processing, **before** the minimizer gets a chance to remove it later.

One possible solution: since you know the function is unused, remove it entirely instead of leaving it unchanged while changing the context around it in ways that would require changes within it for it to remain correct. Don't wait for the minimizer to do this, as by that time other optimizations have already gotten confused by it.
author: anomiex

body:
> The incorrect transformation of the unused function causes side effects elsewhere. I don't know how to better explain this to you.

Unused function should not generate any side effects, if it generates side effect for you case you can't use the inner graph optimization, just disable it, we can do nothing here, sorry

> I think you need to go back to what I wrote earler, starting with "Let me try to explain it to you, step by step", and read it again more carefully. You seem to be confusing my explanation of the incorrect transformation Webpack applies with what the input code is doing.

There is not incorrect transformation, unused function **never creates side effects** it is like javascript (not only) works

We don't remove unused functions because terser will do it, we avoid extra work where we don't need it

> This incorrect introduction of global variable references causes side effects in Webpack's later processing, before the minimizer gets a chance to remove it later.

Which and how? That is why I am asking multiple times about it above

> Don't wait for the minimizer to do this, as by that time other optimizations have already gotten confused by it.

How? It is just top level unused function, any minimizers (and other optimization tools too) will catch this scenario
author: alexander-akait

body:
> > This incorrect introduction of global variable references causes side effects in Webpack's later processing, before the minimizer gets a chance to remove it later.
> 
> Which and how? That is why I am asking multiple times about it above

This is the one I know of, as mentioned in the original report:

* Which: In the test case provided here, it causes `optimization.entryIife` to unnecessarily rename a constant.
* How: The incorrect introduction of a global variable reference to a variable of the same name as the constant causes the code for `optimization.entryIife` to decide that it needs to avoid a name collision with that imaginary global variable.

There may be other side effects that I'm not aware of.
author: anomiex

body:
> Which: In the test case provided here, it causes optimization.entryIife to unnecessarily rename a constant.
> How: The incorrect introduction of a global variable reference to a variable of the same name as the constant causes the code for optimization.entryIife to decide that it needs to avoid a name collision with that imaginary global variable.

it requires to avoid problems with concatenation with other files, to avoid externals importing and more.

Can you create the **real** example where your code will be **broken**, will **not work** as expected, will throw **an error**?

Again - unused function hasn't **side effects**.
author: alexander-akait

body:
> Can you create the **real** example where your code will be **broken**, will **not work** as expected, will throw **an error**?

You want real code? Fine. Usually maintainers of tooling packages don't want to look at a 14000-file repository using thousands of other npm packages as a "reproduction", but since you insist, have a look at the test failures on https://github.com/Automattic/jetpack/pull/46000/commits/479952490a579eb0b95e422472dbc0578f7d7e41. The error you're looking for starts at [line 15335 of the log for the build step](https://github.com/Automattic/jetpack/actions/runs/19576807939/job/56064279084#step:9:15336). An explanation of what's going on with that error can be found in [this blog post](https://developer.wordpress.com/2022/01/06/wordpress-plugin-i18n-webpack-and-composer/#problem-2-webpack-optimization-breaking-i18n-function-calls). As the blog post explains, the error in the build is to catch what would otherwise result in the i18n system not extracting those strings for translation, which would result in end users with their language set to non-English seeing English text rather than text in their language.

The unused function is somewhere inside https://www.npmjs.com/package/@wordpress/dataviews. Probably there's more than one in there. The "i18n.js" is actually https://www.npmjs.com/package/@wordpress/i18n, which gets externalized rather than included in the bundle.

What it all boils down to is what I've been saying here all along: the incorrect transformation of a module-local variable reference to a global variable reference (by changing the context around an unused function while keeping the unused function unchanged) causes `optimization.entryIife` to unnecessarily rename a constant that would otherwise not be renamed.

> Again - unused function hasn't **side effects**.

You really should stop saying this. At best it's irrelevant if you consider "an incorrectly introduced global variable reference inside an unused function" as being different from "an unused function".
author: anomiex

body: If you need the original names of variables, you should be running your tool over the source code or much earlier in the pipeline. The expectation that you can pass code through a build tool and that the build tool shouldn't make changes to the code is unreasonable, webpack makes zero promises about preserving variable names. You have also already been told what setting you can disable to achieve your desired behaviour.
author: absidue

body: Alternatively if correcting your translation system to look at the source code, instead of bundled code is too much work, you could instead request a webpack setting for a blocklist that should be avoided when renaming variables, as the crux of the issue is not that webpack is renaming variables but that it is renaming them to names that collide with the magic globals that your translation system is looking for but webpack doesn't know about.
author: absidue

body: @absidue If you don't have anything useful to contribute, please don't butt in. Thanks.
author: anomiex

body:
If you don't want to hear from me that's okay, but it is obvious from the existing discussion that this issue is deadlocked, as the webpack maintainers consider this to be working as designed as the webpack output is supposed to be passed to terser where all variables names would be mangled anyway (with the default settings) whereas you are insisting that that behaviour is a bug.
So changing the approach to asking about making the variable renaming configurable (e.g. blocklist of names or supplying a function to generate names), may resolve the deadlock.

Making a feature request for something that might (but probably won't) work around the bug won't change the fact that there's a bug that is introducing an erroneous global variable reference, as I've explained at length above.

Whether they choose to do anything about it or not won't change that either (until it gets fixed anyway).

author: MattIPv4

body:
> their usage isn't determined at runtime

Sorry for the confusion. What I meant is that, for [alveusgg/data@9370cf4/src/ambassadors/images.ts#L846](https://github.com/alveusgg/data/blob/9370cf4b0d88b69fc917b0aea1054e5eca678a2c/src/ambassadors/images.ts#L846), the usages of those import bindings of the asset modules cannot be determined at compile-time right now.

Because in [optimization.innerGraph](https://webpack.js.org/blog/2020-10-10-webpack-5-release/#inner-module-tree-shaking) (a.k.a. deep scope analysis), we donŌĆÖt track `ObjectPattern` (your case: `ambassadorEmoteImages` and `ambassadorBadgeImages` mapping to many images) to form the entire usage chain (export -> import). But, I think we can improve it.

> their usage is dropped from the bundle

This is actually done by `Terser` (default minimizer), not `webpack` itself. You can verify webpackŌĆÖs raw bundle via `optimization.minimize: false`.

body:
> Does Webpack not give Terser the ability to drop assets when it drops their imports? 

Yeah. `Terser` only applies to JavaScript, not to image assets.

> This still feels like a Webpack issue as Terser is the default minimizer that I haven't configured at all?

You are right. When you enable `optimization.minimize: true` (it's enabled by default in `production` mode), `webpack` will use it as default minimizer.

author: hai-x

body:
Thanks! :)

I can definitely see how that would be easier for Webpack. It'd be nice if Webpack tracked which entry points use which functions and inserted them accordingly, though.

Workarounds that I'm considering:

- Maintain separate util files for each entry point. (Kind of a pain)
- Split my util file into a bunch of different files, with one util function per file. (Also kind of a pain XD)

Since my project has `sideEffects: "false"`, I think Webpack technically has enough information to safely split imported functions into different chunks, but I can see how this might be a fair amount of work to implement.

Does this also mean that if my entry points use separate functions from `lodash-es`, for example, then all of the used lodash functions will end up in the main bundle?
author: neoncube2

body:
I understand the problem youŌĆÖre trying to solve: you want to make the entry (main) bundle as small as possible.

In your case, `await import('./separate-bundle')` creates an async chunk, but `webpack` already knows that the `./separate-bundle` module has been loaded because it was imported and init via the `./util` import. So the async chunk(separate-bundle) will not include the same module content that has already been rendered into the parent (sync) chunk(main-bundle).

> It'd be nice if Webpack tracked which entry points use which functions and inserted them accordingly, though.

We already track export usage (not individual functions) across different runtimes(i.e. [entry points](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/entry-context/#entry)). However, a dynamic import does not create a new runtime; it remains the runtime of its parent chunk.

> Since my project has sideEffects: "false", I think Webpack technically has enough information to safely split imported functions into different chunks

`sideEffects` is used for export usage detection; it is not meant for splitting module to different parts according to export usage.

> but I can see how this might be a fair amount of work to implement.

Ideally, this could be implemented via export inlining (that is, inlining the entire function declaration into the importer module). However, this would introduce significant complexityŌĆöfor example, how to correctly handle access to outer closures within the function.

> Does this also mean that if my entry points use separate functions from lodash-es, for example, then all of the used lodash functions will end up in the main bundle?

Yes, it will.
author: hai-x

body:
Thanks for the detailed reply! :)

> I understand the problem youŌĆÖre trying to solve: you want to make the entry (main) bundle as small as possible.

Yes, exactly :)


To work around this, I just tried splitting each of my util functions into separate files. Following the original example, this would mean that I now have two files:
* utils/separateBundleUtilFunc.js
* utils/mainBundleUtilFunc.js

For my actual project, this resulted in ~100 util files, and it successfully reduced the size of the main entry point when gzipped by 3.2% (96.3KB -> 93.2KB) or when raw by 2.5% (304KB -> 296KB).

This makes me think that a relatively simple way to implement a feature like this might be for Webpack to automatically split each function into its own separate module.


Following the original example, this would mean that  Webpack would treat `util.js` as two separate modules:

**Module util.js::separateBundleUtilFunc.js**
```js
export function separateBundleUtilFunc() {
  console.log('util function only used in separate bundle');
}
```

**Module util.js::mainBundleUtilFunc.js**
```js
export function mainBundleUtilFunc() {
  console.log('util function only used in main bundle');
}
```

And if the original import code looked like this:

```js
import { separateBundleUtilFunc } from './util.js'
```

Then it'd be treated as
```js
import { separateBundleUtilFunc } from './util.js::separateBundleUtilFunc.js'
```

Since Webpack already has good support for splitting functions from different files into different chunks, this approach seems like it might automatically take care of tricky edge cases. But maybe I'm being naive :)
author: neoncube2

body:
From my side what I understand is that ---> WebpackŌĆÖs chunking decisions are made at the module level, and once a module is synchronously reachable from an entry runtime, it must be fully instantiated there. Even though export usage is tracked, webpack canŌĆÖt safely split a single moduleŌĆÖs exports across chunks without changing execution semantics (shared state, side effects, execution order, etc.).

The manual workaround of splitting utilities by file aligns with how webpack expects bundle boundaries to be expressed today. While it does increase file count, it makes sync vs async ownership explicit and avoids surprising behavior at runtime.

The idea of treating individual exports as virtual modules is interesting conceptually, but as mentioned above, it would require export inlining or similar transformations and would introduce significant complexity around closures, shared state, sourcemaps, and tooling assumptions.

Given those constraints, this seems like expected behavior rather than a bug, and the current recommendation is to structure source files so that async-only code is not reachable from the sync entry graph.

When a file has been marked as not have any side effects (via the `sideEffects` flag), it should be safe to split its functions into different bundles, I think. @hai-x, do you agree? This is what I was trying to say with my early `sideEffects` comment.

author: alexander-akait

body:
# Bug Analysis: module-import externals hoisted to wrong chunk

## Problem Description

When using `externalsType: 'module-import'` with dynamic imports, external modules are incorrectly hoisted to the main entrypoint chunk instead of the dynamic chunk that actually uses them.

### Current Behavior (BUG)
- External import statement appears at the top of `dist/main.js` (line 1)
- External module is included in the main chunk's `__webpack_modules__` object
- The main chunk doesn't actually use the external - only the dynamic chunk does

### Expected Behavior
- External import statement should appear at the top of `dist/1.main.js` (the dynamic chunk)
- External module should only be included in the dynamic chunk that imports it
- Main chunk should not contain any reference to the external

## Root Cause Analysis

### 1. ExternalModule.chunkCondition() Method

Located in `lib/ExternalModule.js` (lines 684-688):

```javascript
chunkCondition(chunk, { chunkGraph }) {
  return this.externalType === "css-import"
    ? true
    : chunkGraph.getNumberOfEntryModules(chunk) > 0;
}
```

**Problem**: This method allows external modules to be added to ANY chunk that has entry modules. It doesn't check if the chunk actually uses the external module.

### 2. ModuleExternalInitFragment Hoisting

When an external module with `externalsType: 'module-import'` is code-generated:
1. A `ModuleExternalInitFragment` is created (line 483 in ExternalModule.js)
2. This fragment contains the ES module import statement
3. The fragment is added to `chunkInitFragments` in the code generation result
4. During chunk rendering, all `chunkInitFragments` from modules in the chunk are collected and hoisted

**Problem**: Since external modules can be incorrectly assigned to the main chunk (due to loose `chunkCondition`), their init fragments get hoisted to the main chunk output.

### 3. Chunk Graph Building Process

During chunk graph building (`lib/buildChunkGraph.js`):
- Modules are assigned to chunks based on their dependencies
- External modules are assigned based on `chunkCondition()` check
- The current implementation allows externals in any chunk with entry modules

## Solution

### Fix Strategy

The `chunkCondition` method should be more restrictive. External modules should only be added to chunks that:
1. Actually contain modules that import the external, OR
2. Are entry chunks that transitively depend on modules using the external

However, checking transitive dependencies during `chunkCondition` is complex. A simpler approach:

**Option 1**: Modify `chunkCondition` to check if any module in the chunk actually imports this external module.

**Option 2**: Don't assign external modules to chunks during graph building. Instead, collect them during code generation based on which chunks actually use them.

**Option 3**: Fix the init fragment collection to only include fragments from modules that are actually used in the chunk.

### Recommended Fix: Option 1

Modify `ExternalModule.chunkCondition()` to check if the chunk contains modules that actually reference this external:

```javascript
chunkCondition(chunk, { chunkGraph, moduleGraph }) {
  if (this.externalType === "css-import") {
    return true;
  }
  
  // For module-import externals, only add to chunks that actually use them
  if (this.externalType === "module-import" || 
      (this.externalType === "module" && this.buildInfo?.javascriptModule)) {
    // Check if any module in this chunk imports this external
    for (const module of chunkGraph.getChunkModulesIterable(chunk)) {
      if (module === this) continue;
      const connections = moduleGraph.getOutgoingConnections(module);
      for (const connection of connections) {
        if (connection.module === this) {
          return true;
        }
      }
    }
    // Also allow if this is an entry chunk (for backwards compatibility)
    return chunkGraph.getNumberOfEntryModules(chunk) > 0;
  }
  
  // Default behavior for other external types
  return chunkGraph.getNumberOfEntryModules(chunk) > 0;
}
```

**However**, this approach has a limitation: during `chunkCondition` evaluation, the chunk might not have all its modules assigned yet, so we can't reliably check if modules in the chunk import the external.

### Better Fix: Option 3 - Fix Init Fragment Collection

The real issue is that init fragments from external modules are being collected even when the external module shouldn't be in that chunk. We should:

1. Only collect init fragments from modules that are actually used in the chunk
2. For external modules, only include their init fragments if they're actually referenced by modules in the chunk

The fix should be in `JavascriptModulesPlugin.renderModule()` or `renderChunk()`:

- When collecting `chunkInitFragments`, verify that the module is actually used in the chunk
- For external modules, check if any module in the chunk actually imports them

### Implementation Plan

1. **Modify ExternalModule.chunkCondition()**: Make it more restrictive for module-import externals
2. **Update init fragment collection**: Ensure external module init fragments are only added to chunks that actually use them
3. **Add test case**: Verify the fix works with the reproduction case

## Files to Modify

1. `lib/ExternalModule.js` - Fix `chunkCondition()` method
2. `lib/javascript/JavascriptModulesPlugin.js` - Potentially update init fragment collection logic

## Testing

Test with the provided reproduction case:
- Main entrypoint: `src/index.js` (no external imports)
- Dynamic chunk: `src/chunk.js` (imports external-lib)
- Expected: external-lib import should only appear in `dist/1.main.js`, not `dist/main.js`

author: alexander-akait

body:
> Webpack does nothing with type of your package.json, generated code doesn't depend on this.

This is strange, because generated profile is explicitly different when built with and without `type` in package.json. i.e., `chunks[0].modules[0].moduleType` field `javascript/esm` vs `javascript/auto` and `chunks[0].modules[0].identifier` ŌĆö it has prefix `javascript/esm|/Users/...` when type is module

> Please provide a code of external-dependency

I haven't provided it, because it doesn't exist, it just some random external library. As far as i found out, webpack is not introspecting externals.

Either way, I'm attaching a full package with the reproduction [testcases.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24943102/testcases.tar.gz). It has 3 projects:
* external-dependency; module that has default export and one named export, built with webpack with 3 output targets: module, amd, commonjs; has exports field in package json to provide correct version for import and require
* main-explicit-type; project that uses external-dependency and basically logs and than re-exports external-dependency default and named export; built with webpack with 3 output targets: module, amd, commonjs; has type="module" defined in package.json;
* main-implicit-type; replica of main-explicit-type with the only difference: missing type field in package.json.

And there are also 3 tests, all of them are checking that default export is a function and named export is a number.

* test-amd.html
  * main-explicit-type re-export of default export is wrong, named export is correct
  * main-implicit-type re-export of default export is correct, named export is also correct;
* test-commonjs.cjs: loads 2 versions of CommonJS modules from main-explicit-type and main-implicit-type projects;
  * main-explicit-type re-export of default export is wrong, named export is correct
  * main-implicit-type re-export of default export is correct, named export is also correct;
* test-module.mjs: loads 2 versions of ESM modules from main-explicit-type and main-implicit-type projects; both produce correct result.


Expected module structure produced by main-explicit-type and main-implicit-type
```
{
  __esModule: true,
  default: function defaultExport() { ... },
  namedExport: ...
}
```

Actual module structure export of `main-explicit-type` project for amd and commonjs tests
```
{
  __esModule: true,
  default: {
    __esModule: true,
    default: function defaultExport() { ... },
    namedExport: ...
  },
  namedExport: ...
}
```

> AMD is outdated format and often your need specify more options for such stuff.

That is expected, but I was not able to find correct combination of settings. There seems to be no way to tell that external is actually ESM module wrapped into AMD callback. And it looks suspicious that adding type to package.json results in different output.

I've also updated build configuration (compared to the original version in the ticket) to include commonjs + ESM output as mentioned above. It seems that only ESM version produces correct result.

After fixing the configuration, I'm seeing an .svg file get generated as expected when using `[contenthash][ext]` or `[path]-[contenthash][ext]`. However, as soon as I add `[name]` to the pattern, I start getting a 0 byte file named `virtual` instead of the SVG file, like before:

body:
@alexander-akait 

> you have `target: 'web'`, and no `module` module in the `web` target

This problem still occurs for `target: 'node'`. I pushed the commit to the reproduction repository and you can try with `npm run build:node`.  
In this case, the build will perform successfully, but `./foo.cjs` does not bundled for entry: `js.mjs`.

If this is still intended behavior, can't we bundle .cjs files from .mjs?

> ts rewrites your code to `import` due `nodenext` (note in `ts` you have **node** target)

No, ts doesn't rewrite both `import` and `require` for `nodenext` and `.mts` extension. (You can run `npm run build:tsc` in the reproduction repository and check the emitted js in `./dist_tsc`.)  
So webpack should receive the ESM code.

P.S. The following code doesn't handle `JAVASCRIPT_MODULE_TYPE_ESM`, but is this mean that `all legacy formats that webpack supports (CommonJS, AMD, SystemJS) are not supported` ?
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/7f662db3ca9002398b1420857c2676b6ba37d837/lib/dependencies/CommonJsPlugin.js#L252-L257
author: jet2jet

body:
Oh, I see your problem, let's mark this as a bug, looks like we handle `createRequire` only for `javascript/dynamic` and `javascript/auto`, but `createRequire` should be handled by `javascript/esm` too, we need to extract logic for `createRequire` into own parser plugin and enable it for modules

> P.S. The following code doesn't handle JAVASCRIPT_MODULE_TYPE_ESM, but is this mean that all legacy formats that webpack supports (CommonJS, AMD, SystemJS) are not supported ?

Yes, no amd, systemjs, commonjs and etc, only `createRequire` in ECMA modules and ECMA modules syntax itself

body:
Unfortunately no, you must specify the type, because CSS can be generated as a separate file, as well as inject into JS, in which case the size is actually other, you are getting `0`, because you don't use API properly to get size, you need to call `CssModule.size("css")` to get the real size of css or `CssModule.size("js")` to get size injected in JS, yes our types is `string=` and ideally it should be `string`, but because some non official plugins register own modules we can't change it right now (due breaking change), I don't recommend to use `CssModule.size` directly, we have `stats` and you can get all information from there by generated source types, in future CSS can be inject in more things, so changing it will report invalid size information that is bad for bundler analyzer. 

Feel free to feedback
author: alexander-akait

body:
Ok, in the end it's easy enough to pass in "css", so I can do that on my side.

I appreciate the nuance of how CSS is represented now, I and it makes sense to accept a type parameter to express that.  But it is a bit unfortunate that the default doesn't do at least something meaningful, since it seems to work for all other modules (at least the ones we use).  What about returning JS + CSS by default, which would capture the complete size of the module representation regardless of how it's split?

It's a fair point that we should probably not hand-roll our own stats generation but it's a legacy implementation that works so we haven't bothered trying to reimplement based on `stats` yet.  Maybe someday.

body:
@alexander-akait Thanks for looking into this. We may have another real-world test case from `vscode-jest` (see jest-community/vscode-jest#1277) if that would be useful.

We can reproduce a runtime break that appears to line up with the nested `__webpack_require__` behavior discussed here.

You can debug with the following approach:
1. In `package.json`, change the "hard" webpack version between:
   - `5.94.0` (works)
   - `5.105.4` (breaks)
2. Run `yarn` after changing the version, to make sure the lockfile and installed package are updated.
3. Run:
   `yarn compile:dev` to invoke webpack 
4. Then inspect the non-minimized output file in:
   `out/extension.js`

In our case:
- `5.94.0` works
- `5.105.4` fails at runtime during extension activation

We also compared the emitted dev bundle and found a meaningful difference in the rebundled Jest code. In the working build, the inner bundled `__webpack_require__` is renamed to a nested name; in the broken build, it remains as `__webpack_require__`.

Working:
```js
/***/ "./src/InlineSnapshots.ts":
/***/ ((__unused_webpack_module, exports, __nested_webpack_require_396__) => {

var _utils = __nested_webpack_require_396__("./src/utils.ts");
```
Broken:
```js
/***/ "./src/InlineSnapshots.ts":
/***/ ((__unused_webpack_module, exports, __webpack_require__) => {

var _utils = __webpack_require__("./src/utils.ts");
```

This seems consistent with the wrong runtime being used for already-bundled Jest code.

IŌĆÖll attach the two generated out/extension.js files from each webpack version for reference:

- [extension-94.js](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25830092/extension-94.js)
- [extension-105.js](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25830111/extension-105.js)

body:
Hi maintainers,

IŌĆÖd love to take this up and work on a fix.

From what IŌĆÖve looked into, ModuleConcatenationPlugin seems to require modules to be in the exact same chunk set, which is why it bails out when shared modules are moved into a parent chunk via splitChunks. But in these cases, the parent chunk is always loaded before the async page chunk, so concatenation should still be safe.

The current check ("Module is not in the same chunk(s)") feels a bit too strict since it doesnŌĆÖt consider the parent-child relationship between chunks. Because of that, we miss out on valid concatenation opportunities.

Impact I noticed:

A large number of bailouts (~5,000 in real apps)

No scope hoisting across these boundaries

Extra __webpack_require__ calls adding runtime overhead

What IŌĆÖm thinking:

Allow concatenation when the module comes from a parent chunk thatŌĆÖs guaranteed to load first

Use the chunk graph to make sure execution order stays correct

Also check if module iteration order is affecting grouping here

Plan:

Dig into the bailout logic in ModuleConcatenationPlugin

Try a small prototype relaxing this constraint

Validate correctness + performance

Let me know if this direction makes sense , I will be happy to work on this issue.

body:
firstly, @alexander-akait i'm not a bot and thanks for looking at this! I understand you might see this as a configuration issue rather than a bug. Could you point me to what the correct splitChunks configuration would be to achieve the expected behavior? I'd like to understand the intended approach before continuing with the PR.
Just to clarify what I was trying to fix ŌĆö this is the issue @trullock raised: a Vue SFC has both a named export (opts) and a default export (the component). The app only statically imports the named export, and dynamically imports the full component. The default export ends up in the initial chunk without being marked as unused, so it never gets tree-shaken.
I traced it to getReferencedExports in HarmonyExportImportedSpecifierDependency when vue-loader's intermediary file does export * from "./module" and webpack can't statically determine all exports, it falls into dynamic-reexport mode and returns EXPORTS_OBJECT_REFERENCED, marking everything including default as used. But export * never re-exports default per the ES spec, and mode.ignored already contains "default" ŌĆö it just wasn't being respected in that code path.
Happy to close the PR if this is intentional behavior or better solved through configuration!

author: shoryax

body:
It looks like this is the same issue discussed in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/20288, about unused export statement ends up being generated in the initial chunk. For reference, hereŌĆÖs the previous comment: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/20288#issuecomment-3730319094. Does it make sense to you?

If not, let me explain more: 

In your case with `vue-loader`, it splits a `Vue` SFC into two separate modules: one for the `template` and one for the `script`. The SFC module then will re-exports those proxied modules.

As shown in your code, youŌĆÖre importing the module synchronously on line 1: `import { opts as PageTest } from './test.vue'`. Because this is a static import, `webpack` includes all exports from the `<script>` part of SFC file in the initial chunk, so the unused default export will also be included.

author: chetan137

body:
Hi everyone,

IŌĆÖve been reading through the discussion and the related issue (#20288) to better understand the behavior here.

From what I understand, the root cause is that vue-loader generates an intermediary module that uses `export *`, which causes webpack to enter dynamic-reexport mode. In this mode, `getReferencedExports()` conservatively marks the exports object as referenced, so the default export is considered used and cannot be tree-shaken.

Since modifying the core behavior could introduce edge cases, IŌĆÖm curious whether a loader-based approach that rewrites `export *` into explicit named exports would be a viable solution for this scenario.

IŌĆÖm currently exploring the module graph logic around `HarmonyExportImportedSpecifierDependency` to understand how webpack determines referenced exports. If there are recommended areas of the codebase to study for this, IŌĆÖd appreciate any guidance.

Thanks for the detailed explanations so far!

body: Thanks to @21duxue for the minimal reproduction repo. This happens because the project has two versions of webpack. A custom plugin loads `ModuleFederationPlugin` from the older version, which ends up creating an old `RemoteModule`. As shown in the screenshot, resolving webpack inside that custom plugin returns the older version.
author: xiaoxiaojx

body: Right, the only valid way to load webpack is using `compiler.webpack` inside a plugin, you should avoid using `import webpack from "webpack";` (the same for `require`) inside plugins and loaders (expect edge cases, but it is not this example)
author: alexander-akait

body:
hi’╝ī@xiaoxiaojx
We have a plugin that uses the ModuleFederationPlugin of webpack internally. The plugin is a public npm package. We can't notify everyone to upgrade just because webpack is upgraded. I think you should be compatible with this situation; otherwise, we can only fix webpack. We think there is something wrong with the latest version you released.

body:
@Philipp91 in your tsconfig you can add an array to the `types` value i.e 

```
"types": [
  "node"
]
```

this would whitelist node as the only type that would be expected in the search. As i don't know what types you have i cannot provide the exact answer, but you'd probably want to add an exhaustive list for every `@types` package you are using.

If you want to go the override route, i didn't directly test this in any long form as i went the whitelisting route, but in your package.json, you add:

```
 "overrides": {
    "@types/eslint-scope": "9.1.0"
  }
```

or something of the sort?

please do not c&p these code blocks and expect them to work directly.

body: Unfortunately, there is no such option, the loader should not mean anything about the system and not change state, yes, there are hacky and roundabout ways to do this, but officially I would say not to do this, also it can break a cache because it was not design to do this (the ability to cache means that the system currently has a configuration that does not change during execution)

I'm working on an explicit plugin-based alternative now and will post a solution shortly.

author: alexander-akait

body:
Hi, I looked into this 
It seems plugins are applied during compiler initialization, before loaders are executed. When adding a plugin inside a loader using `this._compiler.options.plugins.push(...)`, the plugin is added too late and its `apply` method is never called.
ThatŌĆÖs why the VirtualUrlPlugin does not handle the `virtual:` scheme in this case.
I think this could be clarified in the documentation or possibly trigger a warning when plugins are added during loader execution.
Happy to work on a docs update or warning if this sounds reasonable.
author: AyushJoshi123

body:
Thanks, everyone :)

I'm not too surprised that webpack doesn't support this, although it does make things a bit more difficult for my loader.

My mental model is that loaders could be executed in parallel, so they generally shouldn't change anything about the compiler, because that would introduce a race condition.

@AyushJoshi123 Thank you for digging into this! :)

I think the fact that the `_compiler` property starts with an underscore (indicating that it's private) is probably already enough.

author: xiaoxiaojx

body:
I looked into this and traced the root cause to the chunk hashing order in `Compilation.createHash()`.

Runtime chunks were being hashed **before** initial/split chunks, even though `GetChunkFilenameRuntimeModule` (which generates `__webpack_require__.u` ŌĆö the chunk ID ŌåÆ filename mapping) reads `chunk.contentHash` for each split chunk at hash time. Because those split chunks hadn't been hashed yet, the module embedded a stale hash from the previous compilation. On the next watch rebuild, the asset cache returned the previous runtime asset unchanged, so the runtime pointed to a filename that no longer existed on disk.

This contradicts a comment already in the same method: *"all non-runtime chunks need to be hashed first, since runtime chunk might use their hashes."*

Fix in PR #20730 ŌĆö chunks are now hashed in four explicit passes that respect the dependency chain:

1. Async chunks
2. Non-entry initial chunks (split chunks) 
3. Runtime chunks
4. Entry chunks (may depend on runtime chunk hash)

author: xiaoxiaojx

body:
I've found that webpack `5.106.0` introduced a regression in how it handles anonymous default exports (`export default () => {}`). 
It now generates `Reflect.getOwnPropertyDescriptor(__WEBPACK_DEFAULT_EXPORT__, "name")` code after each anonymous default export, which conflicts with the `eval-based` source map mode.
The fix is to pin webpack to `~5.105.4` as @ihabadham mentions

author: alexander-akait

body:
took a look. the only commit between v5.105.0 and v5.106.1 that touches `lib/ProgressPlugin.js` is b404817 (`feat: add validate option to enable/disable validation` #20275). that commit moved the schema validation from the constructor into a `compiler.hooks.validate` listener registered in `_applyOnCompiler`, and started storing the unmerged user input in `this.options` directly.

i tried to reproduce against `master` with the simple cases:

```js
new ProgressPlugin()
new ProgressPlugin((p, msg) => {})
new ProgressPlugin({})
new ProgressPlugin({ profile: false })
new ProgressPlugin({ profile: false, handler: (p, msg) => {} })
```

all of these run cleanly on `master` with `mode: "production"`, no validation error. the precompiled `schemas/plugins/ProgressPlugin.check.js` also accepts each of those when called directly.

so i can't get to the actual schema rejection without your config. could you share the exact `plugins:` array webpack-cli is being handed? or, if it's coming from laravel-mix, the resolved plugin entry that ends up in the merged config? something like a one-line `console.log(this.options)` injected into `lib/ProgressPlugin.js` constructor (or just the plugins list before `webpack(config)` is called) would pin down what the validator is rejecting.

my current guess is that something in the Mix ŌåÆ webpack-cli ŌåÆ ProgressPlugin path is passing a non-plain-object (e.g. a class instance, or an object with inherited enumerable keys) that v5.105's constructor validate happened to coerce away via the spread but v5.106 stores as-is and then validates. but that's a guess until i can see what your `this.options` actually is at validation time.

body: This is more like one trade-off between `ES spec` alignment and bundle size. In practice, accessing `.name` on the default import binding is quite rare. That said, it's a good suggestion, we'll look into how to handle this more gracefully.

body:
> [fullhash:6] and [hash:base64:6] is not supported because [this line](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/css/CssGenerator.js#L164)

this is expected, we have the option for this in css generator options, we don't support it at all for any places in webpack, I am not sure we should introduce it because mix hash digest and hash generation brings a lot of problems, better to provide it directly via options, will revisit (ref https://github.com/webpack/loader-utils)

> hash is overrided to module hash because [this line](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/lib/TemplatedPathPlugin.js#L486), which differs from css-loader behavior

This is a breaking change between css-loader and css modules inside webpack
